topics.title: Introduction to Mechatronics - Key Elements of Mechatronic Systems, Functions of Mechatronic Systems, Design Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Mechatronics - Key Elements of Mechatronic Systems, Functions of Mechatronic Systems, Design Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Mechatronics (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Mechatronics** is a *multi-disciplinary, synergistic* design philosophy that holistically integrates **Mechanical Engineering, Electronics, Computer Engineering, Control Engineering, and Information Technology** to produce optimized, intelligent products, processes, and systems.

The term **"Mechatronics"** was first coined in **1969** by a Japanese engineer, **Tetsuro Mori** (Senior Engineer at Yaskawa Electric Corporation), as a portmanteau of **"Mecha"** (mechanism) + **"Tronics"** (electronics). It is therefore a **20th-century engineering paradigm** that became essential in the 21st-century Industry **4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** revolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (use verbatim in exams):**
> *“Mechatronics is the synergistic integration of mechanical engineering with electronic and computer-based intelligent control systems in the design and manufacture of industrial products and processes.”*
> — Standard KTU Board Reference phrasing

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Think of **mechatronics as building an artificial human body**:

| Human Body Part | Mechatronic Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain** | Microcontroller / PLC / DSP / Processor | Decision making & logic |
| **Nervous System** | Sensors + Signal Conditioning + Wiring | Sensing & data transmission |
| **Muscles** | Actuators (Motors, Solenoids, Pneumatics) | Physical action |
| **Skeleton** | Mechanical Frame / Linkages / Chassis | Structural support |
| **Mouth/Speech** | HMI (Display, Buzzer, Speaker) | Communication to user |
| **Heart & Blood** | Power Supply, Battery, Power Electronics | Energy distribution |
| **Reflexes** | Closed-loop Control Algorithms | Automatic response |

Just as a human cannot walk effectively without the brain, nerves, and muscles working **simultaneously**, a mechatronic system fails if its mechanical, electronic, and software subsystems are designed **in isolation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Mechatronics is NOT the sum of its parts — it is the **product of their synergy**. A car with a perfect engine but no ECU is just an expensive sculpture.

## 1.3 Key Elements of a Mechatronic System

A mechatronic system has **six core elements** that work in closed synergy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Mandatory KTU Elements of Mechatronics (Memorize for Board Exam):**
> 1. **Mechanical System** – physical structure, kinematics, dynamics
> 2. **Sensors & Transducers** – convert physical variables to electrical signals
> 3. **Actuators & Drivers** – convert control signals to physical motion
> 4. **Controllers (Electronic / Microprocessor)** – the "brain" executing logic
> 5. **Software / Firmware** – algorithms, control laws, user interface logic
> 6. **Power Supply & Signal Conditioning** – energy management & signal integrity

### 1.4 Typical Real-World Examples (KTU Frequently Repeated)

| System | Mechatronic Elements Used |
|---|---|
| **CNC Machine** | Servo motors, encoders, PLC, hydraulic actuators, CAD/CAM software |
| **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** | Wheel-speed sensors, ECU, hydraulic modulator |
| **Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR)** | LIDAR, IMU, BLDC motors, ARM processor, ROS software |
| **Smart Air Conditioner** | Temperature sensor, microcontroller, compressor actuator, IoT module |
| **3D Printer** | Stepper motors, thermistor, Arduino/Marlin firmware, lead screw mechanics |
| **Washing Machine** | Water level sensor, motor, microcontroller, fuzzy-logic control software |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: *"Is a purely mechanical clock a mechatronic system?"* — **NO**, because it lacks electronics, sensors, and intelligent control. Conversely, an electronic quartz watch is **borderline** mechatronics.

## 1.5 The Mechatronic Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Design vs. Mechatronic Design (High-Yield Exam Comparison):**

| Aspect | Traditional (Sequential) Design | Mechatronic (Concurrent) Design |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Disciplines designed **separately** | Disciplines designed **in parallel** |
| Communication | Late, error-prone | Continuous, via shared models |
| Optimization | Local optimum | **Global optimum** |
| Cost | High redesign cost | Low (issues caught early) |
| Time-to-market | Long | Short |
| Philosophy | "Mechanism first, then add electronics" | "Function first, then partition into mechanics + electronics + software" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of a Mechatronic System

Every mechatronic system, regardless of complexity, can be decomposed into **four functional blocks** arranged in a closed-loop chain:

$$\boxed{\text{Power Supply} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Mechanism} \rightarrow \text{Process Output}}$$

While in parallel:
$$\boxed{\text{Process Output} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Controller (Feedback)}}$$

### 2.1.1 Block-by-Block Explanation

**(a) Power Supply**
- Provides regulated DC voltage to all electronics
- Critical parameters: ripple < **50 mV**, efficiency > **85%**, isolation > **1 kV**
- Examples: SMPS, linear regulator, battery with BMS

**(b) Controller / Decision Element**
- Heart of the system — runs the embedded firmware
- May be an 8-bit MCU (ATmega328 in Arduino), 32-bit ARM Cortex-M, DSP, or FPGA
- Executes control law **u(t) = Kₚe(t) + Kᵢ∫e(t)dt + K_d·de(t)/dt** (PID)
- The PID equation is **central to KTU mechatronics**:
$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\int_{0}^{t}e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**(c) Actuator & Driver**
- Driver circuit amplifies low-power MCU signal (typically **3.3 V / 5 V, ~20 mA**) to actuator-grade power (**12 V – 240 V, several amps**)
- Actuator types:
  * **Electrical**: DC motor, stepper, BLDC, solenoid, relay
  * **Hydraulic**: cylinder, motor (high force)
  * **Pneumatic**: cylinder, valve (clean, fast)

**(d) Mechanical System (Mechanism)**
- Convert actuator motion into desired output motion
- Includes: gears, lead screws, cams, linkages, belts, chains, ball screws
- Resolves trade-off between **force, speed, and precision**

**(e) Sensor**
- Converts physical measurand into electrical signal
- Categories:
  * **Active** (generate voltage): piezoelectric, thermocouple, photovoltaic
  * **Passive** (change resistance/capacitance): RTD, thermistor, LDR, strain gauge
- Key specs: **range, accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, bandwidth, hysteresis**

**(f) Signal Conditioning**
- Bridges sensor low-output to ADC-compatible range
- Operations: amplification, filtering, linearization, isolation
- Example: a thermocouple's **40 µV/°C** output must be amplified by ~**250×** to be readable

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU's classic 6-mark question**: Draw the block diagram of a mechatronic system and explain the function of each block. Use *one specific industrial example* in your explanation.

## 2.2 Functions Performed by a Mechatronic System

A mechatronic system performs **seven primary functions**. KTU commonly asks for an "explain-the-functions" 14-mark question.

| # | Function | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensing / Monitoring** | Detecting physical state via sensors | Thermistor reading temperature |
| 2 | **Actuation** | Producing physical action | DC motor rotating a joint |
| 3 | **Control** | Comparing output with reference & correcting | Cruise control maintaining 80 km/h |
| 4 | **Data Processing** | Computation, filtering, decision-making | Kalman filter on IMU data |
| 5 | **Communication** | Interfacing with other systems / users | CAN bus, Bluetooth, HMI |
| 6 | **Self-Diagnostics** | Detecting faults & reporting | ABS warning light |
| 7 | **Optimization / Adaptation** | Improving performance over time | Self-tuning PID in furnace |

### 2.2.1 Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop Function

- **Open-Loop**: Actuator driven without checking result (cheap, inaccurate). Example: toaster.
- **Closed-Loop**: Output measured, fed back to controller for correction (accurate, robust). Example: robotic arm position control.

The **closed-loop transfer function** for a standard unity-feedback system is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

Where $G(s)$ = forward path transfer function, $H(s)$ = feedback path transfer function.

## 2.3 The Mechatronic Design Approach

The **KTU-recommended design flow** follows the **V-Model** (or modified waterfall):

1. **Need Identification** (Customer / Market Requirement)
2. **Problem Definition** (Specifications: range, accuracy, cost, weight)
3. **Functional Decomposition** (Black-box model of desired behavior)
4. **Conceptual Design** (Multiple mechatronic concepts generated)
5. **Mathematical Modeling** (Differential equations, transfer functions)
6. **Simulation** (MATLAB/Simulink, Adams, ANSYS)
7. **Prototype Fabrication** (3D printing, PCB fabrication)
8. **Testing & Validation**
9. **Iteration** (Refine based on test results)
10. **Production**

### 2.3.1 Design Trade-off Triangle — **Critical KTU Concept**

A mechatronic design is always subject to the **iron triangle** trade-off:

$$\text{Cost} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Performance} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Time-to-Market}$$

You can optimize **only two** — the third must suffer. This is fundamental to mechatronics design philosophy.

### 2.3.2 Modeling Hierarchy in Mechatronic Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-domain modeling principle:** A mechatronic system spans **multiple energy domains**. At each step, decide which domain model is required.

| Level | Model Type | Equations Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Behavioral** | Black box, IO mapping | $y = f(x)$ lookup tables |
| **Static** | Steady-state relations | Algebraic equations |
| **Dynamic** | Differential equations | Newton's 2nd law, Kirchhoff's laws |
| **Transfer Function** | Laplace-domain | $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ |
| **State-Space** | Multi-variable, time-domain | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas to memorize** for any Mechatronics KTU exam (Module 1 + linked modules):

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_{p}e(t) + K_{i}\int e(t)dt + K_{d}\frac{de}{dt}$ | u in V, e in V, $K_{p}$ dimensionless, $K_{i}$ in 1/s, $K_{d}$ in s |
| Laplace of derivative | $\mathcal{L}\!\left[\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right] = sX(s) - x(0)$ | s = complex variable |
| Laplace of integral | $\mathcal{L}\!\left[\int x(t)dt\right] = \dfrac{X(s)}{s}$ | Used in PID integrator |
| Closed-Loop TF | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | H(s) = 1 for unity feedback |
| Open-Loop TF | $G_{OL}(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Required for stability check |
| First-Order Plant | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau$ = time constant, K = DC gain |
| Second-Order Plant | $G(s) = \dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ | $\zeta$ = damping ratio, $\omega_{n}$ = natural freq |
| DC Motor Voltage | $V = IR + L\dfrac{dI}{dt} + K_{b}\omega$ | $K_{b}$ = back-EMF constant |
| DC Motor Torque | $T = K_{t}I - J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} - B\omega$ | $K_{t}$ = torque constant |
| Transfer Function (Motor) | $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K}{(Ls+R)(Js+B) + K_{b}K_{t}}$ | Crucial for motor control design |
| Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_{f}\varepsilon$ | $G_{f}$ = gauge factor (~2), $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| Thermistor (NTC) | $R(T) = R_{0}\,e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{0}}\right)}$ | Steinhart-Hart approximation |
| Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{pulses}}$ | N = pulses per revolution |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer scripts, **always carry units** in the final answer. Losing 0.5–1 mark per question for "dimensionless answers" is the #1 valuation complaint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a PID Controller is the Universal Mechatronic Controller

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Error Signal

Define the **tracking error** $e(t)$ as the difference between the desired setpoint $r(t)$ and the measured output $y(t)$:

$$e(t) \;=\; r(t) \;-\; y(t)$$

A mechatronic controller must generate a control signal $u(t)$ that **drives $e(t)$ to zero** (or as close as possible). The three classical actions are P, I, and D.

### 3.1.2 Proportional Action

We want the controller's effort to be **proportional to the current error**. The stronger the error, the harder we push:

$$u_{P}(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t)$$

**Problem**: P-only control leaves a steady-state error for step inputs (called *offset*). The system settles at a value where $u_P$ exactly balances the disturbance, but never reaches setpoint.

### 3.1.3 Integral Action — Eliminating Steady-State Error

If a residual constant error $e_{ss}$ exists, integrating it over time produces a **continuously growing** control effort. Eventually it must overcome the disturbance:

$$u_{I}(t) \;=\; K_{i}\int_{0}^{t}e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Substituting into Laplace:

$$U_{I}(s) \;=\; \frac{K_{i}}{s}\,E(s)$$

This is why integral action **guarantees zero steady-state error** for step references.

### 3.1.4 Derivative Action — Adding Damping

For a fast-moving error (large $de/dt$), we want a strong anticipatory corrective force — like braking before a sharp turn:

$$u_{D}(t) \;=\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Laplace form:

$$U_{D}(s) \;=\; K_{d}\,s\,E(s)$$

The derivative term acts as a **damping agent**, reducing overshoot and oscillation.

### 3.1.5 Combining P + I + D

The control signals are summed (linear superposition holds for linear plants):

$$u(t) \;=\; u_{P}(t) \;+\; u_{I}(t) \;+\; u_{D}(t)$$

$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\int_{0}^{t}e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is the **universal PID control law** — every ABS, every furnace, every drone uses it.

### 3.1.6 Transfer Function Form

Taking the Laplace transform of each term:

$$U(s) \;=\; K_{p}\,E(s) \;+\; \frac{K_{i}}{s}\,E(s) \;+\; K_{d}\,s\,E(s)$$

Factoring $E(s)$:

$$U(s) \;=\; \left[K_{p} \;+\; \frac{K_{i}}{s} \;+\; K_{d}\,s\right]E(s)$$

The controller transfer function is therefore:

$$C(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p} \;+\; \frac{K_{i}}{s} \;+\; K_{d}\,s$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$C(s) \;=\; \frac{K_{d}s^{2} \;+\; K_{p}s \;+\; K_{i}}{s}$$

### 3.1.7 Stability Insight via Characteristic Equation

For unity feedback, the closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 \;+\; C(s)\,G(s) \;=\; 0$$

Substituting $C(s)$ and assuming a first-order plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$1 \;+\; \frac{(K_{d}s^{2} + K_{p}s + K_{i})}{s} \cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \;=\; 0$$

Multiply through by $s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$s(\tau s + 1) \;+\; K(K_{d}s^{2} + K_{p}s + K_{i}) \;=\; 0$$

Expanding:

$$\tau s^{2} \;+\; s \;+\; K K_{d}s^{2} \;+\; K K_{p}s \;+\; K K_{i} \;=\; 0$$

Grouping like powers of $s$:

$$(\tau + K K_{d})\,s^{2} \;+\; (1 + K K_{p})\,s \;+\; K K_{i} \;=\; 0$$

By **Routh-Hurwitz stability** (all coefficients positive), this second-order system is **always stable** for $K_p, K_i, K_d > 0$. This explains the ubiquity of PID.

## 3.2 Derivation: Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Motor

This is **the single most-asked derivation** in KTU mechatronics papers.

### 3.2.1 Electrical Subsystem (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)

The armature circuit contains resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, applied voltage $V_a$, and a back-EMF proportional to speed:

$$V_a(t) \;=\; R_a i_a(t) \;+\; L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} \;+\; e_b(t)$$

The back-EMF $e_b$ is induced by the rotating armature:

$$e_b(t) \;=\; K_b\,\omega(t)$$

Substituting:

$$V_a(t) \;=\; R_a i_a(t) \;+\; L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} \;+\; K_b\,\omega(t)$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$V_a(s) \;=\; (R_a + L_a s)\,I_a(s) \;+\; K_b\,\omega(s)$$

### 3.2.2 Mechanical Subsystem (Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation)

The developed torque drives the inertia $J$ against viscous friction $B$ and any load torque $T_L$:

$$T(t) \;=\; J\frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} \;+\; B\,\omega(t) \;+\; T_{L}(t)$$

The motor torque is proportional to armature current:

$$T(t) \;=\; K_t\,i_a(t)$$

Substituting:

$$K_t\,i_a(t) \;=\; J\frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} \;+\; B\,\omega(t) \;+\; T_{L}(t)$$

Laplace transform (ignoring load torque for simplicity):

$$K_t\,I_a(s) \;=\; (J s + B)\,\omega(s)$$

### 3.2.3 Solving for $I_a(s)$ in Terms of $\omega(s)$

From the mechanical equation:

$$I_a(s) \;=\; \frac{(Js + B)}{K_t}\,\omega(s)$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting into Electrical Equation

$$V_a(s) \;=\; (R_a + L_a s)\left[\frac{(Js + B)}{K_t}\right]\omega(s) \;+\; K_b\,\omega(s)$$

Factor out $\omega(s)$:

$$V_a(s) \;=\; \left[\frac{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B)}{K_t} \;+\; K_b\right]\omega(s)$$

### 3.2.5 Final Motor Transfer Function

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) \;+\; K_t K_b}\;}$$

This is a **second-order system** of the form:

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$$

Where the **motor gain constant** is:

$$K \;=\; \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_b}$$

### 3.2.6 Special Case — Neglecting Armature Inductance

If $L_a$ is small (typical for low-power DC motors):

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;\approx\; \frac{K_t}{R_a(J s + B) + K_t K_b} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

This reduces to a **first-order lag** with motor time constant:

$$\tau_m \;=\; \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_t K_b}$$

This simplified form is the workhorse for **KTU numerical problems**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors classification matrix** — frequently tested in KTU 2-mark questions:

| Measurand | Sensor Type | Output | Range (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Thermocouple | Voltage | $-200\,°C$ to $+1350\,°C$ |
| Temperature | RTD (PT100) | Resistance | $-50\,°C$ to $+600\,°C$ |
| Temperature | Thermistor | Resistance (non-linear) | $-55\,°C$ to $+150\,°C$ |
| Position | Potentiometer | Voltage divider | 1 mm to 1 m |
| Position | LVDT | AC voltage | $\pm$ 0.1 mm to $\pm$ 25 cm |
| Velocity | Tachogenerator | DC voltage | 0 to 10 000 rpm |
| Force | Strain gauge | Resistance change | 0 to 100 kN |
| Acceleration | Accelerometer | Voltage | $\pm$ 1 g to $\pm$ 100 g |
| Light | LDR / Photodiode | Resistance / Current | Lux range |
| Proximity | Inductive / Capacitive | Binary | 1 mm to 30 mm |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Actuator Selection

| Actuator | Strengths | Weaknesses | Typical Mechatronic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor** | Cheap, easy to control, precise | Brush wear, EMI | Small robots, wheels |
| **Stepper Motor** | Open-loop positioning, no encoder needed | Resonance, low torque at high speed | 3D printers, CNC |
| **BLDC Motor** | High efficiency, no brushes | Needs ESC, complex drive | Drones, EVs |
| **Servo Motor** | Built-in feedback, plug-and-play | Limited rotation | Robotic joints |
| **Hydraulic** | Extremely high force | Leaks, heavy, noisy | Excavators |
| **Pneumatic** | Clean, fast, cheap | Compressible, low precision | Pick-and-place |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Mechatronic System — Block Diagram

This is the **master diagram** that the KTU board expects for any "draw block diagram" question (typically 7–10 marks).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POWER SUPPLY] --> B[CONTROLLER MCU DSP PLC]
    B --> C[DRIVER AND ACTUATOR]
    C --> D[MECHANICAL SYSTEM MECHANISM]
    D --> E[PROCESS OUTPUT]
    E --> F[SENSOR]
    F --> G[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    G --> B
    H[HMI DISPLAY KEYPAD] <--> B
    I[COMMUNICATION MODULE] <--> B
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> F
    A --> G
```

**Reading this diagram:**
- **Solid blue arrows** = forward control signal flow (setpoint → plant)
- **Red arrows** = feedback signal flow (sensor → controller)
- **Green arrows** = power distribution
- The HMI and communication blocks allow human/system interaction.

## 4.2 Sensor-Conditioner-ADC-Controller Signal Chain

This is the **complete signal-processing chain** that every sensor-equipped mechatronic system follows. KTU often asks to "explain signal conditioning" — this diagram answers it visually.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Measurand Heat Force Position] --> S[Sensor Transducer]
    S --> SG[Signal Conditioning Amplify Filter Linearize]
    SG --> MUX[Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> PROC[Microcontroller DSP]
    PROC --> DA[Decision Algorithm]
    DA --> ACT[Actuator Driver]
    ACT --> A[Actuator Motor Solenoid Valve]
    A --> O[Physical Action]
    O --> P
```

## 4.3 Mechatronic Design V-Model Flow

The KTU-recommended **V-Model design methodology** maps design phases on the descending arm and validation phases on the ascending arm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Need Identification] --> V2[Requirement Specification]
    V2 --> V3[Functional Decomposition]
    V3 --> V4[Conceptual Design]
    V4 --> V5[Mathematical Modeling]
    V5 --> V6[Detailed Design Mechanical Electrical Software]
    V6 --> V7[Prototype Fabrication]
    V7 --> V8[Unit Testing Software]
    V7 --> V9[Subsystem Integration]
    V9 --> V10[System Testing]
    V10 --> V11[Validation Against Requirements]
    V11 --> V12[Deployment and Production]
    V6 -.-> V8
    V5 -.-> V9
    V2 -.-> V11
```

## 4.4 PID Control Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Setpoint R s] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    SUM --> E[Error E s]
    E --> C[PID Controller C s]
    C --> G[Plant G s]
    G --> Y[Output Y s]
    Y --> H[Feedback H s]
    H --> SUM
    D[Disturbance] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram**: The error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$. The PID controller produces $U(s) = C(s)E(s)$. The plant responds with $Y(s) = G(s)[U(s) + D(s)]$. The loop is stable when all closed-loop poles lie in the left half of the $s$-plane.

## 4.5 Mechatronic System Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerLayer["POWER LAYER"]
        PS[SMPS Battery Power Bus]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer["COMPUTE LAYER"]
        MCU[Microcontroller]
        FW[Embedded Firmware]
        OS[RTOS if Any]
    end
    subgraph SenseLayer["SENSING LAYER"]
        S1[Temperature]
        S2[Position]
        S3[Force]
        SC[Signal Conditioner]
    end
    subgraph ActLayer["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        DRV[Driver H Bridge]
        ACT[Actuator Motor Valve]
    end
    subgraph MechLayer["MECHANICAL LAYER"]
        MECH[Linkage Gear Train Frame]
    end
    subgraph CommLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        HMI[Display Keypad]
        NET[CAN UART WiFi]
    end
    PowerLayer --> ComputeLayer
    PowerLayer --> SenseLayer
    PowerLayer --> ActLayer
    SenseLayer --> SC --> MCU
    MCU --> FW
    MCU --> OS
    MCU --> DRV --> ACT --> MECH
    MECH --> S1
    MECH --> S2
    MECH --> S3
    MCU <--> HMI
    MCU <--> NET
```

## 4.6 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What physical variable to measure] --> B{Variable type}
    B -->|Temperature| C{Accuracy needed}
    C -->|Low under 2 deg C| D[Thermistor]
    C -->|High over 0 1 deg C| E{Environment}
    E -->|Wide range minus 200 to 1350| F[Thermocouple]
    E -->|Moderate minus 50 to 600| G[RTD PT100]
    B -->|Position| H{Range needed}
    H -->|Short under 1 cm linear| I[LVDT]
    H -->|Long and rotational| J[Encoder]
    H -->|Simple binary| K[Proximity Switch]
    B -->|Force| L[Strain Gauge Bridge]
    L --> M[Amplifier and ADC]
```
