### B. By Motion Domain
- **Planar chain:** All motion in a single plane. 3 DOF per link. Examples: 4-bar, slider-crank, cam-follower.
- **Spatial chain:** Motion in 3D space. 6 DOF per link. Examples: humanoid arm, hexapod robot.

### C. By Conversion (Inversions)
A given kinematic chain can yield **multiple mechanisms** by fixing different links. This is called *Kutzbach's Inversion Theorem*. Example: The 4-slider chain gives Whitworth quick-return, oscillating cylinder, etc.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Kinematic Chain Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Serial (RRR) arms, parallel (Delta) pickers |
| **Automotive** | Suspension (5-bar), wiper mechanisms, engine valve trains |
| **Aerospace** | Landing gear, flap actuators, gimbal systems |
| **Biomedical** | Prosthetic limbs, surgical robots (Da Vinci) |
| **Manufacturing** | CNC machines, 3D printers (cartesian) |
| **Mechatronics** | Foundation for motion control + feedback design |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Gruebler–Kutzbach Equation (Planar Case)

This is the derivation that the KTU examiner expects in a 7-mark or 14-mark question. We will write it out **line by line, no skipped steps**.

### Step 1 — Assign DOF to a Single Free Link in 2D
In a 2D plane, a rigid body can:
1. Translate along the X-axis.
2. Translate along the Y-axis.
3. Rotate about an axis perpendicular to the plane.

So a free, unconstrained link in 2D has **3 DOF**.

### Step 2 — Count the Total Raw DOF of All Links
If we have $n$ links (including the ground), and all of them are floating freely, the total raw DOF would be:

$$
\text{Total raw DOF} \;=\; 3n
$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the Effect of the Ground (Fixed Link)
One of the $n$ links is the **ground (frame)**, which by definition is stationary. This link loses all **3** of its DOF (no translation, no rotation). So the remaining moving DOF of the system becomes:

$$
\text{Net DOF} \;=\; 3n \;-\; 3 \;=\; 3\,(n - 1)
$$

This is the *baseline* before any joints are introduced.

### Step 4 — Account for Lower Pairs (1-DOF Joints)
A lower pair such as a revolute (hinge) or prismatic (slider) joint allows exactly **1** relative motion between the two connected links. In 2D, since each link has 3 DOF, joining them with a 1-DOF joint removes:

$$
3 \;-\; 1 \;=\; 2 \text{ DOF}
$$

If there are $j_1$ lower pairs, the total DOF removed is $2j_1$.

### Step 5 — Account for Higher Pairs (2-DOF Joints in Planar Case)
In planar mechanisms, a higher pair (e.g., cam-follower, gear teeth mesh) is treated as allowing 2 relative DOF. So each higher pair removes:

$$
3 \;-\; 2 \;=\; 1 \text{ DOF}
$$

If there are $j_2$ higher pairs, the total DOF removed is $j_2$.

### Step 6 — Assemble the Final Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; \text{Baseline} \;-\; \text{Lower pair loss} \;-\; \text{Higher pair loss} \\
\;&=\; 3\,(n - 1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$M \;=\; 3\,(n-1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2$$

This is the **Gruebler equation** for planar kinematic chains. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Slider-Crank Mechanism

**Problem:** A slider-crank mechanism is used in an internal combustion engine. It has 4 links (crank, connecting rod, slider, ground), 3 revolute joints, and 1 prismatic joint. Find the mobility.

**Step-by-step solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters from the given diagram:
- $n = 4$ (crank + connecting rod + piston + ground)
- $j_1 = 4$ (three revolute joints + one prismatic joint — all are 1-DOF lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$ (no higher pairs)

Step 2 — Substitute into the Gruebler equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 3\,(n - 1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2 \\
\;&=\; 3\,(4 - 1) \;-\; 2 \times 4 \;-\; 0 \\
\;&=\; 3 \times 3 \;-\; 8 \\
\;&=\; 9 \;-\; 8 \\
\;&=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 1$ → The slider-crank has **1 DOF**. This is correct: one input (crank rotation) determines the entire motion of the piston. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Four-Bar Linkage

**Problem:** A planar four-bar linkage has 4 binary links (including ground) connected by 4 revolute joints. Compute its mobility.

**Step-by-step solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters:
- $n = 4$ (input link, coupler, output link, ground)
- $j_1 = 4$ (four pin joints, each is a 1-DOF lower pair)
- $j_2 = 0$

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 3\,(4 - 1) \;-\; 2 \times 4 \;-\; 0 \\
\;&=\; 9 \;-\; 8 \\
\;&=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 1$ → The 4-bar chain has **1 DOF**. Driving the input crank (1 rotation) completely determines the output link's angular position. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism

**Problem:** A Whitworth QRR mechanism has 6 links, 6 revolute pairs, and 1 higher pair. Calculate its DOF.

**Step-by-step solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters:
- $n = 6$
- $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute joints)
- $j_2 = 1$ (one cam or higher pair)

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 3\,(6 - 1) \;-\; 2 \times 6 \;-\; 1 \\
\;&=\; 3 \times 5 \;-\; 12 \;-\; 1 \\
\;&=\; 15 \;-\; 13 \\
\;&=\; 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 2$ → This is **incorrect** for a real Whitworth (which has 1 DOF). The mismatch indicates a *passive DOF* (idle/extra rotation) — this is a classic Grashof-class phenomenon that the KTU examiner loves to test. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Mechanisms (Memorize This Table)

| # | Mechanism | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | DOF $M$ | Real DOF | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Four-bar linkage | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ✅ Standard |
| 2 | Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ✅ Standard |
| 3 | Double-slider crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ✅ Standard |
| 4 | Six-bar Stephenson | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | 1 | ⚠️ Passive DOF |
| 5 | Six-bar Watt | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | 1 | ⚠️ Passive DOF |
| 6 | Planetary gear | $n$ | $2n$ | $n$ | 1 | 1 | ✅ Standard |
| 7 | Cam-follower | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ✅ Standard |
| 8 | Robot arm (RRR planar) | 4 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 3 | ✅ Open chain |
| 9 | Delta robot | 12+ | 18+ | 0 | 3 | 3 | ✅ Parallel chain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passive / Idle DOF** (rows 4, 5): The Gruebler count gives $M = 2$, but the mechanism has only **1 useful** input. The extra DOF is a *self-motion* or *idle DOF*. Always note this explicitly in your KTU answer for full marks.

## 3.6 Kutzbach's Equation for Spatial Chains — Quick Verification

For a **spatial** RR chain (like a 2-axis gimbal):
- $n = 3$ (2 links + ground)
- $j_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints, each 1-DOF in space)
- All other $j_i = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 6\,(3 - 1) \;-\; 5 \times 2 \;-\; 0 \;-\; 0 \;-\; 0 \;-\; 0 \\
\;&=\; 12 \;-\; 10 \\
\;&=\; 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 2$ → Correct, since a 2-link gimbal has 2 independent rotations. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mechatronic Kinematic Chain System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a kinematic chain is *augmented* with mechatronic subsystems to form a working automated system. This is the conceptual architecture you should sketch (or describe) in 14-mark KTU answers when asked to "explain a mechatronic system using kinematic chains."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller\nMicrocontroller PLC] --> B[Driver\nPower Amplifier]
    B --> C[Actuator\nMotor Cylinder]
    C --> D[Kinematic Chain\nLinks plus Joints]
    D --> E[End Effector\nTool Gripper]
    D --> F[Sensor Module\nEncoder LVDT]
    F --> A
    G[User Interface HMI] --> A
    H[Power Supply] --> B
    H --> A
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0f766e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#4d7c0f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#52525b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right, top to bottom):**
1. The **Controller** issues low-power command signals.
2. The **Driver** amplifies these to high-power levels.
3. The **Actuator** converts electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic energy into mechanical motion.
4. The motion is *shaped* by the **Kinematic Chain** (the topic of this module).
5. The **End Effector** performs the useful task (welding, gripping, etc.).
6. **Sensors** on the chain feed back position/velocity to close the control loop.
7. The **HMI** lets the human operator supervise the system.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *closed-loop mechatronic architecture* expected in PEEBT863 Module 2 problems. The kinematic chain is the *physical embodiment* — without it, all the electronics are useless.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DOF Calculation Workflow

Use this decision flow when solving any KTU mobility problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify Mechanism] --> B{Planar or Spatial}
    B -- Planar --> C[Count n including ground]
    B -- Spatial --> D[Count n including ground]
    C --> E[Count j1 lower pairs]
    E --> F[Count j2 higher pairs]
    F --> G[Apply M equals 3 of n minus 1\n minus 2j1 minus j2]
    D --> H[Count j1 j2 j3 j4 j5]
    H --> I[Apply Kutzbach 6 DOF formula]
    G --> J{M equals}
    I --> J
    J -- 0 --> K[Structure Static Determinate]
    J -- 1 --> L[Mechanism One Input]
    J -- greater than 1 --> M[Multiple DOF Robot]
    J -- less than 0 --> N[Locked Statically Indeterminate]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Open-Chain vs Closed-Chain Comparison (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpenChain[Open Chain Serial Manipulator]
        A1[Ground] --- A2[Link 1 R] --- A3[Link 2 R] --- A4[Link 3 R] --- A5[End Effector]
    end
    subgraph ClosedChain[Closed Chain Parallel Manipulator]
        B1[Ground] --- B2[Link A]
        B1 --- B3[Link B]
        B1 --- B4[Link C]
        B2 --- B5[Moving Platform]
        B3 --- B5
        B4 --- B5
    end
    style A1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Open Chain (left):** Single linear path from ground to end effector. High dexterity, low stiffness.

**Closed Chain (right):** Multiple parallel paths from ground to moving platform. Lower dexterity, high stiffness, high payload. Example: Delta robot, Gough-Stewart platform.

---

## 4.4 Joint Type Reference (ASCII Visual Summary)

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF in 2D | DOF in 3D | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Revolute (R) | ⭕ | 1 | 1 | Door hinge |
| Prismatic (P) | ➡️ | 1 | 1 | Drawer slide |
| Cam/Higher | ⚙️ | 2 | 2 | Cam-follower |
| Cylindrical (C) | 🔘 | — | 2 | Screw + rotate |
| Universal (U) | ✖️ | — | 2 | Car drive shaft |
| Spherical (S) | 🔵 | — | 3 | Ball-socket |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a kinematic chain. How is it different from a mechanism?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kinematic chain** is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by kinematic pairs (joints) that allows relative motion between the links. It is a purely geometric-mechanical assembly.

The **difference** between a kinematic chain and a mechanism lies in *intent*:
- A *kinematic chain* is a general assembly of links and joints without a specific prescribed motion.
- A *mechanism* is a kinematic chain in which **one of the links is fixed (grounded)** and the chain is designed to produce a *specific, desired motion* (e.g., straight-line, oscillatory, rotary).

> For example, a 4-bar chain without a fixed link is a *kinematic chain*; once the ground link is chosen and motion is intended, it becomes a *mechanism* (e.g., a bicycle suspension or sewing machine drive). **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State the Gruebler–Kutzbach equation for mobility of a planar mechanism. What is the significance of $M = 0$ and $M < 0$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gruebler–Kutzbach equation** for the mobility (M) of a planar kinematic chain is:

$$
M \;=\; 3\,(n - 1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2
$$

where $n$ = number of links, $j_1$ = number of 1-DOF lower pairs, $j_2$ = number of higher pairs.

**Significance:**
- **$M = 0$:** The structure has *no* relative motion. It behaves as a **statically determinate structure** (a truss or frame).
- **$M < 0$:** The mechanism is **locked** or *statically indeterminate*; the constraints are redundant, and the chain cannot move without distortion. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)

**Q.A) (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **four-bar kinematic chain**. List its three primary inversions and one practical application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planar mechanism has **6 links and 7 lower pairs**. Using the Gruebler equation, determine the mobility of the mechanism. Is the result physically meaningful? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Four-Bar Chain

**Sketch (describe in exam):**
A four-bar chain consists of 4 rigid links connected in a closed loop by 4 revolute (pin) joints. Label the links: 1 = fixed ground (frame), 2 = input crank, 3 = coupler, 4 = output rocker/lever.

**Construction:**
- Link 1 is the *ground* (fixed pivots $O_2$ and $O_4$).
- Link 2 rotates about $O_2$ (the input crank).
- Link 4 rotates about $O_4$ (the output lever).
- Link 3 (coupler) connects the free ends of links 2 and 4 via revolute joints at $A$ and $B$.

**Working:**
- When link 2 is given an angular input $\theta_2$, link 3 (coupler) traces a *coupler curve* in the plane.
- The motion of link 3 is transferred to link 4, producing a constrained angular output $\theta_4$.
- The relationship between $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$ is governed by the loop-closure equation:

$$
\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_4 - \vec{r}_1 = \vec{0}
$$

**Three Primary Inversions (by fixing each link in turn):**

| # | Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism | Application |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ground (1) | Four-bar linkage (crank-rocker / double-crank / double-rocker depending on Grashof) | Bicycle frame, sewing machine |
| 2 | Input link (2) | Drag-link mechanism (both opposite links fully rotate) | Wagon steering, parallel motion |
| 3 | Coupler (3) | Drag-link with floating link fixed — *Whitworth* style | Quick-return shaper |
| 4 | Output link (4) | Rocker-rotated variant | Pump rocker, wiper mechanism |

> **(7 marks complete with sketch, loop equation, and inversion table)**

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Mobility Calculation

**Given:** $n = 6$, $j_1 = 7$, $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into Gruebler equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 3\,(n - 1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2 \\
\;&=\; 3\,(6 - 1) \;-\; 2 \times 7 \;-\; 0 \\
\;&=\; 3 \times 5 \;-\; 14 \\
\;&=\; 15 \;-\; 14 \\
\;&=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 1$.

**Physical Meaning:** A mobility of 1 is **physically meaningful** — the mechanism has exactly **1 independent input** (1 DOF). This is the typical case for most practical machines. With 1 input (e.g., a motor turning one crank), the entire mechanism's configuration is determined. The configuration forms a **1-dimensional curve** in its configuration space, which matches the working of common 6-bar mechanisms like the **Stephenson** and **Watt** chains.

> ✅ **Important Note for Full Marks:** While the *formula* says $M = 1$, six-bar linkages like Stephenson III and Watt II have a *passive (idle) DOF* that does not affect the output. Mention this nuance for bonus credit. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate] (14 Marks)

**Q.B) (a)** Explain the concept of **degree of freedom (DOF)** in a kinematic chain. Derive the **Gruebler–Kutzbach equation** for a planar mechanism, clearly stating the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cam-follower mechanism has **3 links (cam, follower, ground)**, **1 revolute pair**, and **1 higher pair (cam contact)**. Calculate its mobility. Also explain why a higher pair is treated as a 2-DOF joint in the planar case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) DOF Concept & Derivation

**DOF Definition:**
The **degree of freedom** of a kinematic chain is the *number of independent parameters* required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of every link in the system. It is also called the **mobility (M)**.

**Derivation of Gruebler's Equation:**

**Step 1:** In 2D, a single free rigid body has **3 DOF**: two translations ($x, y$) and one rotation ($\theta$). So a system of $n$ free links has $3n$ DOF.

**Step 2:** One link is the **ground** (fixed). It loses all 3 of its DOF. Net DOF $= 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$.

**Step 3:** Each **lower pair** (revolute or prismatic) is a 1-DOF joint. It removes 2 DOF from the 3-DOF link. For $j_1$ lower pairs, total DOF removed $= 2j_1$.

**Step 4:** Each **higher pair** (cam, gear) in planar case is a 2-DOF joint. It removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF. For $j_2$ higher pairs, total DOF removed $= j_2$.

**Step 5 — Final equation:**

$$
M \;=\; 3\,(n - 1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2
$$

**Meaning of each term:**
- $3(n-1)$: net unconstrained DOF of all moving links in 2D.
- $2j_1$: DOF removed by all lower (1-DOF) pairs.
- $j_2$: DOF removed by all higher (2-DOF) pairs.
- $M$: residual mobility / independent inputs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Cam-Follower DOF

**Given:** $n = 3$, $j_1 = 1$ (revolute joint between cam and ground or pivot), $j_2 = 1$ (cam-follower higher pair).

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;&=\; 3\,(3 - 1) \;-\; 2 \times 1 \;-\; 1 \\
\;&=\; 3 \times 2 \;-\; 2 \;-\; 1 \\
\;&=\; 6 \;-\; 3 \\
\;&=\; 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Physical Interpretation:**
Wait — this gives $M = 3$, which seems wrong for a cam-follower. The Gruebler equation assumes *rigid* higher pairs. In practice, a cam-follower has **1 DOF** (cam rotation → follower motion). The discrepancy arises because the cam is *constrained* by the driver; Gruebler gives a *theoretical* count. In KTU, the correct interpretation is to mark the cam-follower with the higher pair counted properly:

If we treat the revolute joint of the cam and the cam-follower contact as $j_1 = 2$ (revolute + cam line contact treated as 1-DOF), then:

$$
M = 3(3-1) - 2 \times 2 - 0 = 6 - 4 = 2
$$

**Standard KTU Convention:** Most textbooks take a *cam-follower with a translating follower* as $n = 3$, $j_1 = 2$ (one revolute for cam shaft + one prismatic for follower), $j_2 = 1$ (cam contact):

$$
M = 3(3-1) - 2(2) - 1 = 6 - 4 - 1 = 1
$$

This gives $M = 1$, which is **physically correct**. ✅

**Why a higher pair is 2-DOF in planar analysis:** A higher pair (e.g., cam-follower) allows the follower to (1) translate along the cam profile and (2) stay in contact while the contact point slides. Both these motions are unconstrained — hence 2 relative DOF. The third motion (penetration into the cam) is blocked, so 1 DOF is removed. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PEEBT863 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link** in $n$ — costs 1 mark. Always count ground.
> 2. **Confusing $j_1$ and $j_2$** — joints are *lower* if they are pin/sliding; *higher* if cam/gear. Misclassifying a cam as lower gives wrong DOF.
> 3. **Ignoring passive DOF** in 6-bar chains — if Gruebler gives $M = 2$ but the mechanism is a single-input one, *mention the passive/extra DOF explicitly* for full credit.
> 4. **Wrong unit in loop-closure equations** — all vectors must be in the same unit (e.g., mm or m).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — a 7-mark question without a sketch loses 2 marks even if the math is correct.
> 6. **Not justifying the answer** — for $M = 0$ or $M < 0$, you must say *what it physically means* (structure or locked).
> 7. **Spatial vs planar mix-up** — for a 3D robot arm, use the **6-DOF Kutzbach** equation, not the planar 3-DOF Gruebler. This is a very common error in mechatronics papers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **kinematic chain** is a connected assembly of *links* and *kinematic pairs* allowing relative motion.
- [x] **Mobility (DOF) $M$** of a planar chain is given by $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [x] For **spatial** chains, use $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- [x] $M = 0$ → static structure; $M = 1$ → typical machine; $M > 1$ → robot-like multi-DOF.
- [x] **Lower pair** (R, P) — surface contact, removes 2 DOF in 2D.
- [x] **Higher pair** (cam, gear) — point/line contact, removes 1 DOF in 2D.
- [x] Always **include the ground link** in $n$ and **fix one link** in the equation.
- [x] **Open chain** → no closed loop, e.g., serial robot arm.
- [x] **Closed chain** → at least one loop, e.g., 4-bar, Delta robot.
- [x] **Grashof's Law** for 4-bar: $s + \ell \le p + q$ → shortest link rotates fully.
- [x] **Inversions** of a kinematic chain (fixing different links) yield different mechanisms.
- [x] In mechatronics, a kinematic chain + actuator + sensor + controller = a complete automated system.
- [x] Passive / idle DOFs exist in Stephenson and Watt 6-bar chains — Gruebler over-counts.
- [x] The **loop-closure equation** $\sum \vec{r}_i = 0$ is the foundation of position analysis.
- [x] Kennedy's theorem: number of instant centers $= n(n-1)/2$ in a planar mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cams # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **CAM (Kinematic Definition):** A **cam** is a rotating or sliding mechanical element, typically of irregular shape (non-circular), which imparts a prescribed, predetermined, and time-dependent motion to another element called the **follower**. The cam–follower pair constitutes a higher-pair kinematic mechanism classified under the category of **direct-contact force-closure systems** (or in some designs, form-closure systems). A cam mechanism essentially converts **uniform rotary (or sliding) input motion** into a **non-uniform, programmable output motion** according to a specified displacement diagram (also called a *motion profile* or *timing diagram*). ### Mathematical Representation The output displacement of the follower $y$ as a function of cam rotation angle $\theta$ is given by a continuous (or piecewise continuous) function: $$y = f(\theta)$$ The input is $\theta(t) = \omega t$, where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (assumed constant for KTU analysis). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Heart Valve"** > Think of a **cam** as the **lobes on a camshaft inside a car engine**. As the camshaft rotates, each lobe pushes a valve (the follower) downward to open it, and a return spring pushes it back up when the lobe rotates away. The shape of the lobe — how steep, how high, how wide — determines exactly how the valve opens and closes. **The lobe is the cam; the valve is the follower; the spring is what maintains contact (force closure).** In a music box, a rotating cylinder with raised pins (the cam) plucks the tines of a steel comb (the follower). In sewing machines, in automatic toys, in printing presses, and in IC engine valve trains — the cam is the "**programmer**" of mechanical motion. ## 1.3 Why Cams Matter in Mechatronics In a mechatronic system, the cam is the mechanical analog of a **software function** — it encodes a desired motion profile into geometry. Just as a microcontroller can be programmed to output a voltage vs. time curve, a cam is *physically programmed* to output a displacement vs. angle curve. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 (Actuators / Kinematic Elements / Programmable Motion Devices), cams are studied as a classical means of **motion programming** in mechatronic subsystems such as packaging machinery, textile automation, and indexing heads. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in **bold**) - **Base Circle Radius ($R_b$ or $R_0$):** The smallest circle of the cam profile; the radius of the circle from which the cam profile emanates. - **Prime Circle Radius ($R_p$):** For roller followers, $R_p = R_b + r_r$, where $r_r$ is the roller radius. - **Pitch Curve:** The path traced by a point on the follower (or its roller center) relative to the cam. - **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact. - **Lift / Stroke ($h$):** Maximum displacement of the follower. - **Cam Angle for Lift ($\beta$ or $\theta_o$):** Angular rotation of the cam during the outstroke. - **Standard input angular velocity:** $\omega$ in **rad/s**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Cams Cams are classified along two independent axes: **by shape (geometry)** and **by follower type**. ### 2.1.1 Classification of Cams (by Geometry) | # | Cam Type | Description | Typical Application | |---|----------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Disc / Plate Cam (Eccentric Cam)** | Flat plate with contoured profile; rotates about a fixed axis perpendicular to its plane. | IC engine valve gear, packaging | | 2 | **Cylindrical / Barrel Cam (Groove Cam)** | Cylindrical body with a groove cut on its surface; follower rides in the groove. | Automatic firearms, printing presses | | 3 | **Conical Cam** | Conical body with a tapered groove; permits inclination of follower. | Shoe machinery | | 4 | **Spherical / Globoidal Cam** | Spherical body with contoured groove. | Copying machines, three-axis automation | | 5 | **Linear / Reciprocating Cam** | Cam performs translation instead of rotation. | Shaper mechanisms, Geneva drives (hybrid) | | 6 | **Heart-Shaped Cam (Constant-Dwell)** | Special cam producing uniform dwell at maximum lift. | Clock escapements, timing devices | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU FOCUS:** The **Disc Cam with Translating or Oscillating Follower** is the most frequently examined type. The KTU examiner expects knowledge of its displacement diagram and the corresponding profile construction. ### 2.1.2 Classification of Followers (by Motion) | # | Follower Motion | Symbol | Description | |---|-----------------|--------|-------------| | 1 | **Knife-Edge Follower** | — | Sharpest contact; ideal but causes wear. | | 2 | **Roller Follower** | $\bigcirc$ | Reduces wear via rolling contact. | | 3 | **Flat-Faced (Mushroom) Follower** | $\square$ | Conveys in-line thrust; lower wear. | | 4 | **Spherical-Faced Follower** | $\bullet$ | Compromise between roller and flat. | Followers can be further classified as: - **Translating (Knife-edge / Roller / Flat-faced)** — moves in a straight line. - **Oscillating (Pivoted)** — pivots about a fixed pin. ## 2.2 Motion Profiles (Displacement Diagrams) The cam profile encodes a **displacement vs. cam angle** relationship. The standard profiles studied in KTU 2024 are summarized below. ### 2.2.1 Uniform Velocity (Rectilinear) Follower moves with **constant velocity** during the stroke. Generates a **straight-line ramp** on the displacement diagram, but suffers from **infinite acceleration** at the start and end of stroke (impractical). $$y(\theta) = h \cdot \frac{\theta}{\beta}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le \beta$$ Velocity: $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ (constant). Acceleration: $a = 0$ during motion, $a = \pm\infty$ at $\theta = 0, \beta$. ### 2.2.2 Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) Follower displacement follows one-half cycle of a sinusoid. **Acceleration is finite** at the boundaries. $$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ $$\text{Velocity: } v(\theta) = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ $$\text{Acceleration: } a(\theta) = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ ### 2.2.3 Uniform Acceleration and Uniform Retardation (Parabolic) The most commonly used profile in **valve gears**. During the first half of the stroke, the follower accelerates uniformly; during the second half, it retards uniformly. $$y(\theta) = 2h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{2}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$$ $$y(\theta) = h\left[1 - 2\left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{2}\right], \quad \beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$$ ### 2.2.4 Cycloidal Motion The **smoothest** of all standard profiles — both velocity and acceleration are continuous (no jerk discontinuities). $$y(\theta) = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ $$v(\theta) = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ $$a(\theta) = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Table for Last-Minute Revision.** Memorize this table — these are the equations the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce verbatim. | # | Profile | Displacement $y(\theta)$ | Velocity $v(\theta)$ | Max Acceleration $a_{\max}$ | Use Case | |---|---------|---------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|----------| | 1 | **Uniform Velocity** | $\dfrac{h\theta}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ (constant) | $\infty$ (impractical) | Slow, low-speed toys | | 2 | **Simple Harmonic** | $\dfrac{h}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}$ | Punch presses, low-speed IC valves | | 3 | **Uniform Accel/Retard** | $2h\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{2}$ (first half) | $\dfrac{4h\omega\theta}{\beta^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta}$ | IC engine valve cams | | 4 | **Cycloidal** | $h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ | High-speed automation, textile cams | ### 2.3.1 Pressure Angle (Critical Design Parameter) For a **radial translating roller follower**, the pressure angle $\alpha$ is given by: $$\tan \alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \mp e}{R_b + y \pm e}$$ where $e$ is the **offset** of the follower from the cam axis (negative sign for offset on the side of follower motion; +ve for opposite side). For a **knife-edge radial follower** ($e = 0$, $r_r = 0$): $$\tan \alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{R_b + y}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** The **maximum pressure angle** is the design-limiting parameter. For moderate-speed cam mechanisms, $\alpha_{\max} \le 30°$ for translating followers and $\le 45°$ for oscillating followers. Exceeding this causes **jamming** — the side thrust becomes too high. ### 2.3.2 Cam Performance Metrics | Metric | Formula / Condition | KTU Significance | |--------|---------------------|------------------| | **Side thrust on follower** | $F_s = F_t \tan\alpha$ | Lower $\alpha \Rightarrow$ lower side thrust | | **Efficiency of cam drive** | $\eta = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{1}$ (approx., for light loads) | Maximized when $\alpha \to 0$ | | **Velocity of follower** | $v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ | Determines inertia forces | | **Acceleration of follower** | $a = \omega^{2} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}}$ | Determines dynamic load | | **Jerk (rate of change of accel.)** | $j = \omega^{3} \cdot \dfrac{d^{3}y}{d\theta^{3}}$ | Must be finite to avoid vibration | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Velocity and Acceleration for SHM Cam **Given:** A disc cam rotating at $\omega = 20$ rad/s drives a translating knife-edge follower through a stroke of $h = 40$ mm in a cam angle of $\beta = 90° = \pi/2$ rad, with **Simple Harmonic Motion** profile. **Find:** (a) Maximum velocity of follower, (b) Maximum acceleration, (c) Time to complete the outstroke. ### Step 1: Establish the displacement equation The SHM displacement equation is given by: $$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ ### Step 2: Differentiate w.r.t. cam angle $\theta$ to get velocity $$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) = \frac{\pi h}{2\beta}\sin\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ Multiplying by $\omega$ to obtain linear velocity: $$v(\theta) = \omega \cdot \frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}\sin\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Maximum velocity occurs when $\sin(\cdot) = 1$, i.e., at $\theta = \beta/2$ $$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$$ Substituting $h = 0.04$ m, $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $\beta = \pi/2$ rad: $$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04 \times 20}{2 \times \pi/2} = \frac{0.8\pi}{\pi} = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Step 4: Differentiate again to obtain acceleration $$a(\theta) = \omega^{2}\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} = \omega^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi^{2}h}{2\beta^{2}}\cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}\cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ ### Step 5: Maximum acceleration occurs at $\theta = 0$ (and $\theta = \beta$) $$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}$$ Substituting values: $$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 0.04 \times 20^{2}}{2 \times (\pi/2)^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 0.04 \times 400}{2 \times \pi^{2}/4} = \frac{16\pi^{2}}{0.5\pi^{2}} = 32 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$ ### Step 6: Time for the outstroke $$t_{out} = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{\pi/2}{20} = \frac{\pi}{40} \approx 0.0785 \text{ s}$$ ### Final Result Table | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Maximum follower velocity | $v_{\max}$ | **0.8** | m/s | | Maximum follower acceleration | $a_{\max}$ | **32** | m/s² | | Outstroke time | $t_{out}$ | **0.0785** | s | --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Angle for a Disc Cam with Offset Roller Follower **Given:** A disc cam has base-circle radius $R_b = 50$ mm. The roller follower (radius $r_r = 10$ mm) is **offset** by $e = 15$ mm from the cam axis. The follower lift is $h = 30$ mm. The motion is **uniform velocity** during the outstroke of $\beta = 120°$ rad. **Find:** The maximum pressure angle during the outstroke. ### Step 1: Velocity ratio for uniform motion For uniform velocity, $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ is constant: $$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta} = \frac{30}{2\pi/3} = \frac{30 \times 3}{2\pi} = \frac{45}{\pi} \approx 14.32 \text{ mm/rad}$$ ### Step 2: Pressure angle equation for offset radial follower $$\tan \alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} - e}{R_b + y + e}$$ (Subtractive offset reduces the pressure angle when the follower is offset in the direction of motion.) ### Step 3: Evaluate at the start and end of the outstroke **At $\theta = 0$:** $y = 0$, $dy/d\theta = h/\beta$ $$\tan \alpha_0 = \frac{14.32 - 15}{50 + 0 + 15} = \frac{-0.68}{65} \approx -0.01046$$ $$\alpha_0 \approx -0.6° \quad (\text{negative} \Rightarrow \text{offset over-compensates})$$ **At $\theta = \beta$:** $y = h$, $dy/d\theta = h/\beta$ (uniform) $$\tan \alpha_{\beta} = \frac{14.32 - 15}{50 + 30 + 15} = \frac{-0.68}{95} \approx -0.00716$$ $$\alpha_{\beta} \approx -0.41°$$ ### Step 4: Identify maximum pressure angle The maximum magnitude of pressure angle occurs at the **start of the outstroke** (the smallest denominator in the magnitude sense). The **maximum** is: $$\boxed{\alpha_{\max} \approx 0.6° \text{ at the start of the outstroke}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Offset followers are intentionally used to **reduce the maximum pressure angle** during the rise portion. This minimizes side thrust and increases the safe operating speed of the cam. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Cam Profile Construction (Graphical Method) For a **disc cam with a translating roller follower**, the construction steps are: 1. **Draw the displacement diagram** $y$ vs. $\theta$ on a Cartesian axis. 2. **Project the base circle** of radius $R_b$ from the cam center. 3. **Subdivide the displacement diagram** into 12 (or 24) equal angular increments along the $\theta$-axis. 4. **For each angular position $\theta_i$**, project horizontally to the displacement curve to obtain $y_i$, then add $R_b$ to get the radial distance $R_i = R_b + y_i$. 5. **Mark the follower center positions** along radial lines drawn from the cam axis. 6. **Draw circles of radius $r_r$** (roller radius) at each follower-center position. 7. **Draw a smooth common tangent** (the **pitch curve**) to all roller circles. 8. **The actual cam profile** is the curve offset *inward* by $r_r$ from the pitch curve. 9. For **knife-edge follower** ($r_r \to 0$), the pitch curve and cam profile coincide. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Note:** In the university lab, the cam is *traced* (kinematically inverted) by rotating the blank and sliding the follower — the same construction, executed by hardware. The knife-edge follower gives the **theoretical cam profile** directly, while the roller follower requires the offset step. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Motion Profiles | Property | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Uniform Accel/Retard | Cycloidal | |----------|------------------|-----|----------------------|-----------| | **Smoothness of velocity** | Smooth (constant) | Smooth | Discontinuous at midpoint | Smooth | | **Smoothness of acceleration** | Impractical (infinite) | Smooth (cosine) | Discontinuous (jump) | Smooth | | **Smoothness of jerk** | Impractical | Discontinuous | Discontinuous | **Smooth (continuous)** | | **Maximum velocity** | $h\omega/\beta$ | $\pi h \omega / (2\beta) \approx 1.57 h\omega/\beta$ | $2h\omega/\beta$ | $2h\omega/\beta$ | | **Maximum acceleration** | $\infty$ | $\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}/(2\beta^{2}) \approx 4.93 h\omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$ | $4h\omega^{2}/\beta$ | $2\pi h\omega^{2}/\beta^{2} \approx 6.28 h\omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$ | | **Suitability for high-speed** | Poor | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** | | **KTU Mark Allocation** | **2 marks** (define) | **5 marks** (derive) | **7 marks** (full profile) | **7–14 marks** (advanced) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Cam–Follower Subsystem The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional architecture of a cam-driven mechatronic subsystem** and the interaction between the cam, follower, and feedback elements. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Prime Mover
Motor at angular velocity w] --> B[Cam Shaft
Rotating element] B --> C[Cam Profile
Geometric program] C --> D[Contact Pair
Higher Pair] D --> E[Follower
Translating or Oscillating] E --> F[Output Work Element
Valve / Indexing Table / Plunger] G[Return Spring
Force Closure] -. Maintains Contact .-> D H[Displacement Sensor
Encoder or LVDT] -. Feedback .-> F I[Controller
PLC or Microcontroller] -. Compares Actual vs Setpoint .-> A subgraph "Motion Profile Encoding" C --> C1[Displacement Diagram
y vs theta] C1 --> C2[Profile Type
SHM / Cycloidal / UAR] end ``` ## 4.2 Classification Tree of Cam Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cam Mechanisms] --> B[By Cam Geometry] A --> C[By Follower Type] B --> B1[Disc Cam
Most common] B --> B2[Cylindrical Cam
Groove type] B --> B3[Conical Cam] B --> B4[Spherical Cam] C --> C1[Translating Follower] C --> C2[Oscillating Follower] C1 --> D1[Knife Edge] C1 --> D2[Roller Follower] C1 --> D3[Flat Faced] C2 --> E1[Pivoted Knife Edge] C2 --> E2[Pivoted Roller] C2 --> E3[Pivoted Flat Faced] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Cam Profile Generation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Specify Follower Motion
y = f of theta] --> S2[Step 2: Construct Displacement Diagram
y vs theta] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Draw Base Circle
Radius Rb] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Mark Angular Divisions
theta1 theta2 ... thetan] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Project Heights
Ri = Rb plus yi] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Mark Follower Centers
on Radial Lines] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Draw Pitch Curve
For Roller Follower] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Offset by Roller Radius
to Get Cam Profile] S8 --> S9[Final Cam Profile
Ready for Manufacture] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Force Analysis on Translating Follower ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Follower Experiences Two Force Components] --> B[Normal Reaction N
Along Common Normal] A --> C[Tangential Friction F
Opposing Sliding] B --> D[Axial Force Ft
Drives the Follower] B --> E[Side Thrust Fs
Pushes Follower Sideways] D --> F[Useful Work Component] E --> G[Wasted Component
Increases Bearing Load] H[Pressure Angle alpha] --> I[Determines Ratio Fs to Ft
Fs over Ft = tan alpha] I --> J[Design Rule: alpha max less than 30 degrees
Translating Follower] I --> K[Design Rule: alpha max less than 45 degrees
Oscillating Follower] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Interpretation Note:** When the KTU paper says "*with neat sketch*", these block diagrams and the standard 12-division construction geometry are the **expected deliverable**. Drawing a freehand or approximately rectangular cam profile loses 2–3 marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a cam. List any four types of cams based on shape.** **Model Answer:** A **cam** is a mechanical element, having a specially profiled surface or a groove, which imparts a predetermined motion to another element called the **follower** through direct contact. The four types of cams based on shape are: 1. **Disc (Plate) Cam** — flat plate with contoured profile. 2. **Cylindrical (Barrel) Cam** — cylindrical body with a groove. 3. **Conical Cam** — tapered conical body. 4. **Spherical (Globoidal) Cam** — spherical body with a contoured groove. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is pressure angle? Why is its magnitude important in cam design?** **Model Answer:** **Pressure angle ($\alpha$)** is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the **common normal** to the pitch curve at the point of contact between the cam and the follower. **Importance:** - It determines the **side thrust** on the follower: $F_s = F_t \tan\alpha$. - A **large pressure angle** causes excessive side thrust, leading to jamming, higher friction, and premature wear. - For translating followers, $\alpha_{\max} \le 30°$; for oscillating followers, $\alpha_{\max} \le 45°$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Options (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You are given an **internal choice** between two questions from the same module. Below are two fully-solved alternatives. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a disc cam profile for a **translating roller follower** following **Simple Harmonic Motion** during the outstroke and the return stroke. Clearly state the steps involved. **(b) [7 Marks)** A disc cam rotating at **300 rpm** drives a **knife-edge follower** through a stroke of **25 mm** in **120°** of cam rotation using **uniform acceleration and retardation** profile. Calculate: (i) Maximum velocity of the follower, (ii) Maximum acceleration, and (iii) Time required for the outstroke. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — Cam Profile Construction **Step 1: Displacement Diagram** - On graph paper, draw two perpendicular axes: cam angle $\theta$ along the abscissa and follower lift $y$ along the ordinate. - Plot the SHM profile: $y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ for outstroke and return. - Include a **dwell** (horizontal line) at top and bottom if required. **Step 2: Base Circle** - Choose base circle radius $R_b$ (typically $R_b \ge 1.5h$ to keep $\alpha$ small). - Draw the base circle of radius $R_b$ with the cam axis $O$ as center. **Step 3: Angular Division** - Divide the displacement diagram (outstroke + return) into **12 equal angular segments** $\Delta\theta = \beta/12$. - Through $O$, draw radial lines $OA_1, OA_2, \ldots, OA_{12}$ at these angles. **Step 4: Project Heights** - From each division point on the displacement curve, project horizontally to the corresponding radial line. Mark these points as the **follower center positions** $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_{12}$. - Distance $OB_i = R_b + y_i$. **Step 5: Roller Circles and Pitch Curve** - At each $B_i$, draw a circle of radius $r_r$ (roller radius). - The **pitch curve** is the smooth curve tangent to all roller circles. **Step 6: Cam Profile** - The **actual cam profile** is drawn offset *inward* from the pitch curve by $r_r$. - This is the working surface of the cam. > *(A neat sketch should accompany the answer; the steps above are the verbal component. Award 7 marks: 1 mark per correct step + 1 mark for the sketch. The KTU examiner awards 1 mark for each correctly labeled element — base circle, prime circle, pitch curve, roller positions, cam profile.)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical on UAR Profile **Given:** - $N = 300$ rpm $\Rightarrow$ $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416$ rad/s - $h = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $\beta = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \approx 2.094$ rad - Motion: Uniform Acceleration & Retardation (UAR / Parabolic) **Formulae for UAR profile:** $$v_{\max} = \frac{2 h \omega}{\beta}$$ $$a_{\max} = \frac{4 h \omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$$ **Step 1: Maximum velocity** $$v_{\max} = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 10\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.5\pi \times 3}{2\pi} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2: Maximum acceleration** $$a_{\max} = \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times (10\pi)^{2}}{(2\pi/3)^{2}} = \frac{0.1 \times 100\pi^{2}}{4\pi^{2}/9} = \frac{10\pi^{2} \times 9}{4\pi^{2}} = 22.5 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$ **Step 3: Outstroke time** $$t_{out} = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi/3}{10\pi} = \frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667 \text{ s}$$ **Final Results:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $v_{\max}$ | **0.75 m/s** | | $a_{\max}$ | **22.5 m/s²** | | $t_{out}$ | **0.0667 s** | --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** and **Cycloidal** displacement profiles for a cam-driven follower. Discuss their suitability for high-speed mechanisms based on the smoothness of velocity, acceleration, and jerk. **(b) [7 Marks]** A disc cam with a **translating roller follower** has a base circle of radius **60 mm**, roller radius **15 mm**, and stroke **30 mm**. The cam rotates at **240 rpm** with uniform velocity motion during an outstroke of **60°**. Determine the **maximum pressure angle**. State whether the cam design is safe. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison SHM vs Cycloidal | Parameter | SHM Profile | Cycloidal Profile | |-----------|-------------|-------------------| | **Displacement** | $\frac{h}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $h\!\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | | **Velocity continuity** | Continuous (zero at boundaries) | Continuous (zero at boundaries) | | **Acceleration continuity** | Continuous, max at $\theta = 0$ and $\beta$ | Continuous, zero at boundaries | | **Jerk continuity** | **Discontinuous** (cosine has finite jumps) | **Continuous** (sinusoidal jerk) | | **Maximum velocity** | $\pi h\omega/(2\beta)$ | $2h\omega/\beta$ | | **Maximum acceleration** | $\pi^{2} h\omega^{2}/(2\beta^{2})$ | $2\pi h\omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$ | | **Dynamic smoothness** | Moderate | **Excellent** | | **Suitability for high-speed** | Good up to moderate speeds | **Preferred for high-speed** | **Engineering Conclusion:** The **cycloidal profile** is preferred for high-speed cam mechanisms because its **jerk (third derivative of displacement) is finite everywhere**. SHM, although smooth in velocity and acceleration, exhibits **discontinuous jerk** at $\theta = 0, \beta/2, \beta$, producing **higher-order vibrations** at high speeds. SHM is recommended for moderate-speed applications like pump plungers and slow valve gear. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Pressure Angle Calculation **Given:** - $R_b = 60$ mm, $r_r = 15$ mm, $h = 30$ mm - $N = 240$ rpm $\Rightarrow$ $\omega = 2\pi \times 240/60 = 8\pi$ rad/s - $\beta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad - Motion: **Uniform velocity** (no offset, $e = 0$) **For uniform velocity motion:** $$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta} = \frac{30}{\pi/3} = \frac{90}{\pi} \approx 28.65 \text{ mm/rad}$$ **Pressure angle for radial translating roller follower** (the roller radius does **not** enter the tangent formula at the pitch curve level; we work with the pitch-curve coordinates): $$\tan \alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}}{R_b + y}$$ **Maximum $\alpha$ occurs at $y = 0$** (start of outstroke) because the denominator is minimum and $dy/d\theta$ is constant. $$\tan \alpha_{\max} = \frac{28.65}{60 + 0} = 0.4775$$ $$\alpha_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(0.4775) \approx 25.5°$$ **Safety check:** Since $\alpha_{\max} \approx 25.5° < 30°$ (KTU standard for translating followers), **the cam design is SAFE** and the follower will not jam. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!)** > 1. **Confusing cam types vs. follower types** — Disc cam and roller follower are *independent* classifications; do not mix them up. (–2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — $\beta$ must be in **radians** in SHM and cycloidal equations, but **degrees** in the displacement diagram. Convert consistently. (–2 marks) > 3. **Ignoring the prime circle** — In the cam profile sketch, the prime circle ($R_p = R_b + r_r$) must be drawn distinctly. Skipping it = –1 mark. > 4. **Wrong sign in pressure-angle formula** — The offset $e$ sign depends on the direction of offset relative to follower motion. Read the KTU diagram carefully. (–2 marks) > 5. **Using knife-edge equation for a roller follower** — Always use the **pitch-curve coordinates** ($R_b + r_r + y$ for radial follower). A common error. (–2 marks) > 6. **Not stating the safety condition** — Always conclude a pressure-angle problem with the statement "$\alpha_{\max} < 30°$, hence the design is safe" or its converse. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!** - ✅ A **cam** converts **rotary motion → prescribed linear/oscillating motion** via a higher-pair contact. - ✅ **Disc cams** with **translating followers** are the most common configuration in KTU problems. - ✅ **Roller followers** reduce wear; the **cam profile** is the pitch curve offset inward by $r_r$. - ✅ **Base circle** = smallest cam circle. **Prime circle** = $R_b + r_r$ (roller follower). - ✅ **Pressure angle** $\alpha$ is critical; $\alpha_{\max} \le 30°$ (translating) / $45°$ (oscillating). - ✅ **Uniform velocity**: simple but has **infinite acceleration** at stroke ends — practically not used for high speeds. - ✅ **SHM**: finite acceleration at boundaries; discontinuous jerk — *use for moderate speeds*. - ✅ **Uniform Accel/Retard (UAR)**: $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$; **standard IC engine valve profile**. - ✅ **Cycloidal**: $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$, $a_{\max} = 2\pi h\omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$; *smoothest, used for high-speed automation*. - ✅ **Side thrust** $F_s = F_t \tan\alpha$ — minimized by offset followers. - ✅ **Pitch curve** is what the *follower center* traces; **cam profile** is what *physically contacts* the follower. - ✅ In the KTU exam, **always draw a neat displacement diagram first** (outstroke, dwell, return, dwell) before the cam profile. - ✅ **Conventions**: $\beta$ in radians for formula, in degrees for diagram; convert using $\beta_{rad} = \beta_{deg} \times \pi/180$. - ✅ The cam with a **constant-dwell** at maximum lift is called a **heart-shaped cam** — used in clock escapements. - ✅ **Eccentric cam** = a special disc cam whose profile is a *circle* of radius $e$ (eccentricity) offset from the cam axis — equivalent to a uniform-velocity mechanism.

topics.title: Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gear Trains ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Gear Train** is a mechanical power transmission system consisting of two or more meshing gears arranged in series, parallel, or coaxial configurations, used to transmit rotational motion and torque from one shaft to another while modifying the **speed**, **torque**, or **direction of rotation** between the input (driver) and output (driven) members. In Mechatronics, gear trains are fundamental **actuation subsystems** that interface prime movers (DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors) with mechanical loads (robot joints, conveyor rollers, steering mechanisms, wheel drives). They are classified into four canonical configurations: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2 – Gear Trains):** > Students must be able to (a) identify the type of gear train, (b) compute the **train value (speed ratio)** and **mechanical advantage (torque ratio)**, and (c) analyze simple, compound, reverted, and **epicyclic (planetary) gear trains** — the last being the most heavily weighted topic in KTU university examinations. ### 1.2 The Four Canonical Gear Train Classes | Class | Defining Geometry | Typical Speed Ratio | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **Simple Gear Train** | One gear per shaft | 2 : 1 to 10 : 1 | Medium | | **Compound Gear Train** | Two or more gears rigidly mounted on the same intermediate shaft | 10 : 1 to 100 : 1 | High | | **Reverted Gear Train** | Input and output shafts are **coaxial** | 5 : 1 to 50 : 1 | Medium | | **Epicyyclic / Planetary Gear Train** | Gears orbit on a movable carrier (arm) | 5 : 1 to 1000 : 1 | **Very High** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Gear System Imagine you are cycling uphill. You shift to a **low gear**: the chain connects a small front sprocket to a large rear sprocket. One full pedal revolution produces only a fraction of a wheel revolution — but your leg force is multiplied into a powerful pushing force at the wheel. This is exactly what a **compound gear train** does in a mechatronic actuator: - **Small driver gear** (high speed, low torque) = the motor shaft. - **Large driven gear** (low speed, high torque) = the load shaft. - **Meshing teeth** = the "gear ratio" that mathematically exchanges rotational speed for torque. > [!NOTE] > **Conservation Principle:** In an ideal (lossless) gear train, the input power equals the output power. Therefore: > > $$P_{in} = P_{out} \;\Rightarrow\; T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}$$ > > Reducing speed by a factor of 5 **multiplies torque by a factor of 5** — the fundamental reason gear trains are indispensable in robotics, EV drivetrains, and CNC machine tools. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics - **Module (m):** the ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth, standardized to ensure interchangeability of meshing gears. For SI gears, **m** is expressed in **mm**, with standard values of **1 mm, 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, 3 mm, 4 mm, 5 mm, 6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm**. - **Pressure Angle (φ):** the angle between the line of action and the common tangent at the pitch point; **standard value is 20°** (with 14.5° as legacy). - **Pitch Circle Diameter (d):** $d = m \cdot T$, where **T** is the number of teeth. - **Circular Pitch (p):** $p = \pi \cdot m$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Law of Gearing For two meshing gears to transmit motion without slip and without tooth interference, the **linear velocities at the pitch point must be equal**: $$v = \pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$$ where: - $d_1, d_2$ = pitch circle diameters (in **m** or **mm**) - $N_1, N_2$ = rotational speeds in **rpm** Substituting $d = m \cdot T$ and canceling common terms yields the **Master Relation** for any meshing pair: $$\boxed{\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Memory Hook:** The gear with the **larger number of teeth rotates slower**. The speed ratio is always **driver : driven = driven teeth : driver teeth**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Gear Trains | # | Gear Train Type | Speed Ratio Equation | Train Value (i) | Notes / Constraints | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Simple Gear Train** (n gears in series) | $\dfrac{N_1}{N_n} = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdots T_{2k}}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdots T_{2k-1}}$ | $i = \dfrac{\prod T_{driven}}{\prod T_{driver}}$ | Each shaft carries **only one** gear | | 2 | **Compound Gear Train** | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_{last}} = \dfrac{\text{Product of driven teeth}}{\text{Product of driver teeth}}$ | Same form as simple, but driven & driver alternate on same shaft | Intermediate shafts have **two** gears rigidly fixed | | 3 | **Reverted Gear Train** | $i = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}$ | Geometric constraint: $m(T_1 + T_2) = m(T_3 + T_4)$ | $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$ | | 4 | **Epicyclic — Tabular Method** | $\dfrac{N_x - N_{arm}}{N_y - N_{arm}} = \dfrac{\text{Product of teeth linking x to y (as driver in reverted arm-frozen frame)}}{\text{Product of teeth linking y to x}}$ | Solve linear equation | Fix one member; rotate arm by +1 | | 5 | **Epicyclic — Algebraic** (Willis Equation) | $\dfrac{N_A - N_C}{N_B - N_C} = \pm \dfrac{\text{Product of driven teeth (A-side)}}{\texttext{Product of driver teeth (B-side)}}$ | $N_C$ = carrier (arm) speed | Sign: (+) same direction, (−) opposite | | 6 | **Mechanical Advantage (Torque Ratio)** | $MA = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} = i \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (≈ 0.95 per mesh) | Power-conservation derived | ### 2.3 Operational Logic of Each Step **Why "driver" vs "driven" flips on intermediate shafts:** In a compound gear train, a gear rigidly fixed to a shaft becomes the *driver* for the *next* meshing pair. The shaft is the rigid mechanical reference — if gear A (40 teeth) and gear B (60 teeth) are both keyed to the same intermediate shaft, they share the same angular velocity: $N_A = N_B$, and gear B then drives the next gear on the subsequent shaft. **Why a reverted gear train is geometrically constrained:** Because the input and output shafts are coaxial, the center distance between driver and driven must be identical for both meshes. Since center distance $C = \dfrac{m}{2}(T_a + T_b)$, equating the two center distances forces $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4$. **Why epicyclic trains are special:** The carrier (arm) is itself a moving member. Each gear's motion is the **vector sum** of its rotation *relative to the arm* and the arm's rotation in space. This gives epicyclic trains their compactness and very high ratios (single-stage ratios of 10:1 or more) — critical in **servo-robot joints** and **automatic transmissions**. ### 2.4 Real-World Mechatronic Applications > [!NOTE] > - **Industrial Robotics:** Harmonic-drive (a special epicyclic variant) gear reducers achieve ratios of 30:1 to 320:1 in a single stage, with near-zero backlash — the gold standard for robot joint actuators. > - **CNC Machine Tools:** Compound gear trains in the spindle headstock provide a wide range of speed selections. > - **Automotive Transmissions:** Modern 8-speed and 10-speed automatic gearboxes are **compound epicyclic** sets (Ravigneaux and Lepelletier arrangements). > - **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Single-speed planetary reducers in Tesla, Nissan Leaf, and Tata Nexon EV drivetrains. > - **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor swashplates use reverted gear trains for pitch control. > - **Aerospace reaction wheels & gyroscopes:** Reverted compound trains maintain high precision in attitude control. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Simple Gear Train — Train Value **Setup:** Three gears in series, gears 1 → 2 → 3, all on separate parallel shafts. **Step 1:** At the first mesh, gears 1 and 2 share the same pitch-line velocity: $$\pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$$ **Step 2:** Since $d = mT$, and modules are equal for meshing gears: $$m T_1 N_1 = m T_2 N_2 \;\Rightarrow\; N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the same logic to the second mesh (gears 2 and 3): $$N_3 = N_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_3}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $N_2$ from Step 2: $$N_3 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_3} = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_3}$$ **Step 5:** Generalize to *n* gears in series. Telescoping cancellation removes all intermediate tooth counts: $$\boxed{N_{last} = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdots T_{2k-1}}{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdots T_{2k}}}$$ **Step 6:** Train value (speed ratio) $i = N_1 / N_{last}$: $$\boxed{i = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdots T_{2k}}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdots T_{2k-1}} = \dfrac{\text{Product of driven teeth}}{\text{Product of driver teeth}}}$$ > **Numerical Sanity Check (KTU-style):** If a 20-tooth driver meshes with a 60-tooth driven, the speed ratio is 1:3. Power balance demands torque ratio = 3:1 (assuming 100% efficiency). If 1 W is input at 100 rpm, output is 33.3 rpm at 0.3 N·m — verifiable in SI units. --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: Reverted Gear Train — Center-Distance Constraint **Setup:** Input shaft 1 carries gear 1 (driver, $T_1$ teeth) meshing with gear 2 ($T_2$ teeth) on the intermediate shaft. On the same intermediate shaft, gear 3 ($T_3$ teeth) drives gear 4 ($T_4$ teeth) on the output shaft, which is **coaxial with the input shaft**. **Step 1:** Center distance between shaft 1 and intermediate shaft: $$C_1 = \dfrac{m}{2}(T_1 + T_2)$$ **Step 2:** Center distance between intermediate shaft and output shaft: $$C_2 = \dfrac{m}{2}(T_3 + T_4)$$ **Step 3:** Because both meshes share the same axis (shaft 1 = shaft 4 axis), the two center distances must be equal: $$C_1 = C_2$$ **Step 4:** Cancel $m/2$ from both sides: $$\boxed{T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4}$$ **Step 5:** The train value for a reverted gear train is: $$i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_4} = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}$$ **Worked Example (KTU Model):** Design a reverted gear train with module 4 mm, speed ratio 8:1, and 20° pressure angle, such that $T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4 = 100$ (assumed minimum-teeth sum to avoid interference). * Choose $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 80$ (ratio of 4:1 on first mesh). * We need $T_2/T_1 \times T_4/T_3 = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; (80/20) \cdot (T_4/T_3) = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; T_4/T_3 = 2$. * Apply center constraint: $T_3 + T_4 = 100$ and $T_4 = 2T_3$. * Solving: $3 T_3 = 100 \;\Rightarrow\; T_3 = 33.33$ (non-integer). Re-try with $T_1 + T_2 = 96$: * $T_1 = 24$, $T_2 = 72$, $T_3 = 32$, $T_4 = 64$. Verify: $T_2/T_1 \times T_4/T_3 = (72/24)(64/32) = 3 \times 2 = 6 \neq 8$. * Try $T_1 = 18$, $T_2 = 90$ (5:1), then $T_4/T_3 = 8/5 = 1.6$, $T_3 + T_4 = 108 \;\Rightarrow\; 2.6 T_3 = 108 \;\Rightarrow\; T_3 = 41.54$ — not integer. > [!NOTE] > Reverted gear train design is **over-constrained**: you cannot freely choose ratio and tooth sum independently. KTU problems often give a *target ratio* and ask the student to determine the nearest integer solution — a common 7-mark problem. --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Epicyclic Gear Train — Willis Equation (Algebraic Method) **Setup:** A 2K-H epicyclic train with sun gear A (driver, $T_A$ teeth), planet gear B ($T_B$ teeth), ring (annulus) gear C ($T_C$ teeth), and arm (carrier) D rotating at $N_D$. **Step 1:** Hold the carrier (arm) D **stationary** by giving the entire gear train an angular velocity of $-N_D$. In this *frozen-arm frame*, all gears rotate at: $$N_i' = N_i - N_D$$ **Step 2:** The frozen-arm frame is now equivalent to a **simple gear train** (gears mounted on fixed centers). The ratio between any two gears $X$ and $Y$ in this frame is: $$\dfrac{N_X - N_D}{N_Y - N_D} = \dfrac{\text{Product of driven teeth between X and Y}}{\text{Product of driver teeth between X and Y}}$$ **Step 3:** For the classic 2K-H planetary, with sun A meshing planet B, planet B meshing ring C (the "internal mesh" reverses sign because ring teeth are cut internally): $$\dfrac{N_A - N_D}{N_C - N_D} = -\dfrac{T_B \cdot T_C}{T_A \cdot T_B} = -\dfrac{T_C}{T_A}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify (Willis Equation): $$\boxed{\dfrac{N_A - N_D}{N_C - N_D} = -\dfrac{T_C}{T_A}}$$ > **Sign Convention:** External meshes contribute **positive** ratios; internal meshes (ring gear) contribute **negative** ratios. **Step 5: Special Cases (KTU-favorite numerical problems):** - **Ring fixed ($N_C = 0$):** $N_A = -N_D \cdot T_C / T_A + N_D = N_D(1 + T_C/T_A)$. Ratio $N_A/N_D = 1 + T_C/T_A$. - **Sun fixed ($N_A = 0$):** $N_C = N_D(1 - T_A/T_C)$ — for reverse direction. - **Arm fixed ($N_D = 0$):** Reduces to simple train ratio $N_A/N_C = -T_C/T_A$. - **Two outputs simultaneously:** The sun, ring, and arm can each be an output (a clutch/transmission selects one) — this is the principle behind **automatic automotive gearboxes**. --- ### 3.4 Derivation D: Epicyclic Gear Train — Tabular Method (Most Frequently Asked in KTU) **Setup:** An epicyclic train with gears A, B, C, D and arm (carrier) H. The arm carries the planet(s); the ring is fixed in this problem. Find the speed of the arm given the speed of the sun. **Step 1:** Construct a **table with 6 columns**: | Operation | Gear A | Gear B | Gear C | Gear D | Arm H | |---|---|---|---|---|---| **Step 2:** Row 1 — *Fix the member whose speed is given or required* and **add +1 rotation to the arm**: * Arm H: +1 (we are adding 1 to the arm). * All gears: assigned values such that the train value of "frozen arm" ratio is satisfied. * For the 2K-H with ring fixed: planet gears rotate at $N_B' = N_C' \cdot (T_C/T_B)$ in the *frozen* frame… but with ring fixed and arm +1, planet gets $-T_C/T_B$ rotations. **Step 3:** Row 2 — Multiply Row 1 by an unknown $x$ so that the boundary condition (fixed ring ⇒ $N_C = 0$) is satisfied: * $N_C^{Row2} = N_C^{Row1} \cdot x = -1 \cdot x = 0$ ⇒ Wait, this means the ring must rotate $-1$ in Row 1. Multiply by $x$ to set ring to its actual boundary value. **Step 4:** Row 3 — Add Row 2 to Row 1 (superposition principle, valid because all equations are linear): $$N_i^{Row3} = N_i^{Row1} + N_i^{Row2}$$ **Step 5:** Apply the second boundary condition (e.g., given sun speed) to Row 3 to find $x$ explicitly: $$N_A^{Row3} = N_A^{given}$$ **Step 6:** Read off the arm speed, ring speed, or any gear speed from Row 3. > [!IMPORTANT] > The tabular method is the **fastest, lowest-error approach** for KTU epicyclic problems — examiners in the 2024 scheme expect students to set up the table correctly in the first 2 minutes of a 14-mark question. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU Problems ``` ┌─ Are gears on separate parallel shafts? ──── YES ──► SIMPLE GEAR TRAIN │ Use i = ∏T_driven / ∏T_driver │ Gear Train? ────┼─ Are two gears on the same shaft? ──────────── YES ──► COMPOUND GEAR TRAIN │ Same form; mark by teeth product │ ├─ Are input & output shafts COAXIAL? ────────── YES ──► REVERTED GEAR TRAIN │ Add T₁+T₂ = T₃+T₄ constraint │ └─ Is there a moving arm carrying the planet? ── YES ──► EPICYCLIC TRAIN Use Willis OR Tabular Method ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Simple vs Compound vs Reverted ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[MOTOR SHAFT] -->|Gear 1
20T Driver| B1[Intermediate Shaft
One gear only] B1 -->|Gear 2
60T Driven| C1[Output Shaft] A2[MOTOR SHAFT
Gear A: 20T] -->|Mesh 1| B2[Intermediate Shaft
Gear B: 60T AND
Gear C: 15T] B2 -->|Mesh 2
Gear C drives D| C2[Output Shaft
Gear D: 45T] A3[Input Shaft 1
Center Axis X-X] -->|Gear 1: 20T| B3[Intermediate Shaft
Offset from X-X] B3 -->|Gear 3: 20T| C3[Output Shaft
Center Axis X-X
Same as input] style A1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B1 fill:#7B68EE,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B2 fill:#7B68EE,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C2 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A3 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B3 fill:#7B68EE,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C3 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EPICYCLIC_ASSEMBLY direction TB ARM[CARRIER / ARM H
Carries planet gear shafts] SUN[SUN GEAR A
External teeth T_A] PLANET[PLANET GEAR B
External teeth T_B] RING[RING GEAR C
Internal teeth T_C] PLANET -.->|External mesh| SUN PLANET -.->|Internal mesh| RING ARM -->|Supports shaft of| PLANET end INPUT[INPUT TORQUE] -->|Applied to| SUN RING -->|Can be FIXED
or OUTPUT| FIXED_OR_OUT{Condition} ARM -->|OUTPUT SHAFT
or INPUT| OUTPUT[OUTPUT SHAFT] FIXED_OR_OUT -->|Fixed to ground| GROUND[GROUND] FIXED_OR_OUT -->|Rotating| RING_OUTPUT[RING OUTPUT] style ARM fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style SUN fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style PLANET fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000 style RING fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000 style INPUT fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style OUTPUT fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style GROUND fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff style RING_OUTPUT fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Power Flow & Speed-Torque Trade-off ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT_END M[MOTOR
High RPM
Low Torque] end subgraph GEAR_TRAIN G1[Gear Train
Ratio i = N_in/N_out] end subgraph OUTPUT_END L[LOAD
Low RPM
High Torque] end M -->|omega_in, T_in| G1 G1 -->|omega_out = omega_in / i
T_out = T_in times i times eta| L M -.->|Power P = T times omega
is conserved| L style M fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Functional Block — Application Mapping (Block-Level Fallback) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ROBOTICS R1[Robot joint actuator] --> R2[Harmonic drive] R2 --> R3[Planetary reducer] R3 --> R4[Output link] end subgraph AUTOMOTIVE A1[Engine / Motor] --> A2[Compound epicyclic set] A2 --> A3[Clutch / Brake selection] A3 --> A4[Drive wheels] end subgraph CNC C1[Spindle motor] --> C2[Compound gear box] C2 --> C3[Spindle] C3 --> C4[Tool and workpiece] end subgraph AEROSPACE AE1[Servomotor] --> AE2[Reverted gear pair] AE2 --> AE3[Swashplate] AE3 --> AE4[Rotor blade pitch] end style R1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A3 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A4 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C2 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C3 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C4 fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#fff style AE1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style AE2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style AE3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style AE4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a "Gear Train" and explain any three types of gear trains used in mechatronic systems with one example application for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **gear train** is a combination of two or more meshing gears used to transmit motion and power between shafts, altering the **speed, torque, or direction** of rotation. *Simple Gear Train:* Each shaft carries only one gear. Example: a hand-cranked winch driving a large spool through a single reduction stage. *Compound Gear Train:* Intermediate shafts carry two or more gears. Example: a CNC lathe spindle gearbox that provides 8 selectable speed steps from one motor. *Epicyclic / Planetary Gear Train:* Has a sun gear, planet gears, a ring gear, and a movable carrier (arm). Example: a single-speed reducer in a Tesla Model 3 EV drivetrain (ratio ~9:1). *(1 mark per type with example; concise — exactly what a 3-mark answer needs.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State the "Law of Gearing" and derive the relationship between the number of teeth and speeds of two meshing gears.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Law of Gearing** states that the common normal at the point of contact of two meshing gear teeth must pass through the **pitch point**, ensuring a constant velocity ratio and rolling (non-slipping) contact. For two meshing gears with $T_1$ and $T_2$ teeth, pitch circle diameters $d_1$ and $d_2$, and rotational speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$: $$\pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2 \quad \text{(equal pitch-line velocities)}$$ Since $d = mT$ and modules are equal: $$\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$$ *(1 mark: statement of law; 1 mark: equality of pitch-line velocities; 1 mark: derived relation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Simple & Compound Gear Trains with Numerical Design* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** A compound gear train consists of four gears. Gear 1 (driver) has 18 teeth and meshes with gear 2 (45 teeth) on the intermediate shaft. Gear 3 (20 teeth) on the same intermediate shaft meshes with gear 4 (driven, 60 teeth). If the input speed is 1440 rpm, determine: (i) the speed of the output shaft, and (ii) the torque at the output shaft if the input torque is 25 N·m. Assume 100% efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **reverted gear train** for a mechatronic actuator with module $m = 4$ mm and a required speed ratio of 9:1. The minimum number of teeth on any pinion (smaller gear) is 18 to avoid undercutting. Determine suitable integer tooth counts for all four gears and verify the center-distance constraint. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) (i) Speed of output shaft — 4 marks** The train value for a compound gear train is: $$i = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3} = \dfrac{45 \times 60}{18 \times 20} = \dfrac{2700}{360} = 7.5$$ Therefore: $$N_4 = \dfrac{N_1}{i} = \dfrac{1440}{7.5} = \mathbf{192 \text{ rpm}}$$ **(a) (ii) Torque at output shaft — 3 marks** With 100% efficiency, power balance gives: $$T_{out} = T_{in} \times i = 25 \times 7.5 = \mathbf{187.5 \text{ N·m}}$$ *(Quick verification: $P_{in} = 25 \times (2\pi \times 1440/60) = 25 \times 150.8 = 3770$ W; $P_{out} = 187.5 \times (2\pi \times 192/60) = 187.5 \times 20.1 = 3769$ W ≈ 3770 W ✓)* --- **(b) Reverted gear train design — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Apply center-distance constraint:** $$T_1 + T_2 = T_3 + T_4 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the speed ratio requirement:** $$\dfrac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3} = 9 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply minimum-tooth constraint:** $$T_1 \geq 18, \quad T_3 \geq 18 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 4 — Systematically search for integer solutions.** Let $T_1 = 18$ (minimum). We need to find $T_2$ such that the equation system has integer solutions. From constraint: $T_3 + T_4 = 18 + T_2$. From ratio: $T_2 \cdot T_4 = 9 \cdot T_1 \cdot T_3 = 9 \cdot 18 \cdot T_3 = 162 T_3$. Substituting $T_4 = 18 + T_2 - T_3$: $$T_2 (18 + T_2 - T_3) = 162 T_3$$ $$18 T_2 + T_2^2 - T_2 T_3 = 162 T_3$$ $$T_2^2 + 18 T_2 = T_3 (T_2 + 162)$$ $$T_3 = \dfrac{T_2^2 + 18 T_2}{T_2 + 162}$$ We need $T_3$ to be a positive integer $\geq 18$. Try values: | $T_2$ | $T_3$ computation | $T_3$ | $T_4 = 18 + T_2 - T_3$ | Valid? | |---|---|---|---|---| | 60 | $(3600 + 1080)/222$ | 21.08 | — | No | | 72 | $(5184 + 1296)/234$ | 27.69 | — | No | | 90 | $(8100 + 1620)/252$ | 38.57 | — | No | | 108 | $(11664 + 1944)/270$ | **50.4** | — | No | | 126 | $(15876 + 2268)/288$ | **63** | $18 + 126 - 63 = 81$ | **YES** ✓ | **Step 5 — Verify the design:** $$T_1 = 18, \quad T_2 = 126, \quad T_3 = 63, \quad T_4 = 81$$ Center-distance check: $T_1 + T_2 = 18 + 126 = 144$ and $T_3 + T_4 = 63 + 81 = 144$ ✓ Ratio check: $\dfrac{126 \times 81}{18 \times 63} = \dfrac{10206}{1134} = \mathbf{9.0}$ ✓ Pitch diameters: $d_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72$ mm; $d_2 = 504$ mm; $d_3 = 252$ mm; $d_4 = 324$ mm. Center distance: $(72 + 504)/2 = (252 + 324)/2 = 288$ mm ✓ **Final Answer:** $T_1 = 18, T_2 = 126, T_3 = 63, T_4 = 81$, module $m = 4$ mm. --- ### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train Analysis* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Aug 2024]** **(a)** An epicyclic gear train has a sun gear with 20 teeth, three identical planet gears (each with 30 teeth), and a ring (annulus) gear with 80 teeth. The arm (carrier) is the output, and the ring gear is **fixed to the ground**. (i) Set up the **Willis Equation** for this arrangement. (ii) Determine the **speed ratio** $N_{sun}/N_{arm}$ and the **direction of rotation** of the arm relative to the sun. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **tabular method**, solve the same problem: if the sun rotates at 600 rpm clockwise, find the arm speed and the planet-gear speed. Also, calculate the **mechanical advantage** if the efficiency of each planet mesh is 0.96. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) (i) Willis Equation Setup — 3 marks** Let: - Gear A = sun, $T_A = 20$, speed $N_A$ - Gear B = planet, $T_B = 30$, speed $N_B$ - Gear C = ring, $T_C = 80$, speed $N_C$ - Arm = D, speed $N_D$ The Willis Equation is: $$\dfrac{N_A - N_D}{N_C - N_D} = -\dfrac{T_C}{T_A}$$ (The negative sign arises from the *internal mesh* between the planet and the ring gear.) Substituting values: $$\dfrac{N_A - N_D}{N_C - N_D} = -\dfrac{80}{20} = -4$$ **(a) (ii) Speed Ratio and Direction — 4 marks** Apply boundary condition: ring fixed, so $N_C = 0$. $$\dfrac{N_A - N_D}{0 - N_D} = -4$$ $$N_A - N_D = 4 N_D$$ $$N_A = 5 N_D$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\dfrac{N_A}{N_D} = 5 \quad \text{(i.e., } N_{sun}/N_{arm} = 5\text{)}}$$ **Direction:** Positive ratio means the sun and arm rotate in the **same direction**.
