As the journal begins to rotate, viscous oil is dragged into a converging wedge between the journal and bush. This creates a **pressurized oil film** that completely separates the two metal surfaces. The pressure distribution follows **Reynolds' equation** and supports the external load $W$ without any metal-to-metal contact. The phenomenon is self-sustaining — the higher the speed, the higher the load it can carry, up to the limit set by the **Sommerfeld number**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between hydrodynamic and hydrostatic bearings.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Hydrodynamic | Hydrostatic |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure source | Self-generated by motion | External pump |
| Can carry load at zero speed | No (fails at start-up) | Yes |
| Starting friction | High (boundary contact) | Near-zero |
| Energy efficiency | Higher at rated speed | Lower (pump runs continuously) |
| Typical application | Engine crankshafts | Heavy machine-tool slides, telescopes |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Define **Sommerfeld number**. Derive the expression for **Sommerfeld number** in terms of viscosity, speed, load, and clearance for a journal bearing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A deep-groove ball bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 35\,\text{kN}$. It carries an equivalent dynamic load $P = 7\,\text{kN}$ at a speed of $1450\,\text{rpm}$. Determine the **L10 life in revolutions, hours, and years of continuous operation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation of Sommerfeld Number:**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** The Sommerfeld number is a **dimensionless number** used in hydrodynamic bearing analysis that relates the bearing operating parameters (load, speed, viscosity) to the geometric parameters (clearance, radius). It uniquely determines the **eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon$ of the journal in the bearing.

> **Derivation (5 Marks):**

Start with the **1-D Reynolds equation** for the unwrapped cylindrical geometry:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U\frac{dh}{dx}$$

With the half-Sommerfeld boundary condition ($p = 0$ at both ends of the positive-pressure region), integrating gives the vertical load:

$$W = \frac{2\mu L r^{3} \omega}{c^{2}}\!\left[\frac{\pi^{2}\varepsilon}{(2+\varepsilon^{2})\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^{2}}} + 4\tan^{-1}\!\frac{\varepsilon}{\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^{2}}}\right]$$

Dividing both sides by projected area $2rL$:

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{\mu r \omega}{c^{2}}\,F(\varepsilon)$$

where $F(\varepsilon)$ is the bracketed function above. Since $\omega = 2\pi N$:

$$P = \frac{2\pi\mu N r}{c^{2}}\,F(\varepsilon)$$

Re-arranging for the dimensionless group:

$$\boxed{S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{F(\varepsilon)}{2\pi}}$$

---

**Part (b) — L10 Life Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Use ball-bearing exponent p = 3:**

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{3}\times 10^{6}\;\text{revolutions}$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{35{,}000}{7{,}000}\right)^{3}\times 10^{6} = (5)^{3}\times 10^{6} = 125\times 10^{6}\;\text{rev}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to hours (N = 1450 rpm = 24.17 rev/s):**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{125\times 10^{6}}{60\times 1450} = \frac{125\times 10^{6}}{87{,}000} \approx 1436.8\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to years (continuous operation, 24×7×365):**

$$L_{10\text{ years}} = \frac{1436.8}{8760} \approx 0.164\,\text{years} \approx 60\,\text{days}$$

> **Interpretation:** The bearing will survive 60 days of **continuous** duty at the stated load before 10% of identical bearings are expected to fail. If the application requires longer life, the designer must either **upgrade the bearing** (higher $C$) or **reduce the equivalent load**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]` — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Reynolds equation** of hydrodynamic lubrication. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **hydrodynamic journal bearing** has the following specifications:
- Diameter $d = 80\,\text{mm}$, Length $L/d = 1.0$
- Radial clearance $c = 0.08\,\text{mm}$
- Speed $N = 1500\,\text{rpm}$
- Viscosity $\mu = 30\,\text{cP}$
- Load per unit projected area $P = 2\,\text{MPa}$

Determine the **eccentricity ratio** using the Sommerfeld number and the **minimum film thickness**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — Reynolds Equation and Assumptions:**

> **Reynolds Equation (3 Marks):** The pressure distribution in a thin lubricant film between two moving surfaces is governed by:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

> **Assumptions (4 Marks):**
> 1. The lubricant obeys **Newton's law of viscosity** (constant $\mu$).
> 2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
> 3. The fluid film is **thin** — curvature of surfaces is neglected.
> 4. **No-slip** at the bearing surfaces.
> 5. **Laminar flow** — Reynolds number of the film $< 2000$.
> 6. **Side leakage** is neglected in 1-D formulation.
> 7. **Inertia forces** of the lubricant are negligible vs. viscous forces.
> 8. The bearing is **isothermal** — viscosity variation in the film is neglected.

---

**Part (b) — Eccentricity and Minimum Film Thickness:**

**Step 1 — Compute inputs.**

$$\mu = 30\,\text{cP} = 0.030\,\text{Pa·s}$$

$$r = 0.040\,\text{m},\quad L = 1.0\times 0.080 = 0.080\,\text{m}$$

$$N = 1500\,\text{rpm} = 25\,\text{rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Sommerfeld number.**

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\frac{\mu N}{P} = \left(\frac{0.040}{0.00008}\right)^{2}\frac{0.030 \times 25}{2\times 10^{6}}$$

$$S = (500)^{2}\times\frac{0.75}{2\times 10^{6}} = 250{,}000 \times 3.75\times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 0.0938}$$

**Step 3 — Look up $\varepsilon$ for finite-width bearing ($L/d = 1$).**

From the standard Sommerfeld chart for $L/d = 1$:

$$S \approx 0.094 \;\Rightarrow\; \varepsilon \approx 0.50$$

**Step 4 — Minimum film thickness.**

$$h_{min} = c(1-\varepsilon) = 0.08 \times (1 - 0.50) = 0.04\,\text{mm} = 40\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Lambda ratio check.**

Assuming combined surface roughness $\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2}+R_{q2}^{2}} = 0.6\,\mu\text{m}$:

$$\Lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2}+R_{q2}^{2}}} = \frac{40}{0.6} \approx 66$$

> **Interpretation:** $\Lambda \gg 3$ → **Full-film hydrodynamic lubrication** is established — safe operation confirmed. However, $\varepsilon = 0.50$ is on the lower side; increasing viscosity would improve load capacity further.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch penalty (–2 marks):** Students frequently mix rpm and rev/s. **Convert N consistently** to either rpm or rev/s throughout the calculation.
> 2. **Wrong exponent (–2 marks):** Many students write $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ but forget that **p = 3 for ball and p = 10/3 for roller**. Writing 10/3 for ball bearing = full mark deduction.
> 3. **Petroff's limitation (–1 mark):** Petroff's equation assumes **concentric ($\varepsilon = 0$)** journal. It is **not valid** for eccentric / loaded bearings — students applying it to a real loaded case will lose marks.
> 4. **Sommerfeld direction (–1 mark):** Students often write the formula as $\frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$ which is correct, but the textbook equivalent $\frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{D}{c}\right)^{2}$ uses **diameter** — the squaring changes! Use **radius** unless the problem gives diameter explicitly.
> 5. **Sommerfeld definition misuse (–2 marks):** Some students compute $S$ then forget to **interpret it** (state $\varepsilon$ and conclude safety). Always close the answer with **design recommendation**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2 — Bearings)**
> - Bearing = mechanical element supporting load + reducing friction in a kinematic pair.
> - Two super-classes: **Sliding (Plain)** vs. **Rolling-Element (Antifriction)**.
> - **Reynolds equation** is the governing PDE of hydrodynamic film pressure.
> - **Petroff's equation** → only for concentric, lightly-loaded journals; friction torque $\propto \mu N$.
> - **Sommerfeld number** $S = (r/c)^{2}\cdot\mu N/P$ — governs eccentricity ratio.
> - **Operating $\varepsilon$ must lie between 0.6 and 0.95** for safe hydrodynamic operation.
> - **L10 life**: ball → exponent 3, roller → exponent 10/3.
> - **Equivalent load** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ for combined loading.
> - **Lubrication regimes**: Boundary, Mixed, Full-film Hydrodynamic, EHL — distinguished by the **Lambda ratio** $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$.
> - **Mechatronic linkage**: bearings govern back-lash, encoder accuracy, and control-loop bandwidth — they are not "passive" components.
> - **Heat removal** capacity $h_c A \cdot \Delta T \ge P_{friction}$ — non-negotiable design constraint.
> - **Failure modes**: fatigue spalling (rolling), scoring/whitening (plain), false brinelling (vibration-induced), electrical-erosion pitting (VFD-driven motors).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical and Solid-State Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical and Solid-State Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mechanical Relays (Electromechanical Relays — EMR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Mechanical Relay** (also called an **Electromechanical Relay, EMR**) is an electrically controllable, galvanically isolated, binary switching element that uses a magnetic field generated by a current-carrying coil to mechanically move one or more sets of metallic contacts between their Normally Open (NO) and Normally Closed (NC) positions, thereby opening or closing a separate electrical circuit.

In simple, plain-English intuition, think of a mechanical relay as a **"remote-controlled light switch"**. Imagine a small switch on your wall that turns on your porch light. Now, instead of your finger flipping the switch, a tiny electromagnet (a coil of wire) does the flipping for you. The moment you send a small control current through this coil, it becomes a temporary magnet, yanks a metal lever across, and "clicks" — the switch contacts touch (or separate), and a completely separate, often much higher-power circuit (like a motor or heater) gets switched on or off. The "click" you hear in old washing machines, car horns, and industrial starters is the unmistakable signature of this mechanical action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Mechanical Relays are a foundational topic in Module 3 (Actuators and Switching Devices). Students are expected to identify the construction, working principle, NO/NC contact logic, ratings, and the principal electromechanical time-delays (**operate time, release time, bounce time**).

**Core Functional Components of a Mechanical Relay:**
- **Coil (Solenoid):** The wound copper wire that creates a magnetic flux when energized.
- **Core / Yoke:** The ferromagnetic path that concentrates the magnetic flux.
- **Armature:** The movable ferromagnetic lever attracted by the magnetic field.
- **Spring (Return Spring):** Restores the armature to its rest position when the coil is de-energized.
- **Contacts:** The actual switching terminals — **Common (COM)**, **Normally Open (NO)**, and **Normally Closed (NC)**.
- **Contact Springs / Terminals:** Mechanical supports and external wiring points.

**Standard Relay Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU):**
- **SPST (Single Pole Single Throw)** — 1 input, 1 output contact.
- **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** — 1 input, switches between 2 outputs.
- **DPST (Double Pole Single Throw)** — 2 simultaneous inputs, each with 1 output.
- **DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw)** — 2 simultaneous inputs, each switching between 2 outputs.

---

### 1.2 Solid-State Relays (SSR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** is a non-moving, fully electronic, semiconductor-based switching device that uses the controlled conduction of a power semiconductor switch (TRIAC, SCR, MOSFET, or IGBT) — coupled to the low-power control input through an opto-isolator (or other galvanic isolation barrier) — to switch a load circuit ON or OFF without any mechanical motion, contact arcing, or audible click.

In simple, plain-English intuition, think of an SSR as a **"silent, invisible light switch"**. Instead of metal contacts physically slapping together, the SSR uses a tiny LED inside that, when lit, tells a powerful electronic valve (a TRIAC, for example) to "open the floodgates" and let mains current flow. There is no moving part, no sound, no spark — just a smooth, silent electronic handshake between a 5 V logic signal and a 230 V AC motor. They are the silent workhorses inside PLCs, modern HVAC systems, and medical equipment where reliability and silence are critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Solid-State Relays are the modern counterpart to EMRs in Mechatronics. The examiner expects knowledge of: (1) the internal block structure (Input → Opto-isolator → Trigger Circuit → Power Semiconductor), (2) the **Zero-Cross vs. Random Turn-On** distinction for AC SSRs, and (3) the unavoidable on-state **voltage drop / heat dissipation** problem and its **heat-sink sizing**.

**Core Functional Blocks of a Solid-State Relay:**
- **Input Circuit (Control Side):** Receives the low-voltage DC control signal (typically 3–32 V DC).
- **Opto-Isolator (Galvanic Isolation Barrier):** An LED + phototransistor/TRIAC pair providing several **kV** of dielectric isolation between input and output.
- **Zero-Cross Detection / Trigger Circuit:** For AC SSRs, decides whether the TRIAC fires exactly at the 0 V crossing of the AC sine wave (Zero-Cross type) or at any phase angle (Random Turn-On / Phase-Control type).
- **Output Power Switch:** A **TRIAC** (for AC SSRs) or a **Power MOSFET / IGBT** (for DC SSRs).
- **Snubber / Heat-Sink Interface:** RC snubbers for dv/dt protection, and a metallic baseplate for thermal dissipation.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize in BOLD:**
> - **Coil Pickup Voltage** ≈ **80% of nominal coil voltage**.
> - **Coil Dropout Voltage** ≈ **10% of nominal coil voltage**.
> - **On-state voltage drop across a TRIAC-based AC SSR** ≈ **1.5 V to 2.0 V (rms)**.
> - **On-state voltage drop across a MOSFET-based DC SSR** ≈ **0.05 V to 0.5 V**.
> - **Typical opto-isolation voltage** between input and output: **4 kV to 5 kV (rms)**.
> - **Maximum junction temperature (T\_j,max)** of internal semiconductor: **125 °C**.

---

### 1.3 Why Do Mechatronics Engineers Care About Both?

Both relays perform the **same logical function** — they isolate a low-power control signal from a high-power load. The choice between them is a classic **engineering trade-off**:
- **Mechanical Relays** are cheap, have **near-zero voltage drop** when ON, and can switch **both AC and DC** with a single device — but they are slow, wear out, and produce **EMI / contact bounce**.
- **Solid-State Relays** are fast, silent, and last billions of cycles — but they **drop a small voltage**, generate **heat**, leak a tiny current when OFF, and can only switch **AC or DC** (not both) in a given device.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Relay — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The operation of a mechanical relay follows a deterministic electromechanical sequence. Below is the structured logic that the KTU examiner expects in a 7- or 14-mark answer.

**Step 1 — Resting (De-energized) State:**
When no current flows in the control coil, the return spring holds the armature against the **NC contact**. Current flowing in the load circuit passes through the **COM-NC** path.

**Step 2 — Energization:**
A control voltage $V_{coil}$ is applied to the coil. Coil current $I_{coil}$ flows, generating a magnetomotive force (MMF):
$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I_{coil} \;\; [\text{At (Ampere-turns)}]$$

**Step 3 — Magnetic Attraction:**
The MMF drives flux $\Phi$ through the ferromagnetic core and yoke:
$$\Phi = \frac{\text{MMF}}{\mathcal{R}} \;\; [\text{Wb}]$$
where $\mathcal{R}$ is the reluctance of the magnetic circuit.

**Step 4 — Armature Motion:**
When the magnetic force $F_m$ on the armature exceeds the spring force $F_s$ and the contact-friction force, the armature snaps over.

**Step 5 — Contact Transfer:**
The COM terminal disconnects from NC and connects to NO. This is the moment of the audible **"click"**.

**Step 6 — Steady State (Energized):**
Current in the load circuit now flows through the **COM-NO** path.

**Step 7 — De-energization:**
When $V_{coil}$ is removed, the magnetic field collapses, the spring restores the armature, and the contacts return to NC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Mechatronics:** The electromechanical delays (operate time, release time, bounce time) limit the **maximum switching frequency** of EMRs to roughly **300–500 operations/minute**, far below the tens of kHz of SSRs.

---

### 2.2 Solid-State Relay — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Control Signal Applied:**
A low-power DC control voltage (typically 3–32 V) is applied to the input terminals, energizing the input LED of the opto-isolator.

**Step 2 — Optical Coupling:**
The LED emits infrared photons that fall on a phototransistor, phototriac, or photovoltaic stack, generating a small base/gate drive signal on the secondary (output) side — **with several kV of galvanic isolation** preserved.

**Step 3 — Trigger / Zero-Cross Detection:**
- For **Zero-Cross AC SSRs**: A detector circuit waits until the AC mains voltage crosses through **0 V**, then fires the internal TRIAC. This eliminates inrush currents and electrical noise.
- For **Random Turn-On AC SSRs**: The TRIAC is fired immediately upon receiving the gate signal, anywhere on the sine wave — used for **phase-angle control** (dimming, heater regulation).

**Step 4 — Power Conduction:**
The TRIAC (or MOSFET/IGBT for DC) enters its ON state and conducts the load current with a small forward voltage drop.

**Step 5 — Turn-Off:**
When the control input is removed, the TRIAC naturally commutates off at the next **zero-current crossing** of the AC wave (for AC SSRs). DC SSRs require the load current to fall below the device's **holding current** or the supply to be actively interrupted.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and represent the core quantitative content KTU expects for relay-related numerical problems.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coil current | $I_{coil} = V_{coil} \div R_{coil}$ | A | Ohm's law on the relay coil |
| 2 | Coil power dissipation | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil} = V_{coil}^{2} \div R_{coil}$ | W | Heat generated in the coil winding |
| 3 | Magnetomotive force (MMF) | $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I_{coil}$ | At (Ampere-turns) | $N$ = number of coil turns |
| 4 | Magnetic flux | $\Phi = \text{MMF} \div \mathcal{R}$ | Wb | $\mathcal{R}$ = reluctance of magnetic path |
| 5 | SSR on-state power dissipation | $P_{d} = V_{drop(rms)} \cdot I_{load}$ | W | $V_{drop}$ ≈ **1.5–2.0 V** for TRIAC |
| 6 | Heat-sink thermal resistance | $R_{\theta SA} = \dfrac{T_{j,\max} - T_{A}}{P_{d}} - R_{\theta JC} - R_{\theta CS}$ | °C/W | **Most-favoured KTU numerical** |
| 7 | Opto-isolator Current Transfer Ratio | $\text{CTR} = (I_{C} \div I_{F}) \times 100\,\%$ | % | $I_F$ = LED forward current |
| 8 | Contact voltage drop (EMR) | $V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact}$ | V | $R_{contact}$ typically **< 50 mΩ** |
| 9 | Snubber RC time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | s | Protects TRIAC from dv/dt false triggering |
| 10 | Max switching freq. (EMR) | $f_{\max} \approx 1 \div (t_{op} + t_{rel})$ | Hz | Limited by mechanical inertia |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, all absolute values and divisions are written using `÷` or `\div` — **never** the vertical pipe `|`. This prevents the markdown table from breaking.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Typical Relay Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Car starter motor switching | Mechanical (high-current) | Handles 200+ A inrush; SSR cannot match economically |
| PLC output modules | SSR (DC) | Silent, fast, no contact bounce for digital logic |
| Industrial furnace control (AC heater) | SSR (AC, zero-cross) | Zero-cross eliminates EMI; millions of cycles |
| Medical infusion pump | SSR | Silent, no spark = safe in oxygen-rich environments |
| Home appliance control board | Mechanical (SPDT) | Lowest cost, sufficient for 100k-cycle life |
| Solar PV inverter output switching | SSR / Contactor hybrid | Combines zero-leak SSR with mechanical safety disconnect |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coil Current and Power in a Mechanical Relay

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A mechanical relay has a coil rated **12 V DC** and a coil resistance of **480 Ω**. Calculate (a) the steady-state coil current, and (b) the power dissipated in the coil.

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities and restate them clearly.**

The nominal control voltage is $V_{coil} = 12\;\text{V}$, and the coil resistance is $R_{coil} = 480\;\Omega$. The coil is a purely resistive winding (the inductive time constant $\tau = L/R$ affects only the *transient* turn-on, not the steady-state current).

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to the coil circuit.**

The coil behaves as a simple resistor in DC steady state:
$$I_{coil} = \frac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$I_{coil} = \frac{12\;\text{V}}{480\;\Omega} = 0.025\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to milliamperes for clarity.**

$$I_{coil} = 0.025\;\text{A} = 25\;\text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the power dissipation in the coil.**

The power converted to heat in the coil winding is:
$$P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$P_{coil} = 12\;\text{V} \times 0.025\;\text{A} = 0.30\;\text{W}$$

**Step 7 — Cross-verify using $P = I^{2}R$.**

$$P_{coil} = (0.025)^{2} \times 480 = 0.000625 \times 480 = 0.30\;\text{W} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(a)** $I_{coil} = 25\;\text{mA}$
- **(b)** $P_{coil} = 0.30\;\text{W}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat-Sink Sizing for a Solid-State Relay (Classic KTU 14-Mark Numerical)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A TRIAC-based AC SSR switches a **5 A (rms)** load. The on-state voltage drop is **$V_{drop} = 1.8\;\text{V}$ (rms)**. The SSR is mounted on a heat sink. Given:
- Maximum junction temperature: $T_{j,\max} = 125\;^\circ\text{C}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_{A} = 40\;^\circ\text{C}$
- Junction-to-case thermal resistance: $R_{\theta JC} = 1.5\;^\circ\text{C/W}$
- Case-to-sink thermal resistance: $R_{\theta CS} = 0.3\;^\circ\text{C/W}$ (with thermal compound)

**Calculate the maximum allowable sink-to-ambient thermal resistance $R_{\theta SA}$ to ensure safe operation.**

**Step 1 — Compute the steady-state power dissipated in the SSR.**

The on-state power dissipation is the product of the rms voltage drop and the rms load current:
$$P_{d} = V_{drop} \cdot I_{load}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$P_{d} = 1.8\;\text{V} \times 5\;\text{A} = 9.0\;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — State the thermal-resistance network equation.**

The total thermal resistance from junction to ambient is the sum of three series resistances:
$$R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$$

**Step 4 — Express the constraint that the junction must not exceed its maximum temperature.**

$$T_{j,\max} = T_{A} + P_{d} \cdot R_{\theta JA}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the total thermal resistance $R_{\theta JA}$.**

$$R_{\theta JA} = \frac{T_{j,\max} - T_{A}}{P_{d}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$R_{\theta JA} = \frac{125 - 40}{9.0} = \frac{85}{9.0} = 9.444\;^\circ\text{C/W}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $R_{\theta SA}$ by subtracting the known series resistances.**

$$R_{\theta SA} = R_{\theta JA} - R_{\theta JC} - R_{\theta CS}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$R_{\theta SA} = 9.444 - 1.5 - 0.3 = 7.644\;^\circ\text{C/W}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result and select a standard heat sink.**

The heat sink must have a thermal resistance **less than $7.6\;^\circ\text{C/W}$** at the specified airflow. The designer should choose a standard extruded-aluminium heat sink with $R_{\theta SA} \leq 6\;^\circ\text{C/W}$ to provide a 25 % safety margin.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{R_{\theta SA} \leq 7.64\;^\circ\text{C/W}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EMR vs SSR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question** ("Compare Mechanical Relays and Solid-State Relays"). Memorize the bolded values and qualitative trends.

| Parameter | Mechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching element** | Moving metallic contacts | Semiconductor (TRIAC / SCR / MOSFET / IGBT) |
| **Switching speed (operate time)** | **5 – 30 ms** | **0.05 – 1 ms** |
| **Bounce time** | **0.5 – 5 ms** (present) | **0 ms** (none — pure electronic) |
| **Electrical lifetime** | $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ operations | $10^{9}$ – $10^{12}$ operations |
| **On-state voltage drop** | **< 0.1 V** (negligible) | **1.5 – 2.0 V (AC) / 0.05 – 0.5 V (DC)** |
| **Off-state leakage current** | **0 mA** (true open circuit) | **1 – 10 mA** (TRIAC snubber leakage) |
| **Galvanic isolation** | Inherent (air gap) | Via opto-isolator (**4 kV** typical) |
| **EMI / RFI generation** | High (contact arcing) | Very low (zero-cross) to moderate (random turn-on) |
| **Audible noise** | **Yes — audible "click"** | **No — silent** |
| **Mounting orientation** | Must be horizontal for reliable contact | Any orientation |
| **Shock / vibration tolerance** | Poor (contacts may bounce open) | Excellent (no moving parts) |
| **Cost per ampere switched** | Low (especially < 5 A) | Higher |
| **Can switch AC and DC with one device?** | **Yes** | **No** — dedicated AC or DC SSR |
| **Heat dissipation** | Negligible (low $V_{drop}$) | **Significant** — requires heat sink |
| **Position in explosive atmospheres** | Unsafe (arcing risk) | Safe (no arcing) |
| **Failure mode** | Contacts weld or fail open | Semiconductor short-circuit (fail-ON) |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Minimum LED Drive Current for an SSR Opto-Isolator

**Problem Statement:**
An SSR's opto-isolator has a **Current Transfer Ratio (CTR) of 50 %** at the operating point. The TRIAC trigger circuit requires a minimum collector/gate current of **$I_{C,\min} = 10\;\text{mA}$** to fire reliably. Find the **minimum LED forward current $I_{F,\min}$** required.

**Step 1 — Write the CTR definition.**

$$\text{CTR} = \frac{I_{C}}{I_{F}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $I_{F,\min}$.**

$$I_{F,\min} = \frac{I_{C,\min}}{\text{CTR} / 100}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$I_{F,\min} = \frac{10\;\text{mA}}{0.50} = 20\;\text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Engineer a practical driver.**
In practice, the designer chooses $I_F = 1.5 \times I_{F,\min} = 30\;\text{mA}$ to allow for **CTR degradation over temperature and aging**.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{I_{F,\min} = 20\;\text{mA} \quad (\text{design for } 30\;\text{mA})}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanical Relay (EMR)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** within an Electromechanical Relay, treating it as an energy-conversion and signal-transmission chain. (Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed; no reserved keywords are used as node IDs; labels contain only clean uppercase text.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    mrCtrl[CONTROL VOLTAGE SOURCE V_COIL] --> mrResistor[COIL RESISTANCE R_COIL]
    mrResistor --> mrCoil[COIL WINDING N_TURNS]
    mrCoil --> mrMMF[GENERATES MMF N TIMES I_COIL]
    mrMMF --> mrFlux[MAGNETIC FLUX PHI THROUGH YOKE]
    mrFlux --> mrArmature[MAGNETIC FORCE F_M ON ARMATURE]
    mrArmature -->|F_M EXCEEDS SPRING FORCE| mrMotion[ARMATURE ROTATES]
    mrMotion --> mrContact[COMMON CONTACT TRANSFER NO TO NC]
    mrContact --> mrLoad[LOAD CIRCUIT GETS SWITCHED]
    mrContact -.->|RELEASE WHEN DE_ENERGIZED| mrSpring[RETURN SPRING PULLS BACK]
    mrSpring --> mrRest[ARMATURE RESTS AT NC]
    style mrCtrl fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style mrLoad fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

**Architectural Reading:**
The flow shows the **causal chain** — Control Voltage → Coil Current → MMF → Flux → Mechanical Force → Armature Motion → Contact Transfer → Load Switching. The dotted line represents the **passive restoration** path (spring returns the armature when de-energized).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solid-State Relay (SSR)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ssrIn[INPUT CONTROL SIGNAL 3 TO 32 V DC] --> ssrLED[INPUT LED INSIDE OPTO_ISOLATOR]
    ssrLED -->|INFRARED PHOTONS| ssrPhoto[PHOTOTRIAC OR PHOTOVOLTAIC STACK]
    ssrPhoto -->|GALVANIC ISOLATION 4 KV| ssrTrigger[TRIGGER AND ZERO_CROSS CIRCUIT]
    ssrTrigger -->|GATE PULSE| ssrSwitch[POWER SEMICONDUCTOR TRIAC MOSFET OR IGBT]
    ssrSwitch --> ssrLoad[LOAD CIRCUIT SWITCHED]
    ssrSwitch -.->|HEAT P_D = V_DROP TIMES I_LOAD| ssrHeatsink[EXTERNAL HEAT SINK REQUIRED]
    ssrHeatsink --> ssrAmbient[AMBIENT AIR T_A]
    style ssrIn fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style ssrLoad fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style ssrHeatsink fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
```

**Architectural Reading:**
The flow illustrates the **all-electronic signal chain**. Note the **dashed heat-dissipation path** — this is the unique design constraint of SSRs: every watt of on-state power must be rejected to the ambient through a properly sized heat sink. The opto-isolator provides the **dielectric barrier** that protects the low-voltage control circuit from the high-voltage load side.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing Between EMR and SSR

```mermaid
graph TD
    dfStart[START CHOOSE A RELAY] --> dfQ1{SWITCHING FREQUENCY GREATER THAN 10 PER SECOND}
    dfQ1 -->|YES| dfSSR1[CHOOSE SSR]
    dfQ1 -->|NO| dfQ2{LOAD IS EXPLOSIVE OR NOISY ENVIRONMENT}
    dfQ2 -->|YES| dfSSR2[CHOOSE SSR ZERO_CROSS]
    dfQ2 -->|NO| dfQ3{LOAD CURRENT GREATER THAN 50 A}
    dfQ3 -->|YES| dfEMR1[CHOOSE EMR OR CONTACTOR]
    dfQ3 -->|NO| dfQ4{BUDGET IS TIGHT AND CYCLES BELOW 100K}
    dfQ4 -->|YES| dfEMR2[CHOOSE EMR LOWER COST]
    dfQ4 -->|NO| dfSSR3[CHOOSE SSR LONGER LIFE]
    style dfStart fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style dfSSR1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style dfSSR2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style dfSSR3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style dfEMR1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style dfEMR2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

**Architectural Reading:**
This is a **binary-decision design aid** that mirrors the typical engineering selection process. SSRs win on **frequency, lifetime, and silence**; EMRs win on **cost, current-handling, and true galvanic isolation**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Type Pattern]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Differentiate between a Mechanical Relay and a Solid-State Relay in terms of switching speed and electrical lifetime.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Switching speed:** A Mechanical Relay has an operate time of **5 – 30 ms** due to armature inertia, whereas a Solid-State Relay switches in **0.05 – 1 ms** because the transition is purely electronic.
- **Electrical lifetime:** A Mechanical Relay is limited to **$10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ operations** by contact wear and arcing, while a Solid-State Relay achieves **$10^{9}$ – $10^{12}$ operations** because there are no mechanical contacts to wear out.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *What is the function of an opto-isolator in a Solid-State Relay?*

**Model Answer:**
The opto-isolator provides **galvanic (dielectric) isolation** between the low-power DC control input and the high-power AC/DC load output. It uses an infrared LED on the input side that illuminates a phototransistor or phototriac on the output side. This allows a signal to cross the isolation barrier **without any electrical conductor**, providing typically **4 kV** of isolation and protecting the control circuit from voltage transients on the load side.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice Format]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Module 3 - Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Solid-State Relay (SSR). Compare its features with those of a conventional mechanical relay.*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** (Block-Level Functional Architecture — SSR).

**Construction — Functional Blocks:**

1. **Input Circuit:** Accepts a low-voltage DC control signal (typically 3 – 32 V DC). It contains a current-limiting resistor in series with the input LED.
2. **Opto-Isolator (Galvanic Isolation):** An LED-phototriac pair that provides up to 4 kV isolation between input and output. This is the **safety barrier** of the SSR.
3. **Zero-Cross Detection Circuit (for AC SSRs):** Senses the AC mains voltage and ensures the internal TRIAC is fired only when the instantaneous voltage is near **0 V**, eliminating inrush currents and EMI.
4. **Trigger / Gate Drive Circuit:** Converts the phototriac's output into a properly timed gate pulse for the power semiconductor.
5. **Output Power Switch:** A **TRIAC** (for AC) or a **MOSFET / IGBT** (for DC) that conducts the load current.
6. **Snubber Network:** An RC network across the TRIAC to limit **dv/dt** and prevent false triggering.
7. **Heat-Sink Baseplate:** Metallic mounting surface to conduct the on-state heat ($P_d = V_{drop} \times I_{load}$) to an external heat sink.

**Working Principle:**
When the control voltage is applied, the input LED emits IR light, which triggers the phototriac. For zero-cross SSRs, the gate pulse is **delayed** until the next zero-voltage crossing of the AC mains, at which point the main TRIAC is fired and conducts load current. Removing the control input turns the LED off, and the TRIAC naturally commutates off at the next zero-current crossing.

**Comparison with Mechanical Relay (Key Points):**

| Feature | Mechanical Relay | Solid-State Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Switching speed | 5 – 30 ms (slow) | 0.05 – 1 ms (fast) |
| Lifetime | $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ ops | $10^{9}$ – $10^{12}$ ops |
| On-state voltage drop | < 0.1 V | 1.5 – 2.0 V (TRIAC) |
| Contact bounce | Yes | None |
| Acoustic noise | Audible "click" | Silent |
| Heat sink | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| Position sensitivity | Sensitive (gravity-aided) | Insensitive |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A TRIAC-based AC SSR controls a 4 A (rms) resistive load. The on-state voltage drop is 1.6 V (rms) and the following thermal data is given:*
- *Maximum junction temperature: $T_{j,\max} = 125^\circ$C*
- *Ambient temperature: $T_A = 35^\circ$C*
- *Junction-to-case thermal resistance: $R_{\theta JC} = 2.0^\circ$C/W*
- *Case-to-sink thermal resistance: $R_{\theta CS} = 0.4^\circ$C/W (with thermal paste)*

*Calculate (i) the power dissipated in the SSR, and (ii) the maximum allowable sink-to-ambient thermal resistance $R_{\theta SA}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Dissipated in the SSR:**

$$P_d = V_{drop} \times I_{load}$$
$$P_d = 1.6\;\text{V} \times 4\;\text{A} = 6.4\;\text{W}$$

**(ii) Maximum Allowable $R_{\theta SA}$:**

Total thermal resistance from junction to ambient:
$$R_{\theta JA} = \frac{T_{j,\max} - T_A}{P_d}$$
$$R_{\theta JA} = \frac{125 - 35}{6.4} = \frac{90}{6.4} = 14.0625\;^\circ\text{C/W}$$

Sink-to-ambient thermal resistance:
$$R_{\theta SA} = R_{\theta JA} - R_{\theta JC} - R_{\theta CS}$$
$$R_{\theta SA} = 14.0625 - 2.0 - 0.4 = 11.6625\;^\circ\text{C/W}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_d = 6.4\;\text{W}$ and $R_{\theta SA} \leq 11.66\;^\circ\text{C/W}$ (select a standard heat sink with $R_{\theta SA} \leq 9\;^\circ\text{C/W}$ for a safety margin).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Module 3 - Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the construction and working of a Mechanical Relay (Electromagnetic Relay) with a neat block diagram. List any four types based on contact configuration.*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** (Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanical Relay).

**Construction (Key Components):**

1. **Coil (Solenoid):** Insulated copper wire wound on a bobbin. Energized by the control voltage $V_{coil}$.
2. **Core / Yoke:** Soft-iron ferromagnetic path that concentrates the magnetic flux.
3. **Armature:** Movable iron lever attracted by the magnetic field when the coil is energized.
4. **Spring:** Provides the restoring force when the coil is de-energized.
5. **Contacts:** Three terminals — **Common (COM)**, **Normally Open (NO)**, and **Normally Closed (NC)**.
6. **Terminals / Pins:** External electrical connections for coil and contacts.

**Working Principle:**
When control voltage $V_{coil}$ is applied to the coil, current flows and produces an MMF, $N \cdot I_{coil}$, which drives flux $\Phi$ through the yoke. The magnetic force attracts the armature towards the yoke, overcoming the spring force. The armature physically moves the common contact from NC to NO, switching the load circuit ON. When $V_{coil}$ is removed, the spring restores the armature, and the contacts return to NC.

**Types Based on Contact Configuration:**
1. **SPST** — Single Pole Single Throw
2. **SPDT** — Single Pole Double Throw
3. **DPST** — Double Pole Single Throw
4. **DPDT** — Double Pole Double Throw

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 12 V mechanical relay has a coil resistance of 360 Ω. Calculate (i) the steady-state coil current, (ii) the power dissipated in the coil, and (iii) the magnetomotive force (MMF) if the coil has 4000 turns.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Steady-State Coil Current:**

By Ohm's law:
$$I_{coil} = \frac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{12}{360} = 0.0333\;\text{A} = 33.33\;\text{mA}$$

**(ii) Power Dissipated in the Coil:**

$$P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil} = 12 \times 0.0333 = 0.40\;\text{W}$$

Cross-check using $P = I^2 R$:
$$P_{coil} = (0.0333)^2 \times 360 = 0.001111 \times 360 \approx 0.40\;\text{W}\;\;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Magnetomotive Force (MMF):**

$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I_{coil} = 4000 \times 0.0333 = 133.33\;\text{At}$$

**Final Answers:** $I_{coil} = 33.33\;\text{mA}$, $P_{coil} = 0.40\;\text{W}$, $\text{MMF} = 133.33\;\text{At}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on MMF (must write **At — Ampere-turns**, not just "A" or "N").
> 2. **Mixing up the thermal-resistance network**: Students often write $R_{\theta SA} = (T_{j,\max} - T_A) / P_d$ and stop, forgetting to **subtract** $R_{\theta JC}$ and $R_{\theta CS}$.
> 3. **Confusing AC and DC SSRs**: A TRIAC-based SSR can only switch AC (commutates at zero current); a MOSFET-based SSR is required for DC. Writing "SSR switches both AC and DC" is a **direct loss of 2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting the heat-sink requirement** in SSR numericals — even if your $R_{\theta SA}$ calculation is correct, you lose 1 mark if you do not mention "the calculated heat sink is necessary for safe operation".
> 5. **Stating "relays provide isolation" without specifying "galvanic / dielectric" isolation** — use the precise KTU terminology.
> 6. **Confusing "Normally Open" and "Normally Closed"** — NO is **open when coil is de-energized**, NC is **closed when coil is de-energized**. This is the #1 conceptual trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- A **Mechanical Relay** is an **electromagnetic, galvanically isolated, mechanically actuated** switch with NO/NC/COM contacts.
- A **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** is a **fully electronic, semiconductor-based** switch with **opto-isolated** control.
- **EMR switching speed:** 5 – 30 ms | **SSR switching speed:** 0.05 – 1 ms.
- **EMR lifetime:** $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ ops | **SSR lifetime:** $10^{9}$ – $10^{12}$ ops.
- **EMR on-state drop:** < 0.1 V (negligible) | **SSR TRIAC drop:** **1.5 – 2.0 V (rms)**.
- **EMR has contact bounce**, **SSR has no bounce** — critical for digital logic interfacing.
- **EMR is silent → NO, EMR is loud → YES (audible click)**.
- **SSR generates heat:** $P_d = V_{drop} \times I_{load}$ — **heat-sink is mandatory**.
- **Heat-sink sizing formula:** $R_{\theta SA} = (T_{j,\max} - T_A)/P_d - R_{\theta JC} - R_{\theta CS}$.
- **Standard $T_{j,\max}$** for SSR internal semiconductor = **125 °C**.
- **Opto-isolator isolation voltage** = **4 kV typical**.
- **AC SSR** uses a **TRIAC** and supports **Zero-Cross** (no EMI) or **Random Turn-On** (phase control).
- **DC SSR** uses a **Power MOSFET or IGBT** and is **polarity-sensitive**.
- **MMF equation:** $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I_{coil}$ [At].
- **Ohm's law on coil:** $I_{coil} = V_{coil} / R_{coil}$.
- **CTR of opto-isolator:** $\text{CTR} = (I_C / I_F) \times 100\,\%$.
- **Coil pickup voltage** ≈ 80 % of nominal; **dropout voltage** ≈ 10 % of nominal.
- **EMR types:** SPST, SPDT, DPST, DPDT — memorize the abbreviations.
- **Failure mode:** EMR fails **open** (safe for motor circuits); SSR fails **short** (potentially hazardous — must fuse).
- **EMR is best for high inrush currents** (motors, solenoids); **SSR is best for high cycle rates** (PWM heating, fast valves).
- **Always remember the three thermal resistances:** $R_{\theta JC}$, $R_{\theta CS}$, $R_{\theta SA}$ — they add in series from junction to ambient.
- **KTU's favourite numerical** is the **heat-sink sizing problem** — practice it 3 times.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solenoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solenoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solenoid (KTU Mechatronics Definition):** A solenoid is a linear electromechanical actuator that converts electrical energy (current through a coil) into controlled linear mechanical motion (displacement of a ferromagnetic plunger or armature) by means of an electromagnetically generated magnetic field. It is classified as an *output actuator* in a mechatronic system and serves as the interface between the electronic control domain and the mechanical load domain.

In mechatronic system architecture, the solenoid is positioned in the **"power domain"** — taking a low-power electrical command signal and producing a high-force mechanical output suitable for actuating valves, latches, clutches, brakes, fuel injectors, pinball flippers, door locks, and hydraulic/pneumatic spool valves.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Magnetic Muscle"**
> Think of a solenoid as an **artificial muscle** that flexes when you "zap" it with electricity. Imagine wrapping copper wire around a nail 500 times. The moment you connect a battery, the nail suddenly becomes a strong magnet and yanks a piece of iron toward it. A solenoid is essentially this exact idea, but engineered with precise geometry, a guided movable plunger, a return spring, and engineered housing to produce **predictable, repeatable linear strokes** every time you energize the coil.

The two "moods" of a solenoid are:
- **Pull-type (Linear Pull Solenoid):** Plunger is sucked *into* the coil when energized.
- **Push-type (Linear Push Solenoid):** Plunger is pushed *out* from the coil (achieved via an internal conical plunger or by mechanical linkage).

### 1.3 Salient Physical Quantities (Constants & Standards)

- **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m} \approx \mathbf{1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \ H/m}$
- **Relative permeability of ferromagnetic plunger:** $\mu_r \approx \mathbf{4000 - 6000}$ (typical low-carbon steel)
- **Standard operating voltage:** $\mathbf{12 \ V \ DC}$, $\mathbf{24 \ V \ DC}$, $\mathbf{110/220 \ V \ AC}$ (50/60 Hz)
- **Duty cycle classifications:** **Continuous**, **Intermittent**, **Pulsed**
- **Stroke range:** $\mathbf{2 \ mm \ to \ 50 \ mm}$ (industrial) and up to $\mathbf{100 \ mm}$ (heavy-duty)
- **Force range:** $\mathbf{0.1 \ N \ to \ 1000 \ N}$ (typical product line)

### 1.4 Block-Level Context in a Mechatronic System

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical closed-loop mechatronic chain, the solenoid sits **downstream of the microcontroller/MOSFET driver** and **upstream of the mechanical load**. The command signal is a current pulse; the output is a linear force-stroke characteristic. It is *not* a continuous servo device — it is fundamentally a **two-state (ON/OFF) or PWM-modulated actuator**, making it ideal for digital logic interfacing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

A solenoid operates on **Ampère's Circuital Law** and the principle of **magnetic circuit reluctance minimization**. When current $I$ flows through a coil of $N$ turns, a magnetomotive force (MMF) is established. The ferromagnetic plunger offers a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux, and the system physically rearranges itself to **minimize the total magnetic reluctance** — which manifests as a mechanical pulling force on the plunger.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Energization:** The driver circuit (e.g., a MOSFET or BJT) completes the coil circuit; current $I$ begins to ramp up.
2. **MMF Generation:** The coil produces a magnetomotive force $\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I$ in ampere-turns (At).
3. **Flux Establishment:** Flux $\Phi$ flows through the iron core, the air gap, and back through the return path. $\Phi = \mathcal{F} / \mathcal{R}$, where $\mathcal{R}$ is total reluctance.
4. **Force Production:** The flux crossing the air gap produces a mechanical attraction force on the plunger pulling it toward the closed position. This force is given by $F = \Phi^2 / (2 \mu_0 A)$ for a uniform air gap of area $A$.
5. **Mechanical Stroke:** The plunger moves by a distance $x$ (the **stroke length**), reducing the air gap $g$.
6. **Spring Return:** Upon de-energization, an internal return spring pushes the plunger back to its rest position.
7. **Flux Collapse & Inductive Kick:** As the current is switched off, the collapsing flux induces a high back-EMF (often hundreds of volts). A **flyback diode** is mandatory across the coil in DC circuits to protect the driver transistor.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Governing Equation | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Note |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|--------|------|------------------|
| 1 | Magnetomotive Force (MMF) | $\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I$ | $\mathcal{F}$ | Ampere-turn (At) | Direct product of turns and current |
| 2 | Magnetic Field Intensity | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{l_c}$ | $H$ | A/m | $l_c$ = mean magnetic path length |
| 3 | Magnetic Flux Density | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | $B$ | Tesla (T) | In air gap, $\mu_r \approx 1$ |
| 4 | Magnetic Flux | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) | $A$ = cross-section area |
| 5 | Reluctance of air gap | $\mathcal{R}_g = \dfrac{g}{\mu_0 A}$ | $\mathcal{R}$ | A-t/Wb (or H⁻¹) | Dominant term in magnetic circuit |
| 6 | Reluctance of iron path | $\mathcal{R}_i = \dfrac{l_i}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | $\mathcal{R}_i$ | A-t/Wb | Often neglected for $\mu_r \gg 1$ |
| 7 | Total Reluctance (series) | $\mathcal{R}_{tot} = \mathcal{R}_i + \mathcal{R}_g$ | $\mathcal{R}_{tot}$ | A-t/Wb | Air gap dominates |
| 8 | Flux in the magnetic circuit | $\Phi = \dfrac{N I}{\mathcal{R}_{tot}}$ | $\Phi$ | Wb | Analogous to Ohm's law in magnetics |
| 9 | Solenoid Pull Force (general) | $F = \dfrac{\Phi^2}{2 \mu_0 A} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | $F$ | Newton (N) | Maxwell stress tensor derivation |
| 10 | Solenoid Force (gap-based) | $F = \dfrac{(N I)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$ | $F$ | N | Valid when $\mathcal{R}_g \gg \mathcal{R}_i$ |
| 11 | Energy stored in air gap | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathcal{R}_g \Phi^2$ | $W$ | Joule (J) | Source of mechanical work |
| 12 | Coil Inductance | $L = \dfrac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}_{tot}}$ | $L$ | Henry (H) | Varies with plunger position |
| 13 | Coil Resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho \ell_w}{A_w}$ | $R_c$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $\rho$ = copper resistivity ($\mathbf{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \ \Omega \cdot m}$) |
| 14 | Time constant (electrical) | $\tau = L / R_c$ | $\tau$ | second (s) | Determines pull-in delay |
| 15 | Inductive kick voltage | $V_L = -L \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | $V_L$ | Volt (V) | Why flyback diodes are required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRAP:** The pull force $F$ is **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap** $g^2$. This is why solenoid force is *highest at the beginning of the stroke* (large $g$) — but wait, this contradicts intuition. Re-read carefully: as the plunger **closes** the gap, $g \to 0$, so $F \to \infty$. So actually, force is highest at the *end* of the stroke. The "useful" working force is therefore defined at a *mid-stroke* position, and the *starting force* is the smaller of the two. KTU questions often test this exact misconception.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Starter motor solenoids, fuel injector valves, transmission shift solenoids, door lock actuators.
- **Industrial Automation:** Pneumatic/hydraulic 5/2 and 5/3 way spool valves, sorting gate diverters.
- **Medical Devices:** Insulin pumps, MRI patient table locks, contrast media injectors.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Dot-matrix printers (print head impact), camera shutters, vending machines.
- **Safety Systems:** Fire suppression valves, emergency door releases, circuit breaker tripping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Solenoid Force as a Function of Air Gap and MMF

**Problem Setup:** A DC solenoid has a coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I$. The plunger is separated from the fixed pole face by an air gap of length $g$ and cross-sectional area $A$. The iron path has negligible reluctance compared to the air gap. Derive the pull force $F$ as a function of $N$, $I$, $g$, and $A$.

---

**Step 1 — Define the magnetic circuit and apply Ampère's Law.**

The total MMF around the closed magnetic loop equals the line integral of $H$ around the path. Assuming the iron reluctance is negligible:

$$
N I = H_g \cdot g
$$

Here $H_g$ is the magnetic field intensity inside the air gap. We solve for $H_g$:

$$
H_g = \frac{N I}{g}
$$

**Reasoning:** The coil MMF is "consumed" almost entirely across the air gap because air is a much poorer magnetic conductor than iron.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the flux density in the air gap.**

In the air gap, $\mu_r \approx 1$, so:

$$
B_g = \mu_0 H_g = \mu_0 \frac{N I}{g}
$$

**Reasoning:** The flux density is directly proportional to MMF and inversely proportional to gap length.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the magnetic flux crossing the gap.**

$$
\Phi = B_g \cdot A = \mu_0 \frac{N I}{g} \cdot A
$$

**Reasoning:** Flux is flux density integrated over the pole face area.

---

**Step 4 — Derive the mechanical force using the energy method (Maxwell Stress / Virtual Work).**

The energy stored in the air gap's magnetic field is:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot B_g \cdot H_g \cdot (A \cdot g) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B_g^2}{\mu_0} \cdot (A \cdot g)
$$

**Reasoning:** Volume energy density in a magnetic field is $\frac{1}{2} B H = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ in air. Multiply by the gap volume $A \cdot g$.

---

**Step 5 — Apply the principle of virtual displacement.**

The mechanical force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to the air gap length:

$$
F = - \frac{\partial W}{\partial g}
$$

Since $W = \dfrac{B_g^2 A g}{2 \mu_0}$ and $B_g$ depends on $g$ via Step 2 ($B_g = \mu_0 NI / g$), substitute:

$$
F = - \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left[ \frac{1}{2 \mu_0} \left( \frac{\mu_0 N I}{g} \right)^2 A g \right]
$$

$$
F = - \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left[ \frac{\mu_0 (N I)^2 A}{2 g^2} \cdot g \right]
$$

$$
F = - \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left[ \frac{\mu_0 (N I)^2 A}{2 g} \right]
$$

---

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to gap $g$.**

$$
F = - \frac{\mu_0 (N I)^2 A}{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{g^2} \right)
$$

$$
F = \frac{\mu_0 (N I)^2 A}{2 g^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Derived Result (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Equation):**
> $$\boxed{\,F = \frac{\mu_0 \, A \, (N I)^2}{2 \, g^2}\,}$$
> This is the **canonical solenoid pull-force equation**. It is valid under the assumption that the air-gap reluctance dominates the magnetic circuit and the fringing effects are neglected.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A solenoid has 1200 turns, a pole-face area of $4 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$, an air gap of $2 \ \text{mm}$, and a coil current of $1.5 \ \text{A}$. Calculate the pull force. Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**
$N = 1200$, $A = 4 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$, $g = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$, $I = 1.5 \ \text{A}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $NI$.**

$$
N I = 1200 \times 1.5 = 1800 \ \text{A·t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the force equation.**

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1800)^2}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$
(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4}) = 16\pi \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
(1800)^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 16\pi \times 10^{-11} \times 3.24 \times 10^{6} = 51.84 \pi \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \approx 162.86 \times 10^{-5} = 1.6286 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$
2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$
F = \frac{1.6286 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 203.58 \ \text{N}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $F \approx \mathbf{203.6 \ N}$ (approx. 20.7 kgf of pull force)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DC vs AC Solenoids

| Parameter | DC Solenoid | AC Solenoid |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Supply | Rectified / battery | Mains 50/60 Hz |
| Force characteristic | Smooth, monotonic | Pulsating at $2f$ (100/120 Hz) |
| Lamination requirement | Solid core OK | Laminated core mandatory (eddy current loss) |
| Inrush current | Equal to steady-state | 5–10× steady-state at stroke end |
| Hum (vibration) | None | Acoustic 100/120 Hz hum → shading coil used |
| Holding force | Depends on steady $I$ | Depends on RMS $I$ |
| Driver circuit | Transistor + flyback diode | TRIAC / SSR |

> [!NOTE]
> **AC hum suppression:** A **shading ring** (a short-circuited copper loop) is embedded in the pole face. The induced current in this ring produces a phase-shifted secondary flux that prevents the total flux from ever reaching zero, eliminating the 100/120 Hz mechanical vibration. This is a frequent KTU 2-mark question.

---

### 3.4 Time Response Analysis — Pull-in and Drop-out Delays

The coil behaves as a series RL circuit during energization:

$$
i(t) = \frac{V}{R_c} \left( 1 - e^{-t / \tau} \right), \quad \tau = \frac{L}{R_c}
$$

The plunger begins to move when $i(t) \geq I_{pull\text{-}in}$, a threshold current. This defines the **pull-in time**:

$$
t_{pull} = \tau \ln \left( \frac{V/R_c}{V/R_c - I_{pull\text{-}in}} \right)
$$

On de-energization, the flux decays and the plunger releases when $i \leq I_{drop\text{-}out}$:

$$
t_{drop} = \tau \ln \left( \frac{I_{hold}}{I_{drop\text{-}out}} \right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often assume $L$ is constant. In reality, $L$ varies with plunger position because the air gap $g$ appears in the reluctance. As the plunger closes, $L$ increases (reluctance drops), making the time response **non-linear and position-dependent**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solenoid Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller\nCommand Signal] --> B[Driver Circuit\nMOSFET or BJT]
    B --> C[Solenoid Coil\nN turns of copper wire]
    C --> D[Magnetic Field\nEstablishment in Core]
    D --> E[Air Gap Flux\nCrosses Pole Face]
    E --> F[Mechanical Force\non Plunger]
    F --> G[Linear Displacement\nStroke Length x]
    G --> H[Mechanical Load\nValve or Latch]
    H --> I[Position Feedback\nOptional Limit Switch]
    I --> A
    C --> J[Flyback Diode\nProtects Driver]
    K[DC Supply\n12V or 24V] --> B
    L[Return Spring] --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Schematic — Solenoid Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HousingAssembly [Solenoid Housing Assembly]
        Coil[Copper Coil\nN turns wound on bobbin]
        Core[Yoke or Return Path\nFerromagnetic iron]
        Plunger[Movable Plunger\nSoft iron armature]
        Gap[Air Gap g\nWorking stroke region]
        Spring[Return Spring\nPushes plunger back]
        Stop1[Fixed Pole Face\nStationary]
        Stop2[Plunger End Face\nMoving]
    end
    Coil --> Gap
    Gap --> Plunger
    Plunger --> Spring
    Stop1 --- Gap
    Gap --- Stop2
    Core -.-> Coil
    Core -.-> Stop1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Force-Stroke Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stroke x = 0\nFully Closed] -->|Force approaches infinity| B[Mid Stroke\nMaximum usable force]
    B -->|Force decreases as gap increases| C[Stroke x = max\nMinimum force]
    C -->|Spring force exceeds pull force| D[Plunger Returns to Rest]
    D -->|De-energized| A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the Diagram:** The force-stroke curve is **highly non-linear and rises sharply near closure**. Designers must ensure the load counter-force (spring + external load) lies *below* the solenoid curve for the *entire* stroke. Otherwise, the solenoid will stall partway.

### 4.4 Mermaid Driver Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[DC Supply Vcc] --> Coil[Solenoid Coil L]
    Coil --> Drain[MOSFET Drain]
    Gate[MOSFET Gate\nFrom MCU PWM] --> Drain
    Source[MOSFET Source] --> GND[Ground]
    Diode[Flyback Diode\nCathode at Vcc] --> VCC
    Diode --- Coil
    Snub[RC Snubber\nOptional] --- Coil
    Snub --- Drain
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The flyback diode** is reverse-biased during normal operation (cathode at $+V_{cc}$, anode at the coil-MOSFET junction) and becomes forward-biased only when the MOSFET switches off, providing a safe path for the inductive kick current to circulate and dissipate as $I^2R$ loss.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a solenoid. List any four engineering applications of solenoids in mechatronic systems.*

**Model Answer:**

> A solenoid is a linear electromechanical actuator that produces a controlled mechanical displacement of a ferromagnetic plunger by the magnetic field generated when an electric current flows through a coil wound around it.

**Four Engineering Applications:**

1. **Automotive starter motor** — engages the pinion gear with the flywheel.
2. **Pneumatic/hydraulic directional control valves** — shifts the spool to direct fluid flow.
3. **Fuel injectors in IC engines** — precisely meters fuel delivery into the cylinder.
4. **Electromagnetic door locks in security systems** — locks/unlocks access-controlled doors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each × 4 = 2 marks for applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a flyback diode and why is it essential in a DC solenoid driver circuit?*

**Model Answer:**

> A **flyback diode** (also called a freewheeling or snubber diode) is a diode connected in **anti-parallel** with the solenoid coil — its cathode tied to the positive supply and its anode tied to the switching transistor's collector/drain terminal.

**Why essential:** When the switching transistor is turned OFF, the magnetic flux in the coil collapses rapidly, inducing a large back-EMF of opposite polarity given by $V_L = -L \, (dI/dt)$. Without the diode, this spike can reach hundreds of volts and **destroy the MOSFET or BJT driver**. The diode provides a low-impedance path for the inductive current to circulate and decay safely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for polarity, 1 mark for destruction prevention logic.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Provision (KTU ESE Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each full question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a linear DC solenoid. Discuss the role of the return spring. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the pull force of a solenoid in terms of the air gap length $g$, pole-face area $A$, number of turns $N$, and coil current $I$. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A linear DC solenoid consists of the following components:
- **Bobbin/Former:** A non-magnetic plastic or aluminum spool on which the copper wire is wound.
- **Coil:** Insulated copper wire (typically enameled) wound in $N$ turns. Resistance $R_c$ is determined by wire gauge and mean turn length.
- **Yoke/Frame:** A ferromagnetic (low-carbon steel) outer cylindrical frame that completes the magnetic circuit and provides mechanical support.
- **Plunger (Armature):** A movable soft-iron rod that slides axially inside the coil.
- **Fixed Pole Face:** A stationary iron disc at the closed end of the housing.
- **Air Gap:** The variable working gap $g$ between the plunger tip and the fixed pole face.
- **Return Spring:** A helical compression spring that pushes the plunger back to its rest position when the coil is de-energized.
- **Lead Wires/Connector:** Power input terminals.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. When DC current $I$ is applied to the coil, the coil generates a magnetomotive force $NI$.
2. The MMF drives magnetic flux through the iron yoke, across the air gap, and back through the plunger.
3. The flux lines crossing the air gap exert an attractive Maxwell stress on the plunger, pulling it axially into the coil.
4. The plunger travels through its full stroke $x$, doing mechanical work on the load.
5. When the supply is cut off, the return spring pushes the plunger back to its rest position, and the magnetic energy is dissipated partly as heat in the flyback diode.

**Role of Return Spring (1 mark):**
The spring provides a **restoring force** for the de-energized state and stores potential energy that is released during the return stroke. It also counterbalances gravity in vertical mounting configurations and ensures the plunger does not stick due to residual magnetism.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Assumption statement (1 mark):**
- The iron path has negligible reluctance compared to the air gap.
- Fringing of flux at the air gap is neglected (uniform field assumption).
- Magnetic circuit is linear (no saturation).

**Step 2 — Derivation (6 marks):**
The detailed derivation is as per **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final boxed result is:

$$
F = \frac{\mu_0 \, A \, (N I)^2}{2 \, g^2}
$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space
- $A$ = pole face area
- $N$ = number of coil turns
- $I$ = coil current
- $g$ = air gap length

The derivation proceeds via Ampère's Law, flux computation, and the virtual work principle (energy method).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Mandatory:** Explicitly state the assumption that iron reluctance is neglected. Marks are deducted if the assumption is missing.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse** the direction of differentiation. Some students write $F = +dW/dg$ — this is the *co-energy* approach. With the energy method, it is $F = -dW/dg$ because decreasing $g$ releases energy.
> 3. **Forgetting to square $NI$** is the single most common algebraic error. KTU examiners award partial credit for the correct structure.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare DC and AC solenoids with respect to (i) supply, (ii) force characteristic, (iii) lamination requirement, (iv) humming noise, and (v) holding current. (7 marks)

**(b)** A solenoid has $N = 800$ turns, pole-face area $A = 6 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$, and air gap $g = 1.5 \ \text{mm}$. If the coil current is $I = 2 \ \text{A}$, calculate: (i) the magnetomotive force, (ii) the flux density in the air gap, and (iii) the pull force. Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

Refer to the **Comparison Matrix in Section 3.3** of these notes. Each row of the matrix earns 1.4 marks, totaling 7 marks.

| Parameter | DC Solenoid | AC Solenoid |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| (i) Supply | Battery / rectified DC | AC mains 50/60 Hz |
| (ii) Force | Smooth constant | Pulsating at 2f (100/120 Hz) |
| (iii) Lamination | Not required | Laminated core mandatory |
| (iv) Humming | Silent | 100/120 Hz hum, suppressed by shading ring |
| (v) Holding current | Steady $I = V/R$ | RMS value, inrush 5–10× higher |

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $N = 800$, $A = 6 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$, $g = 1.5 \ \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$, $I = 2 \ \text{A}$.

**(i) Magnetomotive Force (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I = 800 \times 2 = \mathbf{1600 \ \text{At}}
$$

**(ii) Flux Density in the Air Gap (2 marks):**

$$
B_g = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{g} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1600}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
B_g = \frac{6.4 \pi \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.267 \pi \times 10^{-1} \approx \mathbf{1.34 \ T}
$$

**(iii) Pull Force (3 marks):**

$$
F = \frac{B_g^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{(1.34)^2 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}
$$

**Numerator:** $(1.34)^2 \approx 1.796$, $\times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.077 \times 10^{-3}$

**Denominator:** $8\pi \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.513 \times 10^{-6}$

$$
F = \frac{1.077 \times 10^{-3}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{428.5 \ N}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** is critical: always convert mm to m before squaring the gap in the denominator. A common error is using $g = 1.5$ in the formula, yielding an answer 10⁶ times too large.
> 2. **Don't round $B_g$ too early.** Round only in the final step to preserve accuracy.
> 3. **Show the units** in every intermediate step. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unitless answers.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Bullet Points Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall:**

- [x] A solenoid is a **linear electromechanical actuator** that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical force and motion.
- [x] The fundamental force equation is $\displaystyle F = \frac{\mu_0 A (NI)^2}{2 g^2}$.
- [x] Force is **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap** — force increases dramatically as the plunger closes the gap.
- [x] **Force is proportional to the square of MMF** — doubling the current quadruples the force.
- [x] The **magnetomotive force** is $\mathcal{F} = NI$ in ampere-turns (At).
- [x] The dominant reluctance in a solenoid magnetic circuit is the **air gap**, not the iron.
- [x] **Flyback diodes** are mandatory protection across the coil in DC driver circuits.
- [x] **AC solenoids** require **laminated cores** to suppress eddy current losses.
- [x] **Shading rings** (copper loops) in AC solenoids eliminate 100/120 Hz acoustic hum.
- [x] The **return spring** restores the plunger to its rest position upon de-energization.
- [x] **Inrush current** in AC solenoids can be 5–10× the steady-state value at the end of the stroke.
- [x] The coil's **time constant** $\tau = L/R_c$ governs the pull-in and drop-out delays.
- [x] Inductance $L$ is **position-dependent** because the air gap appears in the reluctance.
- [x] Solenoids are **ON/OFF actuators**, not proportional servos — they are not suitable for closed-loop position control without a separate feedback mechanism.
- [x] **Key applications:** fuel injectors, pneumatic valves, automotive starters, door locks, and printer hammers.
- [x] **Standard KTU values to memorize:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, copper resistivity $\rho \approx 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — SOLENOIDS (Module 3, PEEBT863)**
> *These notes are tuned for KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation patterns. Practise the 14-mark derivations under timed conditions and verify all numerical computations twice before submission.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC and AC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC and AC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electric Motors in Mechatronics — The Foundational Definition

An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational motion) through the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors. In the context of mechatronics, motors are the **primary actuation elements** of any system — they are the "muscles" that execute commands issued by the controller (brain).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):** "A motor is an actuator that produces a controlled rotary or linear mechanical output in response to an electrical input signal, forming the output stage of a closed-loop mechatronic system."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Wheel of Magnets"

Imagine a water wheel where, instead of gravity pulling water, **magnetic push and pull** spin a rotor. Inside a motor:
- A **stator** (stationary part) creates a steady magnetic field.
- A **rotor** (rotating part) carries coils that, when energized, are pushed or pulled by the stator's field.
- The result: continuous rotation, just like water keeps a wheel turning, but here it is **current** that keeps the magnetic wheel turning.

> [!NOTE]
> In mechatronics, motors are rarely used "open-loop." They are almost always paired with **sensors** (encoders, resolvers, tachometers) and **controllers** (PLCs, microcontrollers) to form a closed-loop feedback system.

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Motors for KTU Syllabus

Electric motors are broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Operating Power Source | Key KTU-Relevant Types |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Motors** | Direct Current | Brushed DC, Brushless DC (BLDC), Servo DC, Stepper (DC variant) |
| **AC Motors** | Alternating Current | Induction (Squirrel Cage, Wound Rotor), Synchronous, Servo AC, Stepper (AC variant) |

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for Mechatronics (PEEBT863)

- **Actuation Selection:** Choosing between DC and AC motors dictates the power electronics, controller type, and sensor feedback scheme.
- **Tuning the Plant:** The motor's transfer function is a core part of the mechatronic system's **plant model** used in PID design.
- **Industrial Relevance:** CNC machines, robotic arms, conveyor belts, and EVs all rely on either DC or AC motor drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping):** This topic directly addresses **CO3** — *Select and Interface appropriate Actuators, Sensors, and Controllers for Mechatronic Systems.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Motors — Operating Principle

A **DC motor** operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction (in reverse)**. When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

where $B$ is the magnetic flux density (Tesla), $I$ is the armature current (Ampere), and $L$ is the length of the conductor (m). This tangential force on multiple conductors produces a **torque** on the rotor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The very same machine, if mechanically driven, becomes a **DC generator** (dynamo). The physical law is identical — only the direction of energy flow differs.

### 2.2 Construction of a Brushed DC Motor

- **Stator (Field System):** Produces the main magnetic flux using either permanent magnets (PMDC) or field windings (electromagnetic).
- **Rotor (Armature):** The rotating part carrying armature windings.
- **Commutator:** A split-ring mechanical rectifier that reverses current direction in the armature coils every half rotation to maintain **unidirectional torque**.
- **Brushes:** Carbon contacts that slide on the commutator to deliver current.

### 2.3 Types of DC Motors (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Field Winding Connection | Torque-Speed Characteristic | Typical Mechatronic Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shunt (Separately Excited)** | Field in parallel with armature | Nearly constant speed, good regulation | CNC spindle drives, machine tools |
| **Series** | Field in series with armature | Very high starting torque, variable speed | Cranes, traction, hoists |
| **Compound (Cumulative)** | Both series + shunt field | Mix of shunt and series traits | Presses, elevators, elevators |
| **PMDC** | Permanent magnet stator | Linear, easy to control | Robotics, small servo loops |
| **BLDC (Brushless DC)** | Electronic commutation | High efficiency, low maintenance | Drones, EV traction, hard drives |

### 2.4 The Two Governing Equations of a DC Motor

These are **the most important equations in this module** — memorize them.

**Equation 1 — Back-EMF (Electromotive Force):**

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

**Equation 2 — Developed Motor Torque:**

$$T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

In SI units, the constants are related: $K_e = K_t$ (numerically equal when SI units are used).

### 2.5 Armature Voltage Equation (KVL of the Armature Loop)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature circuit:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

Substituting $E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$:

$$V_a = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega + I_a \cdot R_a$$

### 2.6 The Speed Equation (Solving for $\omega$)

Rearranging the armature voltage equation:

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e \cdot \Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation reveals the **two classic ways to control DC motor speed:**
> 1. **Armature Voltage Control** — vary $V_a$ (keep $\Phi$ constant). Linear, fast response.
> 2. **Field Flux Control** — vary $\Phi$ (keep $V_a$ constant). Used above base speed.

### 2.7 Torque-Speed Characteristic Curve (Linear)

Combining the two governing equations:

$$T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot \left(\frac{V_a - K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}{R_a}\right)$$

This is a **straight line** in the $T$–$\omega$ plane. At $\omega = 0$ (stall), $T$ is maximum (= starting torque). At $T = 0$ (no load), $\omega$ is maximum (= no-load speed).

### 2.8 AC Motors — Operating Principle

AC motors operate on the principle of a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**, discovered by **Nikola Tesla (1888)**. A polyphase supply (typically 3-phase) fed to spatially displaced stator windings produces a magnetic field that rotates at a speed called the **synchronous speed**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$

where $f$ is supply frequency (Hz) and $P$ is the number of poles.

### 2.9 The Three-Phase Rotating Magnetic Field — Step Logic

1. Three stator coils are placed **120° apart in space**.
2. Three-phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are **120° apart in time**.
3. At any instant, the **vector sum** of the three flux components produces a resultant flux of **constant magnitude**.
4. This resultant flux vector **rotates around the stator** at synchronous speed $N_s$.

### 2.10 Three-Phase Induction Motor — The Workhorse of Industry

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Three-phase windings embedded in laminated silicon steel slots.
- **Rotor:** Two variants — **Squirrel Cage** (most common, robust) and **Wound Rotor** (with slip rings for variable resistance control).

**Operating Principle:**
- The stator RMF cuts the rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's law).
- Since the rotor circuit is closed, induced current flows.
- This current-carrying rotor in the stator's field experiences a force (Lorentz force), producing **torque**.
- The rotor can NEVER reach $N_s$ — it must "slip" so that relative motion induces current.

### 2.11 Slip — The Heart of Induction Motor Operation

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

where $N_r$ is the actual rotor speed. Typical full-load slip: **2% to 5%** for industrial motors.

Rotor speed in terms of slip:

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

### 2.12 Torque Equation of an Induction Motor (KTU Critical Formula)

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot \left(\frac{R_r}{s}\right)}{\left(R_s + \frac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum (Breakdown) Torque** occurs at slip $s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}$ and is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_r$. However, the slip at which $T_{max}$ occurs IS dependent on $R_r$ — this is the key principle behind **rotor resistance control** for starting.

### 2.13 Synchronous Motor — Constant Speed Champion

- Rotor is either a **salient-pole** (low speed) or **cylindrical** (high speed) electromagnet (or permanent magnet).
- Rotor locks to and rotates at $N_s$ exactly — **zero slip** under steady state.
- Not self-starting (without auxiliary means) — requires damper windings or VFD.
- Used in **constant-speed applications**: power factor correction, large pumps, compressors.

### 2.14 Stepper Motors — Mechatronic Precision Actuator

- A **stepper motor** rotates in **discrete angular steps** (typically 0.9°, 1.8°, 7.5°) when energized by a digital pulse train.
- **Open-loop positioning** — no encoder needed.
- **Types:** Variable Reluctance (VR), Permanent Magnet (PM), Hybrid.
- Used in 3D printers, CNC, robotics, camera focus mechanisms.

### 2.15 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque | N·m |
| 3 | $V_a = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | Armature KVL | V |
