# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section develops the **canonical KTU-style derivations** a student must be able to reproduce in the 14-mark questions: (i) the LVDT output, (ii) the strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge, (iii) the accelerometer mass–spring–damper, and (iv) the NTC thermistor sensitivity.

---

## 3.1 LVDT — Derivation of the Differential Output Voltage

**Setup.** Primary coil $P$ excited by $V_p \sin(\omega t)$. Two identical secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ connected in series-opposition. A movable core shifts the mutual inductances.

Let:
- $M_1$ = mutual inductance between $P$ and $S_1$
- $M_2$ = mutual inductance between $P$ and $S_2$

By the transformer EMF equation, the induced EMFs are:

$$
e_{S_1} \;=\; -M_1 \dfrac{d i_p}{dt} \;=\; -M_1 \,\omega\, \dfrac{V_p}{X_p} \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
e_{S_2} \;=\; -M_2 \dfrac{d i_p}{dt} \;=\; -M_2 \,\omega\, \dfrac{V_p}{X_p} \cos(\omega t)
$$

With the secondaries in series-opposition (their polarities opposite), the net output is:

$$
e_\text{out} \;=\; e_{S_1} - e_{S_2} \;=\; -\omega \dfrac{V_p}{X_p} (M_1 - M_2) \cos(\omega t)
$$

For small displacements from the null, $M_1 - M_2$ is a linear function of $x$:

$$
M_1 - M_2 \;=\; k_\text{LVDT} \cdot x
$$

Therefore:

$$
e_\text{out}(t) \;=\; -K_\text{LVDT} \, x \,\cos(\omega t)
$$

where the constant $K_\text{LVDT} = \omega V_p \, k_\text{LVDT} / X_p$ absorbs all system parameters.

> **Key facts for the answer script:**
> - At the **null** position ($x = 0$) the output is **zero** (no phase, no magnitude).
> - The **magnitude** of $e_\text{out}$ is proportional to $\vert x \vert$.
> - The **phase** of $e_\text{out}$ flips by 180° when $x$ changes sign → indicates direction.
> - Hence a **phase-sensitive demodulator** is required for proper direction sensing.

---

## 3.2 Strain-Gauge Wheatstone Bridge — Full Derivation

**Bridge circuit.** Four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ form a diamond. Excitation $V_\text{exc}$ is applied across one diagonal. Output $V_\text{out}$ is measured across the other diagonal.

**Step 1 — Node voltages.** The output node is between the mid-points of the two arms. Using the standard voltage-divider relations:

$$
V_A \;=\; V_\text{exc} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
V_B \;=\; V_\text{exc} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage.** The bridge output is the difference of these two node voltages:

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; V_A - V_B \;=\; V_\text{exc} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Balanced condition.** The bridge is balanced (zero output) when $V_A = V_B$:

$$
\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \;=\; \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

Cross-multiplying gives the well-known balance equation:

$$
R_1 \cdot R_3 \;=\; R_2 \cdot R_4
$$

**Step 4 — Linearization for small $\Delta R$.** For a single active gauge $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and the other three at $R$, the output becomes:

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; V_\text{exc} \left( \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)
$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$, the binomial expansion $\dfrac{1}{2 + (\Delta R/R)} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - \Delta R/(2R))$ gives:

$$
V_\text{out} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_\text{exc}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

Substituting $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$
V_\text{out} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_\text{exc}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 — Full-bridge advantage.** With all four gauges active (two in tension $\varepsilon$, two in compression $-\varepsilon$), the output doubles four-fold:

$$
V_\text{out} \;\approx\; V_\text{exc} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

— exactly the formula quoted in §2.4.1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a full bridge?** It (i) doubles the output, (ii) cancels temperature-induced common-mode resistance changes, and (iii) compensates for non-linearity to first order. This is why commercial load cells ship as full bridges.

---

## 3.3 Accelerometer — Mass–Spring–Damper Derivation

**Free-body diagram.** A proof mass $m$ is connected to the housing by spring $k$ and damper $c$. The housing is fixed to the structure under test, which has acceleration $a(t)$. The mass moves by $x(t)$ *relative* to the housing.

**Newton's second law on the mass** (in the housing's reference frame, the housing imparts an inertial force $-m\,a$ on the mass):

$$
m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x \;=\; -m\,a(t)
$$

**Transfer function.** Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$
\bigl(m s^2 + c s + k\bigr) X(s) \;=\; -m\,A(s)
$$

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{A(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{-m}{m s^2 + c s + k} \;=\; \dfrac{-1}{s^2 + (c/m) s + (k/m)}
$$

Define the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{A(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{-1}{s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2}
$$

**DC sensitivity** (let $s = 0$):

$$
\left.\dfrac{X}{A}\right\vert_{s=0} \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} \;=\; -\dfrac{m}{k}
$$

— meaning that at very low frequencies, the *deflection* $x$ is proportional to the *acceleration* $a$. The constant $m/k$ is the accelerometer's *static sensitivity* [m·s²/m].

**High-frequency asymptote** ($s \gg \omega_n$):

$$
\dfrac{X}{A} \;\to\; -\dfrac{1}{s^2}
$$

Integrating twice in the time domain: $x = -\int\!\!\int a\,dt^2$, i.e., the *displacement* of the proof mass becomes a measure of the *vibration displacement* of the housing. This is the basis of seismic vibration pickups.

**Resonance.** The peak of $\vert X/A \vert$ occurs at $\omega \approx \omega_n$ for low $\zeta$. For $\zeta = 0.707$, the response is maximally flat (Butterworth).

---

## 3.4 NTC Thermistor — Sensitivity Derivation

The NTC resistance is:

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

Differentiate with respect to $T$ to get the rate of change of resistance:

$$
\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} \;=\; R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \cdot \left(-\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} \;=\; -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}\,R_T
$$

The **temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR)** is therefore:

$$
\alpha_T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{R_T}\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} \;=\; -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}
$$

At $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$ with $\beta = 4000\ \text{K}$:

$$
\alpha_T \;=\; -\dfrac{4000}{(298.15)^2} \;\approx\; -0.045\ \text{K}^{-1} \;=\; -4.5\,\%/\text{K}
$$

— confirming the rule of thumb that NTC thermistors change resistance by about **4 % per °C** at room temperature, and the resistance **decreases** with increasing temperature (hence "NTC" — Negative Temperature Coefficient).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Example 1 — Potentiometer Loading Error

> A linear potentiometer has total resistance $R_\text{tot} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and is excited with $V_\text{exc} = 10\ \text{V}$. The wiper is at 40 % of full travel. A voltmeter with input resistance $R_M = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected across the wiper and one end. Find (a) the *unloaded* output, (b) the *loaded* output, (c) the *loading error* in %.

**Solution.**

(a) Unloaded: $V_\text{out,ideal} = V_\text{exc} \cdot (x/L) = 10 \times 0.4 = 4.0\ \text{V}$.

(b) The wiper and one end form a resistance $R_p = 0.4 \times 10 = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$. This is in parallel with the voltmeter $R_M = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$
R_\text{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{R_p R_M}{R_p + R_M} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 20}{4 + 20}\ \text{k}\Omega \;=\; \dfrac{80}{24}\ \text{k}\Omega \;\approx\; 3.333\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

The remaining portion of the track is $R_\text{rem} = 0.6 \times 10 = 6\ \text{k}\Omega$, in series with the parallel combination. Total track:

$$
R_\text{track-loaded} \;=\; 6 + 3.333 \;=\; 9.333\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

The actual output is the voltage divider across the parallel combination:

$$
V_\text{out,actual} \;=\; V_\text{exc} \cdot \dfrac{R_\text{eq}}{R_\text{track-loaded}} \;=\; 10 \cdot \dfrac{3.333}{9.333} \;\approx\; 3.571\ \text{V}
$$

(c) Loading error:

$$
\delta \;=\; \dfrac{V_\text{ideal} - V_\text{actual}}{V_\text{ideal}} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{4.0 - 3.571}{4.0} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 10.7\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often use the formula $\delta = Z_s/(Z_s + Z_\text{in}) \times 100\%$ directly. That is valid only when the source is a pure voltage source with source impedance $Z_s$ — for a potentiometer at the *middle of travel*, the effective source impedance is $R_p \Vert R_\text{rem}$, not $R_\text{tot}/4$. Always re-derive for the wiper position given.

---

### Example 2 — Strain-Gauge Load Cell

> A 1000 N load cell uses a full-bridge with four active gauges, $G_f = 2.0$, $V_\text{exc} = 10\ \text{V}$. The Poisson's ratio of the structural material is $\nu = 0.3$. Two gauges measure axial strain, two measure transverse strain. Find the bridge output for an axial force of 500 N. The cross-section is $A = 50\ \text{mm}^2$ and $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$.

**Solution.**

Axial stress:

$$
\sigma_a \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{500}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 10 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \;=\; 10\ \text{MPa}
$$

Axial strain:

$$
\varepsilon_a \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_a}{E} \;=\; \dfrac{10 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 50\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

Transverse strain (Poisson contraction):

$$
\varepsilon_t \;=\; -\nu\, \varepsilon_a \;=\; -0.3 \times 50\ \mu\varepsilon \;=\; -15\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

For a Poisson bridge, two gauges sense $+\varepsilon_a$ and two sense $-\varepsilon_t$. The effective strain seen by the bridge is:

$$
\varepsilon_\text{eff} \;=\; \varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_t \;=\; 50 - (-15) \;=\; 65\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

The full-bridge output:

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; V_\text{exc} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon_\text{eff} \;=\; 10 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 65 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.30\ \text{mV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Poisson bridge gives $(1 + \nu)$ times the output of a single-gauge bridge. With $\nu = 0.3$, that is a 30 % gain — a free sensitivity boost that is often exploited in real load-cell designs.

---

### Example 3 — Encoder Velocity Computation

> An incremental encoder has 2000 PPR and is read in ×4 quadrature. In a 10 ms sampling window, the counter reads 320 counts. Find the shaft speed in rpm and rad/s.

**Solution.**

Counts per revolution in ×4 mode:

$$
N_\text{cpr} \;=\; 2000 \times 4 \;=\; 8000\ \text{counts/rev}
$$

Pulses in 10 ms = 320 counts. Revolutions in 10 ms:

$$
\Delta \text{rev} \;=\; \dfrac{320}{8000} \;=\; 0.04\ \text{rev}
$$

Speed in rev/s:

$$
n \;=\; \dfrac{0.04}{0.010} \;=\; 4\ \text{rev/s}
$$

Speed in rpm:

$$
N \;=\; 4 \times 60 \;=\; 240\ \text{rpm}
$$

Angular speed:

$$
\omega \;=\; 2\pi n \;=\; 2\pi \times 4 \;\approx\; 25.13\ \text{rad/s}
$$

---

### Example 4 — NTC Thermistor Cold-Joint Compensation Problem

> An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\ \text{°C} = 298.15\ \text{K}$ and $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$. The thermistor is in a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ series resistor and 5 V supply. Find the output voltage at $T = 75\ \text{°C} = 348.15\ \text{K}$.

**Solution.**

Resistance at 75 °C:

$$
R_T \;=\; 10 \exp\!\left[3950 \left(\dfrac{1}{348.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Compute the bracket:

$$
\dfrac{1}{348.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15} \;=\; \dfrac{298.15 - 348.15}{348.15 \times 298.15} \;=\; \dfrac{-50}{103{,}798} \;\approx\; -4.817 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_T \;=\; 10 \times \exp(-3950 \times 4.817 \times 10^{-4})\ \text{k}\Omega \;=\; 10 \times \exp(-1.903)\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\exp(-1.903) \;\approx\; 0.1492
$$

$$
R_T \;\approx\; 1.492\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Output voltage:

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; 5 \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_T + 10} \;=\; 5 \cdot \dfrac{1.492}{1.492 + 10} \;=\; 5 \cdot \dfrac{1.492}{11.492} \;\approx\; 0.649\ \text{V}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

This section provides the **block diagrams, sensor signal-conditioning chains, and mechatronic feedback loops** that examiners expect in 7- or 14-mark answers. All diagrams use Mermaid (with the safe-node rule: no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels, all IDs alphanumeric and prefixed with letters).

---

## 4.1 Internal Sensor Functional Architecture Flow (Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the universal signal chain from a physical variable through an internal sensor, signal conditioner, ADC, controller, actuator, and back to the plant. Use this as the framework to position *any* internal sensor in a mechatronic loop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PHYVAR[Physical Variable e.g. Position Force Pressure] --> TRANS[Transducer Element Converts physical to electrical]
    TRANS --> SIGCOND[Signal Conditioner Amplify Filter Linearize]
    SIGCOND --> ADC[ADC Converter Analog to Digital]
    ADC --> CTRL[Microcontroller PLC DSP Controller]
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Motor Valve Heater]
    ACT --> PLANT[Plant Mechanical System]
    PLANT --> PHYVAR
    CTRL --> DISPLAY[HMI Display Data Logger]
    CTRL --> ALARM[Alarm Threshold Check]
```

> **Reading the loop:** The internal sensor sits on the feedback path from the **Plant** back to the **Controller**. The controller compares the measured value with the reference and drives the actuator to minimise the error. The display and alarm are auxiliary outputs of the controller.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Internal Sensors

```mermaid
graph TD
    ISENS[INTERNAL SENSORS] --> POS[Position and Displacement]
    ISENS --> VEL[Velocity]
    ISENS --> ACC[Acceleration]
    ISENS --> FRC[Force and Torque]
    ISENS --> PRS[Pressure]
    ISENS --> TMP[Temperature]
    ISENS --> OTH[Others Touch Flow Current]

    POS --> POT[Potentiometer]
    POS --> LVDT[LVDT]
    POS --> ENC[Optical Encoder Incremental Absolute]
    POS --> HALL[Hall Effect Sensor]

    VEL --> TACH[Tachogenerator DC AC]
    VEL --> SFTV[Software Tachometer from Encoder]

    ACC --> PIEZO[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    ACC --> PR[Piezoresistive Accelerometer]
    ACC --> MEMS[MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer]

    FRC --> SG[Strain Gauge Load Cell]
    FRC --> PFR[Piezoelectric Force Sensor]

    PRS --> DSP[Diaphragm plus Strain Gauge]
    PRS --> DPR[Piezoresistive MEMS]
    PRS --> CAP[Capacitive Diaphragm]

    TMP --> NTC[NTC Thermistor]
    TMP --> RTD[RTD PT100]
    TMP --> TC[Thermocouple Type J K T]
    TMP --> LMS[Semiconductor LM35 DS18B20]
```

---

## 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge

```mermaid
graph LR
    VEXC[Vexcitation DC Source] --> N1
    N1((Node 1)) --> R1
    R1 --> N2((Node 2))
    R1[Active Gauge R1]:::gauge
    N1 --> R2[R2 Reference]
    R2 --> N2
    N2 --> GND[Ground]
    N2 --> N3((Node 3))
    R3 --> N3
    R3[Reference R3] --> N4((Node 4))
    R4 --> N4
    R4[Active Gauge R4] --> GND
    N1 --> R3
    VOUT[Vout Differential] --> N1
    VOUT --> N2
    VOUT --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> AD[ADC]

    classDef gauge fill:#fdd,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px;
```

---

## 4.4 LVDT Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    OSC[AC Excitation Oscillator 1 to 20 kHz] --> PRIM[Primary Coil P]
    PRIM --> CORE
    CORE[Magnetic Core Slidable]:::core --> S1[Secondary Coil S1]
    CORE --> S2[Secondary Coil S2]
    S1 --> DEMOD
    S2 --> DEMOD
    S1 -.- SER[Series Opposition Connection]
    S2 -.- SER
    SER --> DEMOD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> OUT[DC Output Vout proportional to x]

    classDef core fill:#bbf,stroke:#006,stroke-width:2px;
```

> **How to read this for the exam:** The arrows from the **core** to $S_1$ and $S_2$ indicate *magnetic coupling*, not electrical connection. The two secondaries are wired in series opposition (one reversed relative to the other) so that their EMFs subtract at the output. At the null position the subtraction is exact and $V_\text{out} = 0$.

---

## 4.5 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACC[Structure Acceleration a t] --> MASS[Proof Mass m]
    MASS --> SPRING[Spring Stiffness k]
    MASS --> DAMPER[Damper c]
    MASS --> PIEZO[Force on Piezo Crystal F equals m times a]
    PIEZO --> CHARGE[Charge Q equals d times F]
    CHARGE --> CA[Charge Amplifier Op amp with Cf]
    CA --> VOUT[Output Voltage Vout]
    CA --> FILTER[Low Pass Filter optional]
    FILTER --> VOUT
    VOUT --> ADC[ADC and Controller]
```

---

## 4.6 Optical Encoder — Incremental & Absolute

```mermaid
graph LR
    LED[LED Light Source] --> DISC[Code Disc Transparent and Opaque Pattern]
    DISC --> CH_A[Photodiode Channel A]
    DISC --> CH_B[Photodiode Channel B]
    DISC --> CH_Z[Photodiode Index Z]
    CH_A --> CMP_A[Comparator and Squarer]
    CH_B --> CMP_B[Comparator and Squarer]
    CH_Z --> CMP_Z[Index Pulse Shaper]
    CMP_A --> DECOD[Quadrature Decoder 1x 2x 4x]
    CMP_B --> DECOD
    DECOD --> CNT[Up Down Counter]
    CNT --> POS[Position theta]
    DECOD --> VEL[Velocity d theta dt]
    CMP_Z --> RESET[Counter Reset once per revolution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For an **absolute encoder** the disc has $n$ tracks and an $n$-bit code (Gray or binary). The output is a parallel word or a serial bitstream (SSI, BiSS, EnDat). The output *is* the position — no counter, no power-loss problem.

---

## 4.7 Mechatronic Closed-Loop Position-Control System

This is the canonical block diagram that should appear in your answer to *any* 14-mark question on a sensor-based control system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REF[r t Reference Input] --> SUM((Sum))
    SUM --> EC[Error e t]
    EC --> CON[Controller PID]
    CON --> AMP[Power Amplifier]
    AMP --> MOT[DC Servo Motor Actuator]
    MOT --> GEAR[Gear Train]
    GEAR --> LD[Load and Shaft]
    LD --> SENS[Internal Sensor Encoder plus Tachogenerator]
    SENS --> FB[Feedback Signal y t]
    FB --> SUM
    SENS --> VFB[Velocity Feedback omega]
    VFB --> SUM2((Inner Sum))
    CON --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> AMP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **inner loop** (velocity) and **outer loop** (position). This is a **cascaded control** architecture, the standard pattern in industrial motion control. The position error drives a velocity reference, which the velocity loop then enforces using a tachogenerator (or differentiated encoder signal) as feedback.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

All questions are tagged with simulated KTU past-year / model-question identifiers. Marks follow the official 2024 scheme: Part A = 3 marks (short answer), Part B = 14 marks (full descriptive, internal choice).

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between internal and external sensors. Give two examples of each as used in a mechatronic system such as a CNC machining centre.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Sensors | External Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Sense the *machine's own* state | Sense the *environment* |
| Examples in CNC | Optical linear encoder on axis, spindle tachogenerator, motor thermistor, spindle current sensor | Tool-length probe (touch probe), vision camera for part inspection, acoustic-emission microphone for chatter detection |
| Function | Provide feedback for *closed-loop control* and health monitoring | Provide *perception* of the workpiece, tool, and surroundings |

A 2-line crisp intro ("Internal sensors measure variables *within* the mechatronic system…") earns 1 mark; a clean table with at least two examples per category earns the remaining 2 marks.

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **List the static characteristics used to specify the performance of an internal sensor. Define any three.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The static characteristics are: **range, span, sensitivity, linearity / non-linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, accuracy, resolution, drift, dead band, threshold, and loading error**.

**Definitions (any three):**

1. **Sensitivity** — ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input, $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. Unit: V/unit input.

2. **Hysteresis** — the maximum difference between the upscale and downscale output readings for the same input, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output (FSO).

3. **Repeatability** — the ability of the sensor to produce the same output for the same input when measured repeatedly under identical conditions, expressed as $\pm 2\sigma$ or similar.

(Alternative third choice: Linearity, Resolution, Accuracy, Drift — all valid.)

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> The 14-mark question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The two choices below cover complementary aspects of Module 3 and offer a true *internal choice*.

---

### **QUESTION B-A  `[KTU Model Paper – Module 3]`**

> **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **incremental optical encoder**. Derive the expression for the angular velocity of the shaft in terms of pulse count, sampling time, and pulses per revolution.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A **load cell** uses four active strain gauges in a full Wheatstone bridge. The bridge excitation is $V_\text{exc} = 10\ \text{V}$, gauge factor $G_f = 2.05$, and the gauge resistance is $R = 350\ \Omega$. The applied force produces an axial strain of $\varepsilon = 200\ \mu\varepsilon$ on the two longitudinal gauges and $\varepsilon_t = -60\ \mu\varepsilon$ on the two transverse gauges. Find the bridge output voltage.

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (a) — 7 marks

**Working principle (4 marks):**
- An incremental optical encoder consists of a **light source (LED)**, a **code disc** with alternating transparent and opaque radial lines, and **two photodetectors** placed at angular positions offset by 90° of one slot pitch. A third photodetector at a single index slot generates one **index pulse** $Z$ per revolution.
- As the disc rotates, the photodetectors produce two square-wave trains $A$ and $B$ in **quadrature** (90° out of phase). The direction of rotation is inferred from whether $A$ leads or lags $B$.
- The pulses are conditioned by comparators and counted by an **up/down counter**. In ×1 mode one edge per slot is counted; in ×2 mode both edges of one channel; in ×4 mode all four edges of $A$ and $B$ are counted — quadrupling the resolution.
- The cumulative count is proportional to the **angular position** $\theta$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $N_\text{PPR}$ be the manufacturer's pulses per revolution (lines per disc) and $M$ the quadrature multiplier (1, 2, or 4).

Pulses per revolution in the digital domain:

$$
N_\text{cpr}} \;=\; M \cdot N_\text{PPR}}
$$

The shaft angle corresponding to a pulse count of $N$ is:

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\, N}{M\, N_\text{PPR}}} \quad [\text{rad}]
$$

The angular velocity is the change in count per sampling interval $\Delta t$:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,\Delta N}{\Delta t \cdot M \cdot N_\text{PPR}}} \quad [\text{rad/s}]\;}
$$

For example, an encoder with $N_\text{PPR}} = 2000$ in ×4 quadrature ($M = 4$) gives 8000 counts/rev. If $\Delta N = 80$ in $\Delta t = 5\ \text{ms}$:

$$
\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 80}{0.005 \cdot 8000} \;=\; \dfrac{160\pi}{40} \;=\; 4\pi \;\approx\; 12.57\ \text{rad/s} \;\approx\; 120\ \text{rpm}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (b) — 7 marks

**Theory (3 marks):** With four active gauges in a full bridge, two measuring $+\varepsilon$ and two measuring $-\varepsilon$ (Poisson bridge arrangement), the effective strain seen by the bridge is the **sum of the algebraic strains**:

$$
\varepsilon_\text{eff}} \;=\; \varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_t \;=\; 200 - (-60) \;=\; 260\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

**Bridge-output formula (3 marks):**

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; V_\text{exc}} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon_\text{eff}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
V_\text{out} \;=\; 10 \cdot 2.05 \cdot 260 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 10 \cdot 533 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_\text{out} \;=\; 5.33 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} \;=\; 5.33\ \text{mV}\;}
$$

**Sanity check (1 mark):** Rated output of a 2 mV/V cell is 2 mV per V per rated strain (typically 1000 µε). Here 5.33 mV / 10 V = 0.533 mV/V, and the strain is 260/1000 = 0.26 of rated. The output is therefore $2 \cdot 0.26 = 0.52$ mV/V — consistent with the calculation (small discrepancy due to gauge factor and Poisson correction).

---

### **QUESTION B-B  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the working principle of an **LVDT** with a neat diagram. Show that the output voltage is proportional to the displacement of the core and explain the role of the **phase-sensitive demodulator**.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] An **NTC thermistor** has $R_0 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298\ \text{K}$ and $\beta = 4000\ \text{K}$. The thermistor is connected in series with a $4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor across a **5 V** DC supply. The output is taken across the resistor. Find the output voltage and the sensitivity (in mV/°C) at $T = 50\ \text{°C} = 323\ \text{K}$.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (2 marks):** The LVDT consists of a single primary coil $P$ and two identical secondary coils $S_1, S_2$ wound on a hollow cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron core slides inside the former. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., the dotted end of $S_1$ is connected to the undotted end of $S_2$).

**Working (3 marks):** The primary is excited by an AC voltage $V_p \sin(\omega t)$. The two EMFs induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$ are $e_{S_1} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt}$ and $e_{S_2} = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}$, where $M_1, M_2$ are the mutual inductances. Because of the series-opposition connection, the net output is $e_\text{out} = e_{S_1} - e_{S_2}$. At the null (centre) position, $M_1 = M_2$, so $e_\text{out} = 0$. When the core moves by $x$ towards $S_1$, $M_1$ increases and $M_2$ decreases, so $e_\text{out}$ becomes positive and proportional to $x$. When the core moves by the same amount in the opposite direction, $M_2$ increases and $M_1$ decreases, so $e_\text{out}$ is *negative* and of the same magnitude. Hence:

$$
V_\text{out}} \;=\; K_\text{LVDT}} \cdot x \quad (\text{AC waveform, sign encoded in phase})
$$

**Phase-sensitive demodulator (2 marks):** The demodulator multiplies the LVDT output with a reference square wave synchronised to the primary excitation. The result is a DC voltage whose magnitude equals the AC amplitude and whose polarity equals the phase relationship. This DC voltage is then low-pass filtered to give a clean unipolar output proportional to $x$, with the sign preserved. Without the demodulator, one would only know the magnitude of $x$, not the direction.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the thermistor resistance at 50 °C (3 marks).**

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \;=\; 5000 \exp\!\left[4000 \left(\dfrac{1}{323} - \dfrac{1}{298}\right)\right]
$$

Compute the bracket:

$$
\dfrac{1}{323} - \dfrac{1}{298} \;=\; \dfrac{298 - 323}{323 \times 298} \;=\; \dfrac{-25}{96{,}254} \;\approx\; -2.597 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_T \;=\; 5000 \exp(-4000 \times 2.597 \times 10^{-4}) \;=\; 5000 \exp(-1.0388)
$$

$$
\exp(-1.0388) \;\approx\; 0.3539
$$

$$
R_T \;\approx\; 1769\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage across the 4.7 kΩ resistor (2 marks).**

$$
V_\text{out}} \;=\; V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_\text{series}}}{R_T + R_\text{series}}} \;=\; 5 \cdot \dfrac{4700}{1769 + 4700} \;=\; 5 \cdot \dfrac{4700}{6469}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_\text{out}} \;\approx\; 3.633\ \text{V}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity at 50 °C (2 marks).**

First find $dR_T / dT$:

$$
\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} \;=\; -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}\,R_T \;=\; -\dfrac{4000}{323^2} \times 1769
$$

$$
\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} \;\approx\; -0.03834 \times 1769 \;\approx\; -67.82\ \Omega/\text{K}
$$

The output voltage derivative:

$$
\dfrac{dV_\text{out}}}{dT} \;=\; -V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_\text{series}}}{(R_T + R_\text{series}})^2} \cdot \dfrac{dR_T}{dT}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dV_\text{out}}}{dT} \;=\; -5 \cdot \dfrac{4700}{(6469)^2} \cdot (-67.82)
$$

$$
(6469)^2 \;\approx\; 4.185 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dV_\text{out}}}{dT} \;\approx\; -5 \cdot 1.123 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (-67.82) \;\approx\; +0.0381\ \text{V/K}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S \;\approx\; 38.1\ \text{mV/°C}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The circuit's output *increases* with temperature because $R_T$ decreases, transferring more voltage to the series resistor. The sensitivity of 38 mV/°C is large enough to be read by a 10-bit ADC over a 0–5 V range (≈ 4.9 mV/LSB), so a 1 °C change is clearly resolvable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 ways students lose marks in Module 3 (Internal Sensors):**
>
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with precision with resolution** — the three are *not* the same. Examiners give zero marks for using the terms interchangeably. State the formula in every definition.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of strain** — strain $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless (µε means $10^{-6}$, not micro-strain "as a number"). The gauge-factor equation is $\Delta R/R = G_f \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon$ dimensionless.
> 3. **Not stating the excitation voltage in the bridge output formula** — the full-bridge output is $V_\text{exc} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, not just $G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
> 4. **Sign of strain in Poisson bridges** — transverse strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$, *not* $+\nu \varepsilon$. Getting this wrong can halve the answer.
> 5. **Encoder ×4 mode** — students often forget the ×4 quadrature multiplier, giving answers that are off by a factor of 4 (a 5-mark penalty).
> 6. **Phase of the LVDT** — failing to mention the **phase-sensitive demodulator** and the role of phase in direction sensing loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Thermistor temperature in K vs °C** — the β-equation uses **kelvin**. Plugging °C gives nonsensical results. Always convert: $T\,[\text{K}] = T\,[°\text{C}] + 273.15$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Module 3 — Internal Sensors:**

- **Definition:** Internal sensors measure *state variables within* a mechatronic system (position, velocity, acceleration, force, pressure, temperature, flow, current).
- **Position sensors:** Potentiometer (simple, contact, wear-limited), LVDT (contactless, AC, phase indicates direction), Optical encoder (digital, ×4 quadrature, no power-loss for absolute type).
- **Velocity sensors:** Tachogenerator (analog, $V = K\omega$), software tachometer (differentiate encoder position).
- **Acceleration sensors:** Mass–spring–damper with piezoelectric / piezoresistive / capacitive readout. Natural frequency $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ sets the usable bandwidth.
- **Force sensors:** Strain-gauge load cell in **Wheatstone bridge**. Equation $\Delta R/R = G_f \varepsilon$. Full bridge with Poisson arrangement gives $(1+\nu)$ gain.
- **Pressure sensors:** Deflect a diaphragm, sense the strain (or the change in capacitance). Center deflection $\propto P a^4 / D$.
- **Temperature sensors:** NTC ($R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$, $\beta \approx 4000$ K, $T$ in K), RTD ($R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ with $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}/°$C), Thermocouple (Seebeck, $V = S \Delta T$, $S \approx 40{-}60\ \mu$V/°C), Semiconductor (LM35: 10 mV/°C).
- **Static characteristics:** Sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, accuracy, resolution, drift, loading error.
- **Wheatstone bridge:** Balance condition $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$. Unbalanced output $V_\text{out} = V_\text{exc}(R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_4/(R_3+R_4))$.
- **Quadrature decoder:** 1×, 2×, 4× modes multiply effective resolution; direction from lead/lag of A over B.
- **Piezoelectric accelerometer:** $Q = d F = d m a$; charge amp output $V_\text{out} = -Q/C_f$.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** usable bandwidth of an accelerometer is approximately $f_n / 3$ for $\zeta = 0.7$.
- **Master trick:** for *any* resistive sensor, read it in a **Wheatstone bridge** to (i) linearise, (ii) temperature-compensate, and (iii) amplify the small signal by a factor of 4 (full-bridge over quarter-bridge).
- **Mechatronic-loop mantra:** "Reference → Controller → Actuator → Plant → **Internal Sensor** → Controller." Internal sensors *close* the loop.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** accuracy ≠ precision ≠ resolution; gauge factor needs *dimensionless* strain; bridge output needs $V_\text{exc}$; thermistor $\beta$-equation needs *kelvin*; encoder velocity needs the ×4 multiplier.
- **Industry-4.0 angle:** every internal sensor is a node on the IoT; the swarm of internal sensors constitutes the *digital twin* of the machine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Sensors and Microsensors, System Modelling - Basic Building Blocks of General Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** External Sensors and Microsensors, System Modelling - Basic Building Blocks of General Mechanical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 External Sensors – Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU Mechatronics (PEEBT863 – 2024 Scheme)**, an **External Sensor** is defined as a *transduction device* that interacts directly with the physical environment surrounding a mechatronic system to detect, measure, and convert a non-electrical physical quantity (such as displacement, force, pressure, temperature, or light intensity) into a usable electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency). The word "external" distinguishes these sensors from *internal sensors* (e.g., encoders mounted on a motor shaft) — external sensors measure quantities **outside** the actuator or controller, often at the end-effector or in the workspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** External sensors form the *perception layer* of any mechatronic system. Without them, closed-loop control is impossible. Common exam questions test **classification, principle of operation, and selection criteria** of these sensors.

### 1.2 Microsensors – Formal Definition

A **Microsensor** is a sensor fabricated using **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** technology, where the mechanical, electrical, and electronic components are miniaturized onto a single silicon chip — typically with feature sizes ranging from **1 µm to 100 µm**. They follow the same transduction principles as macroscale sensors but exploit silicon's **piezoresistive**, **capacitive**, **piezoelectric**, and **thermal** properties at microscopic scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept Callout:** Microsensors are NOT just "small sensors." They are fabricated using semiconductor batch-processing techniques (lithography, etching, doping), which makes them **cheap, mass-producible, and integrable with on-chip signal conditioning circuits.**

### 1.3 System Modelling – Basic Building Blocks of General Mechanical Systems

**System modelling** in mechatronics is the process of representing a physical mechanical system using idealized **building-block elements** whose mathematical behavior is well-defined. The three **canonical building blocks** of any general mechanical (translational or rotational) system are:

| Block | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass / Inertia** | $M$ or $J$ | Stores kinetic energy | $F = M \dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$ |
| **Spring / Stiffness** | $K$ | Stores potential energy | $F = Kx$ |
| **Damper / Friction** | $B$ | Dissipates energy | $F = B \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy for External Sensors:** Think of a human skin. Your skin has millions of "sensors" — touch (pressure), temperature (heat/cold), pain (force). Each converts a physical stimulus into nerve signals (electrical). External sensors in mechatronics are the *robot's skin* — they convert the physical world into electrical signals a controller can understand.
>
> **Real-world analogy for Microsensors:** Consider an ant. An ant can sense tiny vibrations, weights, and temperatures that a human cannot. A microsensor is the engineering equivalent — it can measure quantities invisible to bulkier instruments, integrated into a smartphone chip.
>
> **Real-world analogy for Mechanical Building Blocks:** Imagine pushing a shopping cart. The **cart's mass** resists your push (inertia), the **wheels' friction** slows it down (damper), and the **shock absorbers** in a car suspension store and release energy (spring). Every mechanical system in engineering is built from these three fundamental elements.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^{2}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Young's Modulus of Silicon (used in MEMS):** $E_{Si} \approx 170 \ \text{GPa}$
- **Density of Silicon:** $\rho_{Si} \approx 2330 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$
- **Piezoelectric coefficient of Quartz:** $d_{11} = 2.3 \ \text{pC/N}$
- **Sensitivity** units vary: $\text{V/V/mmHg}$ (pressure), $\text{mV/°C}$ (temperature), $\text{V/g}$ (acceleration)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of External Sensors

External sensors can be classified by the **physical quantity** they measure:

### 2.1.1 Position / Displacement Sensors
- **Potentiometric sensor:** Resistance varies linearly with slider position. Output: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$
- **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT):** Two secondary coils; output voltage is proportional to core displacement.
- **Capacitive sensor:** Capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ varies with plate separation $d$.
- **Eddy current sensor:** Measures proximity through induced currents in a target.
- **Optical encoder (linear):** Light pulses through a slotted scale.

### 2.1.2 Velocity Sensors
- **Tachogenerator:** DC generator producing voltage proportional to rotational speed: $V = K_{t} \omega$.
- **Electromagnetic pickup:** Coil in magnetic field generates EMF proportional to velocity.

### 2.1.3 Force / Torque Sensors
- **Strain gauge load cell:** Resistance changes with strain: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ where $G$ is the gauge factor (≈2.0 for metal, ≈100 for semiconductor).
- **Piezoelectric force sensor:** Charge generated: $Q = d \cdot F$ (where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge constant).

### 2.1.4 Pressure Sensors
- **Diaphragm-type:** Deflection of a thin plate under pressure measured via strain gauges or capacitive plates.
- **Piezoresistive pressure sensor:** Silicon diaphragm with diffused resistors — change in resistance under stress.

### 2.1.5 Temperature Sensors
- **Thermocouple:** $V \approx S \cdot \Delta T$ where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient (40–60 µV/°C for Type K).
- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$
- **Thermistor:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$

### 2.1.6 Light / Optical Sensors
- **Photodiode:** Reverse current proportional to light intensity.
- **Phototransistor:** Amplified photocurrent.
- **CCD/CMOS image sensors:** Array-based for vision systems.

## 2.2 Microsensors – Operating Principles

Microsensors exploit four primary transduction principles at the microscale:

1. **Piezoresistive:** Stress changes resistivity of doped silicon. *Used in:* pressure sensors, accelerometers.
2. **Capacitive:** Displacement changes capacitance between micro-fabricated comb fingers. *Used in:* accelerometers (ADXL series), gyroscopes.
3. **Piezoelectric:** Mechanical strain generates charge. *Used in:* microphones, ultrasonic sensors.
4. **Thermal:** Heat transfer changes temperature-dependent resistance. *Used in:* flow sensors, infrared detectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Silicon?** Silicon is the **defacto** MEMS material because: (1) it has excellent mechanical properties (high Young's modulus, no fatigue), (2) it can be batch-fabricated using IC processes, and (3) signal conditioning circuitry can be integrated on the same chip.

## 2.3 Basic Building Blocks – Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Mass Element (Translational)
- **Physical meaning:** Stores kinetic energy, resists acceleration change.
- **Equation:** $F(t) = M \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$
- **Energy stored:** $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} M \left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2}$
- **Analogy:** Inertia is a system's "reluctance to change motion."

### 2.3.2 Spring Element (Translational)
- **Physical meaning:** Stores potential energy, resists displacement.
- **Equation:** $F(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ (Hooke's Law)
- **Energy stored:** $E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K x^{2}$
- **Types:** Linear spring (constant $K$), nonlinear spring $F = K x + K_3 x^{3}$.

### 2.3.3 Damper Element (Translational)
- **Physical meaning:** Dissipates energy as heat, resists velocity.
- **Equation:** $F(t) = B \cdot \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ (viscous damping)
- **Power dissipated:** $P = B \left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2}$
- **Types:** Viscous (linear in velocity), Coulomb (constant friction), aerodynamic (proportional to $v^2$).

### 2.3.4 Rotational Equivalents
| Translational | Rotational Equivalent | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Force $F$ | Torque $T$ | $T = J \ddot{\theta}$ |
| Mass $M$ | Moment of Inertia $J$ | $T = J \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$ |
| Spring $K$ | Torsional spring $K_t$ | $T = K_t \theta$ |
| Damper $B$ | Rotational damper $B_t$ | $T = B_t \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ |

### 2.3.5 Mechanical Coupling Elements
- **Lever (mechanical amplifier):** $F_1 L_1 = F_2 L_2$ — torque balance.
- **Pulleys and belts:** Velocity ratio $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$
- **Gears:** $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ where $N$ is number of teeth.
- **Cam-follower:** Converts rotational motion to translational motion with prescribed profile.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** Memorize this table for KTU ESE. Many 3-mark and 14-mark questions are solved directly from these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd law | $F = M \ddot{x}$ | N | Mass element EOM |
| 2 | Hooke's law | $F = Kx$ | N | Spring element |
| 3 | Viscous damping | $F = B \dot{x}$ | N | Damper element |
| 4 | Rotational inertia | $T = J \ddot{\theta}$ | N·m | Rotational EOM |
| 5 | Torsional spring | $T = K_t \theta$ | N·m | Rotational spring |
| 6 | Strain gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \varepsilon$ | — | Force/pressure |
| 7 | Capacitive sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Displacement |
| 8 | Piezoelectric | $Q = d \cdot F$ | C | Force sensor |
| 9 | RTD | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | Ω | Temperature |
| 10 | LVDT output | $V_{out} \propto x$ | V | Position |
| 11 | Tachogenerator | $V = K_t \omega$ | V | Velocity |
| 12 | Gear ratio | $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | rad/s | Mechanical linkage |
| 13 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$ | — | 2nd-order system |
| 14 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ | rad/s | 2nd-order system |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** MEMS accelerometers trigger airbags within 15 ms of crash detection.
- **Medical:** Microsensors in catheters measure blood pressure (1 mm diameter).
- **Manufacturing:** External force/torque sensors in robotic arms enable precision assembly.
- **Consumer electronics:** MEMS gyroscopes stabilize camera images in smartphones.
- **Building modelling:** Mass-spring-damper models predict earthquake response of skyscrapers.
- **Vehicle suspension:** Quarter-car model with $M$, $K$, $B$ predicts ride comfort.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion for a Mass-Spring-Damper System

Consider a mass $M$ attached to a wall through a spring $K$ and damper $B$ in parallel, acted upon by an external force $F(t)$. Displacement of the mass from equilibrium is $x(t)$.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram (FBD)
Forces acting on mass $M$:
- Applied force: $F(t)$ (assumed positive in $+x$ direction)
- Spring force: $-Kx$ (restoring, opposite to displacement)
- Damping force: $-B\dot{x}$ (opposite to velocity)

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
By Newton's second law, sum of forces equals mass times acceleration:

$$F(t) - Kx(t) - B\dot{x}(t) = M\ddot{x}(t)$$

### Step 3: Rearrange into Standard Form
Move all terms to LHS:

$$M\ddot{x}(t) + B\dot{x}(t) + Kx(t) = F(t)$$

### Step 4: Define Standard Parameters
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$
- **Undamped natural frequency in Hz:** $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$

### Step 5: Normalized Form
Dividing through by $M$:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + \dfrac{B}{M}\dot{x}(t) + \dfrac{K}{M}x(t) = \dfrac{1}{M}F(t)$$

Substituting standard parameters:

$$\ddot{x}(t) + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x}(t) + \omega_n^{2} x(t) = \dfrac{1}{M}F(t)$$

### Step 6: Transfer Function (Taking Laplace Transform with zero initial conditions)

$$M s^{2} X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

$$(Ms^{2} + Bs + K) X(s) = F(s)$$

$$\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$$

### Step 7: Damping Cases
- **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** Oscillatory decay, $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$
- **Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$):** Fastest return without oscillation
- **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** Slow exponential return, no oscillation
- **Undamped ($\zeta = 0$):** Pure oscillation at $\omega_n$

## 3.2 Derivation: Force-Current Analogy (Through Analogy)

In the **Force-Current (Through) Analogy**, mechanical and electrical quantities map as:

| Mechanical | Electrical |
|---|---|
| Force $F$ | Current $i$ |
| Velocity $\dot{x}$ | Voltage $v$ |
| Mass $M$ | Capacitance $C$ |
| Damper $B$ | Conductance $1/R$ (or $G$) |
| Spring $K$ | Inverse Inductance $1/L$ |

**Proof:** For a mass $M$, $F = M\ddot{x}$. For a capacitor, $i = C\dot{v}$. Differentiate force equation w.r.t. time:

$$\dot{F} = M\dddot{x}$$

In the analogy, $F \leftrightarrow i$ and $\dot{x} \leftrightarrow v$:

$$\dot{F} = M\ddot{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{i} = C\ddot{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i = C\dot{v}$$

This is exactly the **capacitor equation** with $M \leftrightarrow C$. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Deriving EOM for Common Mechanical Configurations

### 3.3.1 Series Connection of Spring and Damper with Mass

For mass $M$ with spring $K$ and damper $B$ in series between wall and mass:

The displacement of mass is $x$. The spring deflection is $x_1$ and damper deflection is $x_2$ where $x_1 + x_2 = x$.

Forces are equal through series: $F_{spring} = F_{damper} = F_M$

$$M\ddot{x} = -K x_1 = -B\dot{x}_2$$

Solving: $M\ddot{x} + \dfrac{KB}{K+B}\dot{x} + \dfrac{KB}{K+B} \cdot \text{(coupled term)} = F(t)$

(Detailed solution requires Laplace or state-space method — out of scope here, but the key insight is that **stiffness and damping combine non-trivially in series**.)

### 3.3.2 Rotational System: Disc with Torsional Spring and Damper

For a rotating disc of inertia $J$ attached to a torsional spring $K_t$ and rotational damper $B_t$:

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B_t\dot{\theta}(t) + K_t \theta(t) = T(t)$$

This has the **same mathematical form** as the translational case — only the variable is $\theta$ and the parameters are rotational.

## 3.4 Numerical Example – Worked Out

**Problem:** A mass of $M = 10 \text{ kg}$ is attached to a spring of stiffness $K = 1000 \text{ N/m}$ and a damper with damping coefficient $B = 50 \text{ N·s/m}$. Find the equation of motion, natural frequency, and damping ratio.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Write the standard form:

$$10\ddot{x} + 50\dot{x} + 1000 x = F(t)$$

**Step 2:** Calculate natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{10}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}} = \dfrac{50}{2\sqrt{1000 \times 10}} = \dfrac{50}{2 \times 100} = \dfrac{50}{200} = 0.25$$

**Step 4:** Interpret:
- Since $\zeta = 0.25 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** → will oscillate before settling.
- Damped natural frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 10\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 10 \times 0.9682 = 9.682 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Response to unit step input — settling time (2% criterion):

$$t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.25 \times 10} = 1.6 \text{ s}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Define an external sensor. List any four examples with the physical quantity each one measures.**

**Model Answer:**
An external sensor is a device that interfaces a mechatronic system with its external environment, converting a non-electrical physical quantity into a measurable electrical signal.

Four examples:
1. **LVDT** – measures linear displacement
2. **Thermocouple** – measures temperature
3. **Strain gauge load cell** – measures force
4. **Piezoelectric accelerometer** – measures acceleration/vibration

*(Full credit requires brief explanation of transduction principle)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Differentiate between macroscale sensors and microsensors (MEMS sensors) in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Macroscale Sensor | MEMS Microsensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Centimeter to meter scale | Micron to millimeter scale |
| **Fabrication** | Discrete mechanical assembly | Semiconductor batch processing (lithography, etching) |
| **Cost & Volume** | Expensive, low volume | Cheap, high volume (millions per wafer) |
| **Integration** | External signal conditioning | On-chip integrated circuitry |
| **Examples** | Industrial LVDT, load cell | ADXL345 accelerometer, MPX pressure sensor |

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse **mass** with **weight** — mass is in kg, weight (force) is in N. Always use $F = Ma$, never $F = Mg$ unless gravity is the applied force.
> - When deriving EOM, **draw FBD first** and label all forces with directions. Examiners deduct 2 marks if FBD is missing.
> - For damping ratio, students often forget to take the **square root** of $KM$. Recheck: $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$, NOT $\dfrac{B}{2KM}$.
> - For rotational systems, use $J$ (moment of inertia) and $K_t$ (torsional stiffness) — substituting $M$ and $K$ loses marks.
> - In numerical problems, **state units clearly** at every step.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**
**Q: With neat block diagrams, classify the basic building blocks of general mechanical systems. Derive the equation of motion for a translational mass-spring-damper system acted upon by an external force $F(t)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the three basic building blocks:
1. **Mass $M$** — stores kinetic energy, $F = M\ddot{x}$
2. **Spring $K$** — stores potential energy, $F = Kx$
3. **Damper $B$** — dissipates energy, $F = B\dot{x}$

**Step 2:** Free body diagram — consider mass $M$ connected to a fixed wall through spring $K$ and damper $B$ in parallel. Force $F(t)$ applied in $+x$ direction.

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's second law. Sum of forces on mass:

$$F(t) - F_{spring} - F_{damper} = M\ddot{x}(t)$$

Substituting:

$$F(t) - Kx(t) - B\dot{x}(t) = M\ddot{x}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$M\ddot{x}(t) + B\dot{x}(t) + Kx(t) = F(t)$$

**Step 5:** This is a 2nd-order linear ODE. Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$(Ms^{2} + Bs + K)X(s) = F(s)$$

Transfer function:

$$\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$$

**Step 6:** Define natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$.

Normalized form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^{2}x = \dfrac{1}{M}F(t)$$

---

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**
**Q: A mass of $5 \text{ kg}$ is supported by a spring of stiffness $2000 \text{ N/m}$ and a damper of coefficient $100 \text{ N·s/m}$. A force of $50 \text{ N}$ is suddenly applied. Calculate: (i) natural frequency, (ii) damping ratio, (iii) damped natural frequency, and (iv) state the type of damping.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $M = 5 \text{ kg}$, $K = 2000 \text{ N/m}$, $B = 100 \text{ N·s/m}$, $F = 50 \text{ N}$

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2000}{5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}} = \dfrac{100}{2\sqrt{2000 \times 5}} = \dfrac{100}{2 \times 100} = \dfrac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

**(iii) Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.25} = 20\sqrt{0.75} = 20 \times 0.8660 = 17.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iv) Type of damping:** Since $\zeta = 0.5 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it will oscillate with exponentially decaying amplitude.

**Steady-state displacement under constant 50 N:**

$$x_{ss} = \dfrac{F}{K} = \dfrac{50}{2000} = 0.025 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**
**Q: Explain the working principle of MEMS microsensors. Describe in detail the piezoresistive and capacitive transduction mechanisms with suitable applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**MEMS Overview:**
Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) integrate mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. The sensing element and signal conditioning are co-fabricated.

**Piezoresistive Transduction:**
- **Principle:** The electrical resistivity of doped silicon changes when mechanical stress is applied.
- **Mathematical relation:** $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi \sigma$ where $\pi$ is the piezoresistive coefficient and $\sigma$ is applied stress.
- **Construction:** A thin silicon diaphragm (typically 10–50 µm thick) is etched from a silicon wafer. Piezoresistors are diffused into the diaphragm surface in a Wheatstone bridge configuration.
- **Application:** Pressure sensors (e.g., Motorola MPX series), accelerometers, force sensors.
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity, simple readout electronics, DC response.
- **Disadvantages:** Temperature sensitivity, larger power consumption than capacitive.

**Capacitive Transduction:**
- **Principle:** Displacement of a movable plate changes capacitance:

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

- **Construction:** Comb-finger structures with one fixed and one movable set of fingers. Acceleration or pressure deflects the movable structure, changing $d$ or overlap area $A$.
- **Application:** MEMS accelerometers (Analog Devices ADXL345), gyroscopes, microphones.
- **Advantages:** Low power, low temperature sensitivity, good DC stability.
- **Disadvantages:** Sensitive to EMI, requires complex readout ASIC.

**Real-world Example:** The ADXL345 accelerometer in smartphones uses **interdigitated comb fingers** with differential capacitive sensing — acceleration deflects a proof mass, changing capacitance in a fully differential bridge.

---

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**
**Q: For a rotational mechanical system consisting of a disc with moment of inertia $J = 0.5 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$, torsional stiffness $K_t = 200 \text{ N·m/rad}$, and rotational damping $B_t = 20 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$, derive the equation of motion and determine the natural frequency and damping ratio.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Free body diagram of disc — applied torque $T(t)$, restoring torque from spring $-K_t \theta$, damping torque $-B_t \dot{\theta}$.

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's 2nd law for rotation:

$$T(t) - K_t \theta(t) - B_t \dot{\theta}(t) = J\ddot{\theta}(t)$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B_t \dot{\theta}(t) + K_t \theta(t) = T(t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:

$$0.5\ddot{\theta}(t) + 20\dot{\theta}(t) + 200\theta(t) = T(t)$$

**Step 5:** Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t}{J}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200}{0.5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6:** Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{B_t}{2\sqrt{K_t J}} = \dfrac{20}{2\sqrt{200 \times 0.5}} = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 10} = \dfrac{20}{20} = 1.0$$

**Step 7:** Interpret: $\zeta = 1.0$ indicates **critically damped** system — fastest return to equilibrium without oscillation.

**Transfer function:**

$$\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Js^{2} + B_t s + K_t} = \dfrac{1}{0.5s^{2} + 20s + 200}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU ESE – Module 3:**

### External Sensors
- [ ] External sensor = "skin of the mechatronic system" (measures outside the actuator).
- [ ] **LVDT** works on mutual induction — output proportional to core displacement.
- [ ] **Strain gauge equation:** $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G\varepsilon$ — gauge factor $G$ is critical.
- [ ] **Thermocouple** uses Seebeck effect; **RTD** uses positive temperature coefficient; **Thermistor** uses exponential relation.
- [ ] **Tachogenerator** output $V = K_t \omega$ — used for rotational velocity.
- [ ] **Capacitive sensor** formula $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — change in $d$, $A$, or $\varepsilon_r$ causes measurable $\Delta C$.

### Microsensors (MEMS)
- [ ] MEMS = **M**icro-**E**lectro-**M**echanical **S**ystems — feature size 1–100 µm.
- [ ] Four primary transduction mechanisms: **Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Thermal**.
- [ ] Silicon is the preferred substrate: high Young's modulus, batch fabrication, IC compatibility.
- [ ] **Piezoresistive** → pressure sensors; **Capacitive** → accelerometers; **Piezoelectric** → microphones; **Thermal** → flow sensors.
- [ ] Advantages: small size, low cost, low power, on-chip integration.

### Mechanical System Building Blocks
- [ ] Three canonical elements: **Mass ($M$)**, **Spring ($K$)**, **Damper ($B$)**.
- [ ] **Mass equation:** $F = M\ddot{x}$ (Newton's 2nd law).
- [ ] **Spring equation:** $F = Kx$ (Hooke's law).
- [ ] **Damper equation:** $F = B\dot{x}$ (viscous damping).
- [ ] **Rotational equivalents:** $J, K_t, B_t$ replace $M, K, B$; $\theta$ replaces $x$; $T$ replaces $F$.
- [ ] Standard 2nd-order EOM: $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t)$.
- [ ] **Transfer function:** $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$.
- [ ] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ — memorize the formula.
- [ ] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$ — square root of $KM$ in denominator.
- [ ] **Underdamped** if $\zeta < 1$, **Critically damped** if $\zeta = 1$, **Overdamped** if $\zeta > 1$.
- [ ] Damped natural frequency: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$ (valid only when $\zeta < 1$).

### Force-Current (Through) Analogy
- [ ] $F \leftrightarrow i$ (through variable)
- [ ] $M \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$ (or $G$), $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$
- [ ] $\dot{x} \leftrightarrow v$ (across variable)

### KTU Exam Tips
- [ ] **Always draw FBD** before writing EOM — earns 2 marks directly.
- [ ] Show all substitutions and **carry units** through calculations.
- [ ] State assumptions clearly (linearity, no friction, ideal spring, etc.).
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, allocate ~3 marks for FBD, ~6 marks for derivation, ~3 marks for parameters, ~2 marks for interpretation.
- [ ] For sensor questions, mention **transduction principle, range, sensitivity, and one application**.
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Using $W = Mg$ (weight) in EOM when the question gives applied force $F$ — read the question carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Mechatronics, the **Electrical Subsystem** refers to the integrated set of electrical and electronic components that handle **power distribution, signal conditioning, actuation, and control feedback** between the mechanical domain and the digital/controller domain. As per the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Mechatronics syllabus (Module 3), this topic explicitly covers **electrical actuators, solenoids, relays, DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, power supply units, signal conditioning circuits, and protective devices** that bridge microcontroller outputs with real-world mechanical motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEBT863 - Module 3 - Electrical):** The electrical domain in Mechatronics is the *translator layer* — it converts low-power digital logic signals (typically **0 V to 5 V** or **0 V to 3.3 V**) into high-power mechanical motion (torques from **mN·m to kN·m**) and vice-versa through sensors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System

Imagine the human body:

| Human Body Part | Mechatronic Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | Microcontroller / PLC | Decision making |
| Nerves | Wires, PCB tracks, signal lines | Signal transmission |
| Muscles | DC motors, solenoids, servo motors | Actuation (movement) |
| Skin (touch sensors) | Limit switches, encoders, LVDTs | Feedback |
| Heart | Power Supply Unit (PSU) | Energy circulation |
| Spinal reflexes | Relay logic, contactors | Pre-processing |

> **Plain English Intuition:** The *Electrical* part of Mechatronics is essentially the **nervous + muscular system** of the machine. Without it, the "brain" (microcontroller) can think all it wants, but nothing will move.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards govern the electrical subsystem:

- **Standard Logic Voltage (TTL):** $V_{OH} = \mathbf{5\,V}$, $V_{OL} = \mathbf{0\,V}$
- **Standard Logic Voltage (CMOS 3.3 V):** $V_{OH} = \mathbf{3.3\,V}$
- **Industrial Standard Voltage:** $\mathbf{24\,V\,DC}$ (sensors), $\mathbf{230\,V\,AC}$ / $\mathbf{415\,V\,AC}$ (actuators)
- **Electromagnetic constant:** $\mu_0 = \mathbf{4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,H/m}$
- **Standard Motor Speeds:** $\mathbf{1500\,RPM}$ (4-pole, 50 Hz), $\mathbf{3000\,RPM}$ (2-pole, 50 Hz)
- **Step Angle Standards:** $\mathbf{1.8°}$ (200 steps/rev), $\mathbf{0.72°}$ (500 steps/rev)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** KTU problem statements routinely specify components like *"a 24 V DC motor"* or *"a 1.8° stepper motor"* — recognizing these standards instantly is half the battle won in numerical problems.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Electrical Subsystems in Mechatronics

The electrical subsystem is hierarchically classified into four functional blocks:

1. **Power Electronics Block** — Transformers, rectifiers, regulators, SMPS, drivers.
2. **Signal Conditioning Block** — Amplifiers, filters, A/D-D/A converters, optocouplers.
3. **Actuation Block** — DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, solenoids, relays.
4. **Protection Block** — Fuses, MCBs, ELCBs, MOVs, free-wheeling diodes, snubbers.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to *"explain the electrical subsystem of a mechatronic system"*, always structure your answer using these **four hierarchical blocks**. Examiners reward logical classification heavily.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solenoids — The Simplest Linear Electrical Actuator

### Operating Principle
A solenoid is a helical coil of insulated copper wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger (armature). When current flows through the coil, it generates a magnetic field that pulls the plunger along the coil axis, producing **linear mechanical motion**.

### Why It Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. Current $I$ flowing through $N$ turns creates magnetomotive force (MMF): $\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I$ (in Ampere-turns, **A·t**).
2. The MMF drives flux $\Phi$ through the air gap: $\Phi = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}}$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is the magnetic reluctance.
3. The flux produces an attractive force on the plunger: $F \propto \Phi^2$.
4. Plunger moves to minimize the air gap, hence minimizing reluctance (seeking minimum energy).

### Solenoid Force Equation

The force generated by a solenoid pulling a plunger across a magnetic circuit is given by:

$$
F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \, g^2}
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Force in Newtons (**N**)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,H/m$ (permeability of free space)
- $N$ = Number of turns
- $I$ = Coil current in Amperes (**A**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of plunger in $m^2$
- $g$ = Air gap length in meters (**m**)

> [!NOTE]
> The **inverse-square dependence on air gap** is the most critical property. This is why solenoids have a *very strong pull at small gaps* and lose force rapidly as the gap opens — a frequent viva question.

---

## 2.2 DC Motors — The Workhorse of Mechatronics

### Construction
A DC motor consists of:
- **Stator** — field windings or permanent magnets providing the magnetic field $B$.
- **Rotor (Armature)** — coil winding rotating in the magnetic field.
- **Commutator + Brushes** — mechanical rectifier that reverses current direction every half-rotation.

### Operating Principle (Lorentz Force)
A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = B \cdot I \cdot L \sin\theta$. Summing up the contributions around the armature produces a continuous torque.

### Key Equations of DC Motor

The governing electrical and mechanical equations are:

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

$$
E_b = K_e \, \omega
$$

$$
T = K_t \, I_a
$$

$$
T - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega
$$

Where:
- $V_a$ = Armature voltage (V)
- $E_b$ = Back-EMF (V)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (A)
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance ($\Omega$)
- $K_e$ = Voltage constant (V·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)
- $T$ = Motor torque (N·m)
- $T_L$ = Load torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $B$ = Viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In SI units, $K_e = K_t$. This equality appears almost every year in KTU derivations. Memorize it!

---

## 2.3 Stepper Motors — Open-Loop Precision Actuators

### Operating Principle
A stepper motor rotates in discrete **angular steps** synchronized with input pulses. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed **step angle** $\theta_s$.

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot p}
$$

Where:
- $\theta_s$ = Step angle
- $N_r$ = Number of rotor teeth
- $p$ = Number of stator pole pairs (phases)

### Types of Stepper Motors (Board Exam Favorite)
- **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper** — No permanent magnet; rotor aligns to minimum reluctance.
- **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper** — Rotor has permanent magnets; provides holding torque at zero current.
- **Hybrid Stepper** — Combines VR and PM; most common (1.8° standard).

---

## 2.4 Servo Motors — Closed-Loop Precision Actuators

Unlike stepper motors, servo motors use **continuous position feedback** (typically via an internal encoder or potentiometer). They are controlled by a **PWM signal** whose pulse width (typically **1 ms to 2 ms** at **50 Hz**) determines the target shaft position.

$$
\theta_{shaft} = \theta_{min} + \frac{(t_p - 1.0)}{(2.0 - 1.0)} \times \theta_{range}
$$

Where:
- $t_p$ = Pulse width in milliseconds
- $\theta_{range}$ = Total angular range (usually $180°$)
- $\theta_{min}$ = Minimum angle (usually $0°$)

---

## 2.5 Relays — Electrical Isolation Switches

A relay uses a low-power control circuit to switch a high-power load through **electromagnetic induction**. The coil energizes, the armature pivots, and contacts close.

### Relay Switching Time Equation

$$
t_{switch} = t_{delay} + t_{rise} + t_{fall}
$$

Typical values: $t_{switch} \approx \mathbf{5\,ms}$ to $\mathbf{15\,ms}$ for general-purpose relays.

> [!TIP]
> **Solid-State Relays (SSRs)** use TRIACs or MOSFETs instead of mechanical contacts — switching time drops to $\mathbf{<1\,ms}$ with no contact bounce. A favorite viva comparison question.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$ | Solenoid pull force | N |
| 2 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | DC motor armature loop | V |
| 3 | $E_b = K_e \, \omega$ | Back-EMF | V |
| 4 | $T = K_t \, I_a$ | Motor torque | N·m |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 6 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Motor efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot p}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees |
| 8 | $f_{step,max} = \dfrac{V}{L \cdot \Delta I}$ | Max step rate | steps/s |
| 9 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 10 | $\tau_{RC} = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant | s |
| 11 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right)$ | Non-inverting amplifier gain | V |
| 12 | $I_{coil,holding} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Relay/solenoid holding current | A |

> **Note:** All formulas above are **standard, derivable, and frequently tested** in KTU 2024 scheme papers.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Applications — Where This Matters

The electrical subsystem is the heart of:
- **Robotics:** Servo motors drive robotic arm joints (e.g., 6-DOF humanoid).
- **CNC Machines:** Stepper motors drive linear axes with $\pm 0.01\,mm$ precision.
- **Automotive (EVs):** BLDC motors with peak torques of $\mathbf{>300\,N\cdot m}$.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs switch relays controlling solenoid valves for pneumatic/hydraulic actuators.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Tiny DC motors run camera autofocus, vibration, and disk drives.
- **Aerospace:** Solenoid-actuated fuel valves and landing gear locks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Insight:** Modern mechatronic systems increasingly use **Brushless DC (BLDC)** motors because they eliminate brush wear — extending operational life from $\mathbf{1{,}000\,hours}$ (brushed) to over $\mathbf{20{,}000\,hours}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC Motor Steady-State Torque-Speed Characteristic

### Objective
Derive the linear relationship between motor torque $T$ and angular speed $\omega$ under steady-state conditions.

### Step 1: Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + E_b
$$

**Explanation:** The applied armature voltage $V_a$ drops across the armature resistance $R_a$ (ohmic drop) and the back-EMF $E_b$ (induced EMF opposing $V_a$).

### Step 2: Substitute the back-EMF equation

Since $E_b = K_e \, \omega$, we get:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \, \omega
$$

### Step 3: Solve for armature current $I_a$

$$
I_a = \frac{V_a - K_e \, \omega}{R_a}
$$

**Explanation:** Rearranging to isolate $I_a$. This tells us current decreases linearly as speed increases.

### Step 4: Substitute $I_a$ into the torque equation $T = K_t I_a$

$$
T = K_t \cdot \frac{V_a - K_e \, \omega}{R_a}
$$

### Step 5: Expand and group terms

$$
T = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \, \omega
$$

### Step 6: Identify the slope and intercept

Comparing with the straight-line form $T = T_{stall} - m \cdot \omega$:

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a}
$$

$$
m = \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a}
$$

### Step 7: Define no-load speed

At no-load, $T = 0$:

$$
\omega_{no-load} = \frac{V_a}{K_e}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\, T = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \, \omega \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** This derivation appears in nearly every KTU Mechatronics past paper. The slope $\dfrac{K_t K_e}{R_a}$ and intercept $\dfrac{K_t V_a}{R_a}$ are *golden numbers* for follow-up numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stepper Motor Step Angle from Construction

### Objective
Show that the step angle depends on rotor teeth and stator pole configuration.

### Step 1: One full rotation in electrical degrees
For a stepper motor, one complete electrical cycle corresponds to the rotor advancing by one tooth pitch:

$$
1 \text{ mechanical revolution} = N_r \text{ tooth pitches}
$$

### Step 2: For a stator with $p$ pole pairs (phases energized alternately)
Each excitation sequence advances the rotor by:

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot p}
$$

### Step 3: Verification with standard values
For a hybrid stepper with $N_r = 50$ and $p = 4$:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8°
$$

This matches the industry-standard 1.8° stepper — **derivation confirmed**.

### Step 4: Steps per revolution

$$
\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Shortcut for KTU:** Whenever you see *"1.8° stepper"*, immediately write **200 steps/rev**. Whenever you see *"0.72°"*, write **500 steps/rev**. No need to re-derive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transfer Function of Armature-Controlled DC Motor

### Objective
Derive the open-loop transfer function $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)}$ for the armature-controlled DC motor.

### Step 1: Write electrical loop equation in Laplace domain

$$
V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \, I_a(s) + E_b(s)
$$

$$
E_b(s) = K_e \, \omega(s)
$$

### Step 2: Write mechanical equation in Laplace domain

$$
T(s) = K_t \, I_a(s)
$$

$$
T(s) - T_L(s) = (Js + B) \, \omega(s)
$$

### Step 3: Combine to eliminate $I_a(s)$

From electrical loop:

$$
I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_e \, \omega(s)}{R_a + L_a s}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into mechanical equation (assuming $T_L = 0$ for simplicity)

$$
K_t \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_e \, \omega(s)}{R_a + L_a s} = (Js + B) \, \omega(s)
$$

### Step 5: Expand and collect $\omega(s)$ terms

$$
K_t \, V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) \, \omega(s) + K_t K_e \, \omega(s)
$$

### Step 6: Factor $\omega(s)$ on RHS

$$
K_t \, V_a(s) = \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e \right] \, \omega(s)
$$

### Step 7: Take the ratio to form the transfer function

$$
\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_e)} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is a **second-order system**. The denominator polynomial gives the characteristic equation whose roots determine the motor's transient response — a frequent topic in KTU control system overlap questions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solenoid Force from Energy Method

### Objective
Derive solenoid force using the principle of virtual work.

### Step 1: Energy stored in magnetic field

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2
$$

### Step 2: Inductance varies with gap

For a magnetic circuit with air gap $g$:

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}
$$

### Step 3: Force as negative gradient of co-energy

For constant current (current-controlled solenoid):

$$
F = +\frac{\partial W}{\partial g}\bigg|_{I = const} = \frac{\partial}{\partial g}\left( \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g} \cdot I^2 \right)
$$

### Step 4: Differentiate

$$
F = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 g^2}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the force acts to *reduce* the gap (pulling the plunger in).

$$
\boxed{\, \vert F \vert = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 g^2} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-verification:** This matches the force equation quoted in Section 2.1. The energy method is a more rigorous derivation and earns full marks in KTU board evaluations.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Parameter | DC Motor | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor | Solenoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motion type | Continuous rotary | Discrete steps | Continuous rotary | Linear |
| Feedback | Optional | Open-loop | Mandatory (closed-loop) | Open-loop |
| Holding torque at zero | No | Yes (PM/Hybrid) | Yes (encoder) | Yes (if energized) |
| Typical efficiency | 60–80% | 30–60% | 70–90% | N/A |
| Positioning accuracy | Depends on encoder | $\pm 1$ step ($\pm 0.01°$ for microstep) | $\pm 1$ encoder count | Binary (in/out) |
| Driver complexity | Medium | High (sequence logic) | High (PID + PWM) | Very low |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Very low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Mechatronic Electrical Subsystem

The following block diagram illustrates how the electrical components interact in a typical closed-loop mechatronic system (e.g., a robotic arm joint position control):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller Unit] -->|PWM Signal 3.3V| B[Signal Conditioner]
    B -->|Isolated 5V Logic| C[Motor Driver IC]
    C -->|High Current Drive 24V| D[DC Servo Motor]
    D -->|Mechanical Shaft Rotation| E[Mechanical Load]
    D -->|Encoder Feedback| F[Optical Encoder]
    F -->|Quadrature Pulses| G[Decoder and Counter]
    G -->|Position Data| A
    P[24V DC Power Supply] -->|Buck Regulator| B
    P -->|Direct 24V Bus| C
    H[Relay Module] -->|230V AC Switching| I[Solenoid Valve]
    J[Safety Fuse 5A] --> P
```

> **Reading guide:** The arrows show the bidirectional flow of power and information. The microcontroller sends low-voltage commands (left side), the driver amplifies these to high-current motor drives (center), and the encoder closes the loop by feeding position back (bottom arc).

## 4.2 Stepper Motor Drive — Multi-Phase Excitation Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PhaseA
    PhaseA --> PhaseAB: Step Pulse 1
    PhaseAB --> PhaseB: Step Pulse 2
    PhaseB --> PhaseBC: Step Pulse 3
    PhaseBC --> PhaseC: Step Pulse 4
    PhaseC --> PhaseCD: Step Pulse 5
    PhaseCD --> PhaseD: Step Pulse 6
    PhaseD --> PhaseDA: Step Pulse 7
    PhaseDA --> PhaseA: Step Pulse 8
    PhaseA --> [*]: Motor Disabled
```

> **Reading guide:** Each transition corresponds to one step pulse. The rotor advances by exactly one step angle every transition. Reversing the pulse sequence makes the motor rotate in the opposite direction.

## 4.3 Relay Switching — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller GPIO 5V] --> B[Transistor Switch BC547]
    B -->|Base Current 5mA| C[Relay Coil 12V 100mA]
    C -->|Magnetic Field| D[Armature Pivots]
    D -->|NO Contact Closes| E[Load 230V Lamp]
    F[Flyback Diode 1N4007] -->|Reverse EMF Clamp| C
    G[Free Wheeling Path] --> F
```

> **Reading guide:** When the GPIO goes HIGH, the transistor saturates, energizing the coil, which pulls the armature, closing the normally-open (NO) contact and switching ON the high-voltage load. The flyback diode protects the transistor from inductive kickback.

## 4.4 Power Supply Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    AC[230V AC Mains Input] --> XFMR[Step Down Transformer 230 to 24V]
    XFMR --> RECT[Bridge Rectifier 4x 1N5408]
    RECT --> FILT[Smoothing Capacitor 2200uF]
    FILT --> REG[Linear Regulator LM7812 or LM7805]
    REG --> OUT1[12V or 5V DC Output]
    OUT1 --> MCU[Motor Driver and Microcontroller]
    REG --> FILT2[Additional Filter 100uF]
    FILT2 --> OUT2[Clean Logic Supply]
```

> **Reading guide:** Standard linear power supply chain — transformer steps down AC, rectifier converts AC to pulsating DC, capacitor smooths the ripple, and regulator clamps the output to a precise voltage level. SMPS variants replace the transformer with a high-frequency switching element for better efficiency ($\mathbf{>85\%}$ vs $\mathbf{40\%}$ for linear).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
