> **KTU Diagram Strategy:** When the question says *"With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a digital control system"* — always include **at minimum** the following blocks: Setpoint → Comparator → Digital Controller → DAC/ZOH → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → Anti-Aliasing Filter → ADC → back to Comparator. Add a clock input arrow to the ADC and the Controller to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define a *digital controller*. State **two** advantages of digital controllers over analog controllers.

**Model Answer:**
A *digital controller* is a microprocessor-, microcontroller-, or PLC-based device that processes sampled and quantized sensor signals to produce discrete control actions for a mechatronic plant.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Flexibility and reprogrammability** — the same hardware can implement different control laws simply by changing the software, whereas analog controllers require hardware reconfiguration.
2. **Communication and networking capability** — digital controllers can interface with fieldbus, Ethernet, and wireless protocols (CAN, Modbus, MQTT) for distributed control, which is impossible with purely analog hardware.

*(Any other valid advantage such as noise immunity, lower cost at scale, ability to implement complex non-linear algorithms — full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
State the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**. What happens if it is violated?

**Model Answer:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Statement:* A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples $x(kT)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$, where $T = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period.

If the theorem is violated (i.e., $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$), the spectral replicas of the signal in the frequency domain **overlap**, causing a distortion known as **aliasing**. Aliasing cannot be undone by post-processing and leads to incorrect controller decisions and unstable closed-loop behaviour.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module-Level Part B questions follow the pattern: **Q.A or Q.B**, each with sub-parts **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**. Both alternatives are given below.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
With a **neat block diagram**, explain the architecture of a digital control system. Identify the role of the **anti-aliasing filter** and the **zero-order hold (ZOH)**.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the canonical architecture.

**Explanation of the block diagram:**

- **Reference Input (R):** The desired value of the controlled variable.
- **Comparator / Summer Junction:** Computes the error $e(kT) = r(kT) - y(kT)$ between the desired and actual outputs.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):** A low-pass analog filter placed *before* the ADC. It band-limits the input signal to frequencies below $f_s/2$, preventing **aliasing**.
- **ADC:** Samples and quantizes the filtered analog signal into a digital word.
- **Digital Controller:** Executes the control algorithm (e.g., discrete PID) using a difference equation.
- **ZOH / DAC:** Holds each controller output constant for one sampling period, converting the discrete code into a stair-step analog signal suitable for an actuator.
- **Actuator:** Applies physical energy to the plant.
- **Plant:** The physical process under control.
- **Sensor:** Measures the actual output.

**Role of the Anti-Aliasing Filter:**
Without the AAF, high-frequency noise or out-of-band signal content above $f_s/2$ would "fold back" into the baseband upon sampling, corrupting the measurement. The AAF guarantees the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.

**Role of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):**
The ZOH reconstructs a piecewise-constant analog signal from the discrete controller output. Mathematically, its transfer function is $G_{ZOH}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$. It introduces an effective time delay of $T/2$ into the loop, which must be accounted for during stability analysis.

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar input range of **[0, 5 V]**. Calculate the **(i) quantization step**, **(ii) number of discrete levels**, and **(iii) digital output code** for an analog input of $V_{in} = 1.735$ V.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Quantization step (LSB):**

For a unipolar ADC: $\displaystyle q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095} \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3}$ V $\approx$ **1.221 mV**.

**(ii) Number of discrete levels:**

$$L = 2^n = 2^{12} = \mathbf{4096 \text{ levels}}$$

**(iii) Digital output code:**

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{q}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{1.735}{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \text{round}(1421.0)$$

$$\boxed{D = 1421_{10} = 0x058D = \text{binary } 0101\,1000\,1101}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (3 marks lost in KTU valuation):** Students often use $q = V_{FS}/2^n$ (i.e., 5/4096) for a unipolar ADC. While acceptable for large $n$, the **strictly correct** unipolar formula uses $2^n - 1$ in the denominator because there are exactly $2^n - 1$ *intervals* between $2^n$ codes. Always state which convention you adopt.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary]**
Derive the **discrete-time (z-domain) transfer function** of a plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s + a}$ when preceded by a ZOH and sampled at period $T$. Hence compute the pulse-transfer function for $K = 2$, $a = 1$, $T = 1$ s.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

- **Step 1:** The ZOH-augmented plant is:

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \cdot G(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot \frac{K}{s + a}$$

- **Step 2:** Apply the Z-transform of ZOH-cascade:

$$G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{K}{s(s+a)}\right\}$$

- **Step 3:** Partial-fraction expansion:

$$\frac{K}{s(s+a)} = \frac{K}{a}\left[\frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+a}\right]$$

- **Step 4:** Z-transform using standard pairs:

$$\mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s}\right\} = \frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s+a}\right\} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-aT}}$$

- **Step 5:** Combine:

$$G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \frac{K}{a} \left[\frac{z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z - e^{-aT}}\right]$$

- **Step 6:** Use $1 - z^{-1} = (z-1)/z$:

$$G(z) = \frac{K}{a} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{z-1}{z - e^{-aT}}\right] = \frac{K}{a} \cdot \frac{(z - e^{-aT}) - (z-1)}{z - e^{-aT}}$$

- **Step 7 — General closed form:**

$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{K}{a} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-aT}}{z - e^{-aT}}}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** for $K = 2$, $a = 1$, $T = 1$ s:

- $K/a = 2/1 = 2$
- $e^{-aT} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$
- $1 - e^{-aT} \approx 0.6321$

$$G(z) = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6321}{z - 0.3679} = \frac{1.2642}{z - 0.3679}$$

Equivalently in $z^{-1}$ form:

$$G(z) = \frac{1.2642 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3679 \, z^{-1}}$$

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary]**
Explain the **architecture and working of a PLC** as a digital controller in industrial automation. List **three advantages** of using PLCs over relay-based control.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of a PLC:**

A Programmable Logic Controller consists of the following modules:

- **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Executes the user program stored in memory; contains the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), program counter, and accumulator.
- **Memory:** Non-volatile ROM stores the system firmware; volatile RAM stores the user program (ladder logic) and live process data.
- **Input Module:** Interfaces with field sensors (limit switches, proximity sensors, thermocouples); provides **isolation**, **signal conditioning**, and **optical isolation** between field signals and the CPU.
- **Output Module:** Interfaces with actuators (contactors, solenoid valves, motors); provides **isolation** and **drive capability** (relay, transistor, or triac outputs).
- **Power Supply:** Converts mains AC to regulated DC voltages (typically 24 V DC and 5 V DC) for internal electronics.
- **Programming Device:** A laptop/handheld terminal used to write and download the ladder-logic program.

**Working — Scan Cycle:**

The PLC operates in an infinite **cyclic scan**:

1. **Input Scan:** Reads the status of all input modules and updates the **Input Image Table (IIT)**.
2. **Program Scan:** Executes the user ladder-logic instructions **one rung at a time**, using the IIT and the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan:** Writes the results from the OIT to the physical output modules, energizing/de-energizing actuators.
4. **Housekeeping:** Communication handling, self-diagnostics, and timer updates.

The total scan time (typically 1–10 ms per cycle) is a critical performance parameter.

**Three advantages of PLCs over relay-based control:**

1. **Programmability and flexibility** — control logic can be modified by editing software, without rewiring hardware.
2. **Reliability and compactness** — solid-state electronics replace dozens of electromechanical relays, drastically reducing failure points and physical space.
3. **Communication and diagnostics** — built-in Ethernet/MODBUS/CAN ports enable remote monitoring, fault logging, and integration with SCADA systems; relays have no such capability.

*(Other valid advantages: lower long-term cost, easier troubleshooting, faster response time, ability to implement complex sequential logic.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Digital Controllers" Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing the two ADC quantization formulas** — $V_{FS}/(2^n)$ for bipolar vs. $V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ for unipolar. Always state the **range explicitly**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1 - z^{-1})$ factor** when converting a ZOH-cascade continuous plant $G(s)$ to $G(z)$. This single omission cascades through the entire derivation.
> 3. **Mixing up $s$ and $z$ domain variables** — writing $G(z) = K/(z + a)$ instead of $G(z) = K/(z - e^{-aT})$. The pole location in $z$-domain is the **discrete-time exponential**, not the continuous-time pole directly.
> 4. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter** in the block diagram. Examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for this block.
> 5. **Writing the Z-transform definition with the wrong summation range** — it is $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}$ (one-sided), **not** $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}$ (two-sided, which is for the bilateral form).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Digital Controllers**

- ✅ Digital controllers process **sampled, quantized** signals — discrete in **time** *and* **amplitude**.
- ✅ A digital control loop contains: **Sensor → AAF → ADC → Controller → ZOH/DAC → Actuator → Plant**.
- ✅ **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ — violation causes **aliasing**.
- ✅ **Quantization step (unipolar):** $q = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$.
- ✅ **Quantization step (bipolar):** $q = 2 V_{FS}/2^n = V_{FS}/2^{n-1}$.
- ✅ **Z-transform** is the discrete-time analogue of the Laplace transform.
- ✅ ZOH has transfer function $(1 - e^{-sT})/s$; introduces delay $T/2$.
- ✅ Discrete PID difference equation in **positional form** is the exam-ready formulation.
- ✅ **PLC architecture** = CPU + Memory + Input Module + Output Module + Power Supply + Programming Device.
- ✅ PLC scan cycle = Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- ✅ Three canonical digital controller platforms: **Microprocessor, Microcontroller, PLC**.
- ✅ Microprocessor = external memory; Microcontroller = on-chip memory + peripherals; PLC = industrial I/O + ladder logic.
- ✅ SQNR ≈ $6.02 n + 1.76$ dB — every extra ADC bit improves SNR by ~6 dB.
- ✅ The Nyquist frequency $f_N = f_s/2$ sets the **anti-aliasing filter cut-off**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Controllers in Mechatronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Controllers in Mechatronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Intelligent Controllers** are adaptive, knowledge-based control systems that emulate the decision-making, reasoning, and learning capabilities of human experts to govern the behavior of dynamic mechatronic systems, especially in the presence of **non-linearity**, **uncertainty**, **time-variance**, and **imprecise sensor data**.

In the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus framework, intelligent controllers are positioned as the evolution of conventional closed-loop control (PID, Lead-Lag) into systems that incorporate **soft-computing paradigms** such as:

1. **Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)**
2. **Artificial Neural Network (ANN) Control**
3. **Expert System-based Control**
4. **Neuro-Fuzzy Hybrid Control**
5. **Genetic Algorithm (GA) Tuned Control**
6. **Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS)**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction:** Conventional PID controllers rely on an *exact mathematical model* of the plant, whereas intelligent controllers rely on *human expertise, heuristic rules, or learned patterns* — making them ideal when modeling is impossible, expensive, or insufficient.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a teenager to drive a car versus programming a robot with differential equations.

| Scenario | Conventional Controller (PID) | Intelligent Controller |
|----------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Driving a car on a highway | Cruise control uses fixed gain for constant speed | An AI-driven system uses **rules, perception, and learning** to handle rain, traffic, and curves |
| Washing machine | Uses preset timer (fixed logic) | Modern inverter machines use **fuzzy logic** to sense load and fabric type, deciding wash time automatically |
| Air Conditioner (AC) | On/Off thermostat (hysteresis) | Modern ACs use **fuzzy logic** to evaluate room temp, humidity, and occupancy to modulate compressor smoothly |

> [!TIP]  
> Think of an intelligent controller as a **"mini human expert" baked into a chip** — it does not solve differential equations, it mimics *how a human would react* using IF-THEN rules, learned experience, or both.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Rule Count in FLC:** Typically 5 to 49 rules
- **Membership Function Types:** Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, Sigmoid
- **Neural Network Layers:** Minimum 3 (Input, Hidden, Output) — universal approximation requires at least **1 hidden layer**
- **Defuzzification Output Range:** Normalized to **0 to 1** (or scaled to actuator range)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Need for Intelligent Control in Mechatronics

Conventional control fails when:

- The plant is **non-linear** (e.g., robotic arm with friction)
- The system has **unknown dynamics** (e.g., soft tissue in surgical robot)
- Sensors are **noisy or imprecise** (e.g., low-cost IR sensor)
- **Real-time re-tuning** is required
- Human expertise is available but not easily transferable into equations

### 2.2 Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) — The Workhorse of Mechatronics

#### 2.2.1 Architecture (4 Blocks)
1. **Fuzzification Module** — Converts crisp input to fuzzy sets
2. **Knowledge Base** — Holds rule base (IF-THEN) and database (MFs)
3. **Inference Engine** — Matches rules to inputs
4. **Defuzzification Module** — Converts fuzzy output back to crisp value

#### 2.2.2 Membership Function — Triangular

$$
\mu(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, & a < x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}, & b < x < c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}
$$

#### 2.2.3 Mamdani Fuzzy Inference (Most Common in Mechatronics)

The output of each rule $R_i$ is a fuzzy set obtained by:

$$
\mu_{B_i}(y) = \min(\mu_{A_i}(x),\, \mu_{B_i}(y))
$$

#### 2.2.4 Defuzzification (Centroid / Center of Gravity — CoG)

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{\int y \cdot \mu_{B}(y)\, dy}{\int \mu_{B}(y)\, dy}
$$

### 2.3 Artificial Neural Network (ANN) Controller

A **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** with back-propagation is the standard ANN used as a controller.

**Activation Function — Sigmoid:**

$$
f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

**Output of a Neuron:**

$$
y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> ANN controllers are used when the plant's inverse dynamics can be learned from **input-output data** (a powerful tool in model-free control).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Triangular MF | $\mu(x) = (x-a)/(b-a)$ for $a \le x \le b$ | Fuzzification |
| 2 | Gaussian MF | $\mu(x) = \exp(-(x-c)^{2}/2\sigma^{2})$ | Smooth FLC |
| 3 | Min (Mamdani) | $\mu_{B_i}(y) = \min(\mu_{A_i}, \mu_{B_i})$ | Rule firing strength |
| 4 | Product (Larsen) | $\mu_{B_i}(y) = \mu_{A_i} \cdot \mu_{B_i}$ | Rule scaling |
| 5 | Centroid (CoG) | $y^{*} = \int y \cdot \mu_{B}(y) \, dy \,/\, \int \mu_{B}(y) \, dy$ | Defuzzification |
| 6 | Mean of Maxima | $y^{*} = \sum y_j / N$ where $\mu_{B}(y_j)$ is max | Defuzzification (fast) |
| 7 | Sigmoid activation | $f(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ | ANN neuron |
| 8 | ReLU activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Deep networks |
| 9 | Back-prop weight update | $w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \cdot \partial E / \partial w$ | ANN training |
| 10 | ANFIS layers | 5 (fuzzification, product, norm, defuzz, sum) | Neuro-fuzzy |

> [!WARNING]  
> Do NOT use the symbol $\vert$ in markdown tables — use $\mid$ or $\vert$ to prevent table-breaking.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Toyota, Honda, Nissan use FLC in automatic transmissions
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines (LG, Samsung), ACs (Daikin)
- **Robotics:** ANFIS-based mobile robot navigation
- **Industrial Automation:** Crane anti-sway control using FLC
- **Aerospace:** Neuro-fuzzy autopilot for UAVs

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Centroid Defuzzification with Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A fuzzy controller has two rules active, producing fuzzy output sets with membership:

- Rule 1: $\mu_1(y) = 0.4$ (constant for $y \in [0, 10]$)
- Rule 2: $\mu_2(y) = 0.8$ (constant for $y \in [5, 15]$)

Find the crisp defuzzified output using the **Centroid method**.

#### Step 1: Combine the Two Fuzzy Sets (Mamdani — Union / Max)

$$
\mu_{B}(y) = \max(\mu_1(y), \mu_2(y))
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

- For $y \in [0, 5]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0.4, 0) = 0.4$
- For $y \in [5, 10]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0.4, 0.8) = 0.8$
- For $y \in [10, 15]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0, 0.8) = 0.8$
- For $y > 15$ or $y < 0$: $\mu_B(y) = 0$

#### Step 2: Apply the Centroid Formula

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y \cdot \mu_{B}(y) \, dy}{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu_{B}(y) \, dy}
$$

Break the integral into the three active regions:

**Numerator:**

$$
N = \int_{0}^{5} y(0.4)\, dy + \int_{5}^{10} y(0.8)\, dy + \int_{10}^{15} y(0.8)\, dy
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\int_{0}^{5} 0.4y\, dy = 0.4 \cdot \left[\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{5} = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{25}{2} = 0.4 \cdot 12.5 = 5.0
$$

$$
\int_{5}^{10} 0.8y\, dy = 0.8 \cdot \left[\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{5}^{10} = 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{100 - 25}{2} = 0.8 \cdot 37.5 = 30.0
$$

$$
\int_{10}^{15} 0.8y\, dy = 0.8 \cdot \left[\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{10}^{15} = 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{225 - 100}{2} = 0.8 \cdot 62.5 = 50.0
$$

Therefore:

$$
N = 5.0 + 30.0 + 50.0 = 85.0
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
D = \int_{0}^{5} 0.4\, dy + \int_{5}^{10} 0.8\, dy + \int_{10}^{15} 0.8\, dy
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\int_{0}^{5} 0.4\, dy = 0.4 \cdot 5 = 2.0
$$

$$
\int_{5}^{10} 0.8\, dy = 0.8 \cdot 5 = 4.0
$$

$$
\int_{10}^{15} 0.8\, dy = 0.8 \cdot 5 = 4.0
$$

Therefore:

$$
D = 2.0 + 4.0 + 4.0 = 10.0
$$

#### Step 3: Final Crisp Output

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{N}{D} = \dfrac{85.0}{10.0} = 8.5
$$

**Interpretation:** The fuzzy controller commands an actuator value of **8.5** (out of a 0–15 range) based on the combined rule firings.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: ANN Neuron Output (Single Hidden Layer)

**Problem:** Compute the output of a single neuron with inputs $x_1 = 0.5$, $x_2 = 0.8$, weights $w_1 = 0.6$, $w_2 = -0.4$, bias $b = 0.1$, and **sigmoid activation**.

#### Step 1: Weighted Sum

$$
S = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
$$

$$
S = (0.6)(0.5) + (-0.4)(0.8) + 0.1 = 0.30 - 0.32 + 0.10 = 0.08
$$

#### Step 2: Apply Sigmoid Activation

$$
f(S) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-S}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.08}}
$$

$$
e^{-0.08} \approx 0.9231
$$

$$
f(S) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.9231} = \dfrac{1}{1.9231} \approx 0.5200
$$

**Final Output:** $y = 0.5200$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Intelligent Controllers

| Criterion | Fuzzy Logic | Neural Network | Neuro-Fuzzy (ANFIS) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Requires training data? | No (rules suffice) | Yes (lots of data) | Yes (refines MF + rules) |
| Handles non-linearity? | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Interpretability? | High (rules readable) | Low (black box) | High |
| Real-time computation? | Very fast | Moderate (depends on size) | Moderate |
| Knowledge source | Expert | Historical data | Both combined |
| KTU exam relevance | Very High | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Intelligent Control System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input] --> B[Fuzzification Module]
    C[Sensor Feedback] --> B
    B --> D[Knowledge Base with IF THEN Rules]
    D --> E[Inference Engine]
    E --> F[Defuzzification Module]
    F --> G[Plant or Mechatronic System]
    G --> H[Process Output]
    H --> C
```

### 4.2 Detailed Fuzzy Logic Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I1[Crisp Input Error e] --> F1[Fuzzifier]
    I2[Crisp Input Change in Error de] --> F1
    F1 --> K1[Knowledge Base Membership Functions]
    F1 --> K2[Rule Base IF THEN Rules]
    K1 --> IE[Inference Engine]
    K2 --> IE
    IE --> DF[Defuzzifier]
    DF --> O1[Crisp Control Output u]
    O1 --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> SYS[Mechatronic Plant]
    SYS --> SENS[Sensor]
    SENS --> I1
    SENS --> I2
```

### 4.3 Neural Network Controller Architecture (MLP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input 1] --> N1
    X2[Input 2] --> N1
    X3[Input n] --> N1
    N1[Input Layer] --> H1[Hidden Layer 1]
    H1 --> H2[Hidden Layer 2]
    H2 --> O1[Output Layer]
    O1 --> Y[Control Signal]
    Y --> P[Mechatronic Plant]
    P --> SENS[Sensor Feedback]
    SENS --> X1
    SENS --> X2
    SENS --> X3
```

### 4.4 ANFIS Five-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Fuzzification Nodes]
    L2[Layer 2 Product T Norm Nodes]
    L3[Layer 3 Normalized Firing Strengths]
    L4[Layer 4 Consequent Nodes Linear Functions]
    L5[Layer 5 Summation Output]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

### 4.5 Comparison: Conventional vs Intelligent Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ConventionalControl[Conventional Control Path]
        C1[Reference Input] --> C2[PID Controller]
        C2 --> C3[Plant Mathematical Model Required]
        C3 --> C4[Actuator]
        C4 --> C5[Output]
    end
    subgraph IntelligentControl[Intelligent Control Path]
        I1[Reference Input] --> I2[Fuzzy or Neural or Neuro Fuzzy]
        I2 --> I3[Heuristic Rules or Learned Model]
        I3 --> I4[Actuator]
        I4 --> I5[Output]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define an Intelligent Controller. List any two advantages over conventional PID.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An **intelligent controller** is a control system that incorporates artificial intelligence techniques — such as fuzzy logic, neural networks, expert systems, or genetic algorithms — to emulate human-like decision-making in controlling complex, non-linear, or uncertain mechatronic systems.

**Two advantages over PID:**

1. **No precise mathematical model required** — uses expert knowledge or learned data instead.
2. **Robust to non-linearity and parameter variation** — adapts via rules or learning, unlike fixed-gain PID.

---

#### Question 2: What is Defuzzification? Name two methods.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Defuzzification** is the process of converting the fuzzy output set (produced by the inference engine) into a single crisp value that can be sent to the actuator.

**Two Methods:**

1. **Centroid (Center of Gravity / CoG)** — uses the geometric center of the area under the aggregated MF.
2. **Mean of Maxima (MoM)** — averages the $y$ values where the membership is maximum.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Explain the block diagram of a **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)** with neat diagram. Discuss each block. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Fuzzy Logic Controller consists of **four major blocks**:

1. **Fuzzification Module:**
   - Converts crisp sensor inputs (e.g., error $e$ and change in error $\Delta e$) into fuzzy sets.
   - Uses **membership functions** (triangular, trapezoidal, Gaussian) to assign a degree of membership $\mu \in [0, 1]$ to each linguistic label such as "Negative Big (NB)", "Zero (ZE)", "Positive Big (PB)".

2. **Knowledge Base:**
   - Contains two components: the **database** (defining MFs) and the **rule base** (a set of IF-THEN statements).
   - Example rule: *IF error is NB AND change-in-error is NS THEN output is PVB.*

3. **Inference Engine:**
   - Performs fuzzy reasoning by matching the fuzzified inputs against the rule base.
   - Uses operators like **MIN (Mamdani)** or **PRODUCT (Larsen)** for AND conjunction.
   - Produces a fuzzy output set for each rule.

4. **Defuzzification Module:**
   - Converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a crisp control signal $u$.
   - Common method: **Centroid** (CoG), which computes the geometric center of the area under the aggregated output MF.

**Working Flow:**

$$
\text{Crisp Input} \xrightarrow{\text{Fuzzification}} \text{Fuzzy Set} \xrightarrow{\text{Inference + Rule Base}} \text{Fuzzy Output} \xrightarrow{\text{Defuzzification}} \text{Crisp Control Signal}
$$

---

**(b)** Two rules in a fuzzy controller fire simultaneously with output MFs:
- Rule 1: $\mu_1(y) = 0.5$ for $y \in [0, 10]$
- Rule 2: $\mu_2(y) = 0.7$ for $y \in [5, 15]$

Compute the defuzzified output using the **centroid method**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Combine using Max (Mamdani)**

- $y \in [0, 5]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0.5, 0) = 0.5$
- $y \in [5, 10]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0.5, 0.7) = 0.7$
- $y \in [10, 15]$: $\mu_B(y) = \max(0, 0.7) = 0.7$

**Step 2: Numerator of Centroid**

$$
N = \int_{0}^{5} 0.5y\, dy + \int_{5}^{10} 0.7y\, dy + \int_{10}^{15} 0.7y\, dy
$$

$$
= 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{25}{2} + 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{100 - 25}{2} + 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{225 - 100}{2}
$$

$$
= 6.25 + 26.25 + 43.75 = 76.25
$$

**Step 3: Denominator**

$$
D = \int_{0}^{5} 0.5\, dy + \int_{5}^{10} 0.7\, dy + \int_{10}^{15} 0.7\, dy
$$

$$
= 0.5 \cdot 5 + 0.7 \cdot 5 + 0.7 \cdot 5 = 2.5 + 3.5 + 3.5 = 9.5
$$

**Step 4: Crisp Output**

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{76.25}{9.5} \approx 8.026
$$

**Final Answer:** $y^{*} \approx 8.03$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, S2 B.Tech]`

**(a)** Describe the **Artificial Neural Network (ANN) based controller** with a neat block diagram. Mention the role of the activation function and back-propagation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An **ANN controller** uses a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) trained on input-output data of the plant to learn the inverse dynamics or the non-linear mapping from error states to control actions.

**Architecture:**

- **Input Layer:** Accepts measured states (e.g., error $e$, $\Delta e$).
- **Hidden Layer(s):** Each neuron computes a weighted sum, applies a non-linear activation, and passes it forward.
- **Output Layer:** Produces the control signal $u$.

**Activation Function Role:**

- Introduces **non-linearity** — without it, the network collapses into a linear regression model.
- Common activations: **Sigmoid** ($\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$), **Tanh**, **ReLU**.

**Back-Propagation Role:**

- Computes the gradient of the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss w.r.t. each weight.
- Updates weights using gradient descent:

$$
w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \cdot \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w}
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $E$ is the error.

**Strengths:** Handles complex non-linear plants with no model; generalizes well.
**Weakness:** Black-box behavior, large training data needed.

---

**(b)** An ANN neuron receives inputs $x_1 = 0.3$, $x_2 = 0.9$, weights $w_1 = 0.8$, $w_2 = 0.5$, bias $b = 0.2$. Compute the output using the **sigmoid activation function**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Weighted Sum**

$$
S = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.8)(0.3) + (0.5)(0.9) + 0.2
$$

$$
S = 0.24 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 0.89
$$

**Step 2: Sigmoid Activation**

$$
y = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.89}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.4107} = \dfrac{1}{1.4107}
$$

$$
y \approx 0.7089
$$

**Final Answer:** $y \approx 0.709$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Intelligent Controller Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to aggregate** all active rules before defuzzification — students often defuzzify one rule only (lose 3–4 marks).
> 2. **Confusing Mamdani (MIN)** with **Larsen (PRODUCT)** — clearly mention which inference you are using.
> 3. **Integral limits:** When applying centroid, define the range where $\mu_B(y) > 0$ correctly. Wrong limits = wrong answer.
> 4. **In ANN questions**, students often forget the **bias term** $b$ — that costs 1 mark easily.
> 5. **Diagram labeling:** Always label **each block** of the FLC block diagram — unlabeled blocks get partial credit only.
> 6. **Units:** If output is a control voltage/current, mention the actuator range; vague answers are penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Intelligent Controllers** = AI-based controllers (Fuzzy, ANN, Neuro-Fuzzy, Expert, GA-tuned) for complex, non-linear, uncertain mechatronic systems.
- **FLC Pipeline:** Crisp → Fuzzify → Infer (rules) → Defuzzify → Crisp.
- **Mamdani** uses MIN + Max aggregation; **Sugeno** uses weighted average of rule consequents.
- **Centroid (CoG)** is the most accurate defuzzifier; **MoM** is the fastest.
- **ANN Controller** learns inverse dynamics via back-propagation using sigmoid/ReLU activations.
- **ANFIS = Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System** — combines learning of MFs (neural) with reasoning of fuzzy rules.
- **Membership Functions:** Triangular (simple, fast) and Gaussian (smooth, accurate) are the most common.
- **Advantages:** No precise plant model needed; handles non-linearity; uses human expertise; robust.
- **Disadvantages:** Design is heuristic; rule explosion for many inputs; ANN needs lots of data; verification is harder.
- **KTU Favourite:** Block diagram of FLC + one defuzzification numerical — these appear in **almost every KTU end-sem paper**.
- **Remember:** Mention both **method name** (e.g., "Mamdani MIN") AND **defuzzifier name** (e.g., "Centroid") in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Numerical Control - Advantages of NC Systems, Classification of NC Systems, Point to Point and Contouring Systems, NC and CNC, Incremental and Absolute Systems, Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Numerical Control - Advantages of NC Systems, Classification of NC Systems, Point to Point and Contouring Systems, NC and CNC, Incremental and Absolute Systems, Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Numerical Control (NC)?

**Numerical Control (NC)** is defined (per KTU 2024 syllabus) as a form of **programmable automation** in which the mechanical actions of a machine tool (or other process equipment) are controlled by a program consisting of coded numerical data (alphabetic and numeric symbols) supplied to the machine via punched tape, magnetic tape, or other media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** "Numerical Control is the technique of controlling the operations of a machine tool by means of numbers, letters, and other symbols that have been suitably coded to represent instructions for the machine to follow."

The system was pioneered by **John T. Parsons** and **Frank L. Stulen** at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in the early **1950s**, in collaboration with the U.S. Air Force, originally to manufacture complex aircraft components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Recipe for a Robot Chef"

Imagine a **traditional chef** in a kitchen. You must verbally instruct every step: "Take 2 spoons of salt, stir for 30 seconds, add coriander..." If you leave and come back, the chef might forget a step.

Now, imagine a **robot chef** with a printed recipe card. The card is a **program** — a coded sequence of instructions. The robot (machine tool) reads each instruction line and executes it exactly. It does not get distracted, does not forget, and can repeat the same dish **10,000 times** with identical precision.

> That printed recipe card is your **NC part program**, and the robot chef is the **NC machine tool**.

The **key idea**: Instead of a human operator turning handles, levers, and dials, a **machine reads numbers and moves itself**.

---

## 1.3 Components of a Basic NC System

A basic NC system consists of three fundamental sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Program of Instructions** | Defines the sequence of operations | Punched tape, G-code, M-code |
| **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** | Reads, decodes, and executes the program | Logic controllers, microprocessor |
| **Machine Tool (or Process Equipment)** | Performs the actual physical work | Lathe, Milling machine, EDM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The first NC machines used **punched paper tape** as the input medium. Modern machines use **USB, Ethernet, or wireless transfer** of G-code text files.

---

## 1.4 Advantages of NC Systems

NC systems offer significant benefits over conventional manually operated machines. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate these clearly:

### 1.4.1 Production / Manufacturing Benefits

1. **Reduced Non-Productive Time** — Less time spent on setup, positioning, tool changing, and manual measuring.
2. **Lower Scrap Rate** — Fewer operator errors mean less wastage of raw material.
3. **Lower Manufacturing Lead Time** — Programs can be prepared offline and reused, drastically reducing production time.
4. **Higher Productivity** — Continuous, unattended operation over long periods.
5. **Complex Geometry Capability** — Curved profiles, 3D surfaces, and contours impossible to machine manually can be cut accurately.

### 1.4.2 Economic Benefits

6. **Lower Inventory** — Parts can be produced "just-in-time", reducing WIP and finished-goods stock.
7. **Reduced Labor Cost** — One operator can supervise several machines.
8. **Better Resource Utilization** — Consistent machine loading.

### 1.4.3 Quality Benefits

9. **Higher Accuracy and Repeatability** — Typical NC precision is **±0.0025 mm to ±0.025 mm** depending on the machine.
10. **Better Quality Control** — Dimensions are encoded numerically; human judgement is removed.
11. **Easier Inspection** — Since dimensions are in the program, inspection is straightforward.

### 1.4.4 Strategic / Engineering Benefits

12. **Greater Flexibility** — Changing a part design only requires a new program, not a new jig/fixture.
13. **Tool Life Extension** — Optimal speeds/feeds are programmed; no operator-induced abuse.
14. **Engineering Data Base** — Cutting data, tolerances, and tool paths form a digital record.
15. **Easier Simulation and Verification** — Modern NC programs can be run on a computer to verify tool paths before machining.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked "List advantages of NC", a common trap is mentioning only productivity. Examiners expect answers covering **all four quadrants**: Production, Economic, Quality, and Strategic. Mentioning at least 2 from each earns full marks.

---

## 1.5 Disadvantages of NC (Quick Note for Balanced Answer)

- High **initial capital cost** of NC machines.
- Need for **skilled programmers** and maintenance technicians.
- Higher **maintenance cost** and reliance on the machine manufacturer.
- Setup time for the very first part is often **longer** than a manual machine.
- Power consumption and infrastructure requirements are higher.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing a 1-sided answer praising only NC. The KTU valuation key often **deducts marks** if a student does not acknowledge that NC is not always economical for small-batch or very simple parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of NC Systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the classification along **four orthogonal axes**. Mastering all four is mandatory.

### Classification Axis 1: Based on Motion Control

| Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path between points** | Uncontrolled (rapid traverse) | Precisely controlled along a defined path |
| **Tool position during transit** | Unimportant | Critical |
| **Typical operations** | Drilling, punching, boring, tapping | Milling, turning, grinding, EDM |
| **Machine cost** | Lower | Higher (multi-axis simultaneous control) |
| **Programming complexity** | Simple | Complex (involves circular/linear interpolation) |
| **Accuracy of path** | Only at the target point | All along the path |

### Classification Axis 2: Based on Feedback (Control Loop)

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback sensor** | None | Present (encoder, resolver, LVDT) |
| **Actuator** | Stepper motor | Servo motor (DC/AC) |
| **Error correction** | Not possible | Automatic via comparator |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (subject to missed steps) | Higher |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Reliability** | High (fewer components) | Slightly lower (more components) |
| **Power** | Limited | High |

### Classification Axis 3: Based on Coordinate Reference

| Parameter | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference origin** | The current tool position | A fixed machine zero point |
| **Coordinates specify** | Distance to move from current position | Distance from fixed origin |
| **Error accumulation** | Yes (errors add up) | No (each coordinate is independent) |
| **Recovery from mid-program stop** | Difficult | Easy (any position is referenced to origin) |
| **Program length** | Typically shorter | Typically longer |
| **Common in** | Older machines, drilling, simple PTP | Modern CNC, contouring, complex parts |

### Classification Axis 4: Based on Hardware/Computing Architecture

| Type | Full Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **NC** | Numerical Control | Hard-wired logic using relays, tubes, or discrete electronics |
| **CNC** | Computer Numerical Control | Uses a dedicated computer/microprocessor; software-based |
| **DNC** | Direct Numerical Control | Multiple NC machines networked to a central computer |
| **HSC** | High-Speed Cutting | Specialized for very high spindle speeds and feed rates |

---

## 2.2 Point-to-Point (PTP) vs. Contouring Systems

### 2.2.1 Point-to-Point NC System (also called "Position-to-Position")

In PTP systems, the goal is to position the tool at a **discrete set of points** to perform an operation. The path **between** those points is irrelevant; the tool typically moves at **rapid traverse (maximum feed)**.

**Characteristic features:**
- Only **2-axis positioning** is needed (X, Y) for drilling.
- Drives the slide to the coordinate, then the cutting operation occurs.
- The slide decelerates, stops, and the spindle performs the operation.
- The cutter is positioned **above** the workpiece, then fed in the Z-direction.

**Examples of PTP machines:**
- **Drilling machines** (most common)
- **Punch presses**
- **Boring machines**
- **Tapping machines**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A PTP machine is like a **delivery drone** that takes off from point A, flies to point B (path is irrelevant), drops the package, then returns to pick up another one. Only the destination matters.

### 2.2.2 Contouring NC System (also called "Continuous Path")

In contouring systems, the cutting tool is required to follow a **precisely defined continuous path**, often involving simultaneous motion in **two or more axes**. The path itself is the product feature (a curve, a slot, a 3D surface).

**Characteristic features:**
- Requires **simultaneous multi-axis interpolation** (linear, circular, helical, etc.).
- The MCU must compute intermediate points along a curve — this is called **interpolation**.
- Used for **2D, 2.5D, 3D, and 4-axis/5-axis machining**.
- Significantly more complex MCU (CNC-class).

**Examples of Contouring machines:**
- **Milling machines** (2D pocketing, 3D sculpting)
- **Lathes** (turning, threading, profiling)
- **EDM** (Wire EDM, Sinker EDM)
- **Grinders** (profile grinding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A contouring machine is like a **pen-plotter drawing a portrait**. The path of the pen must exactly trace the picture lines; deviation means a bad drawing.

### 2.2.3 Key Interpolation Methods (Contouring)

| Interpolation Type | Motion Description | G-Code (Fanuc) |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Interpolation** | Straight line between two points | G01 |
| **Circular Interpolation (CW)** | Clockwise arc | G02 |
| **Circular Interpolation (CCW)** | Counter-clockwise arc | G03 |
| **Helical Interpolation** | Circular + perpendicular linear motion | G02/G03 with Z motion |
| **Parabolic / Spline** | Higher-order curves (advanced CNC) | G05 / G06.2 (NURBS) |

---

## 2.3 NC vs. CNC: The Architectural Evolution

| Feature | Conventional NC | CNC |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Logic** | Hard-wired (relays, diodes, vacuum tubes) | Software (microprocessor) |
| **Programming Medium** | Punched paper tape | Magnetic, USB, network |
| **Program Storage** | None (must reload tape) | Internal memory (MBs to GBs) |
| **Editing Programs** | Manual re-punching | On-screen editor |
| **Compensation** | Manual (tool length, leadscrew) | Automatic in software |
| **Diagnostics** | Limited LED indicators | Self-diagnostics, error logs |
| **Interpolation** | Limited (often only linear in NC) | All types (linear, circular, helical, spline) |
| **Cost** | Lower initially (now obsolete) | Higher initial, lower lifecycle |
| **Flexibility** | Low (logic is fixed) | Very high (soft logic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Reality:** Almost every NC machine sold today is technically a **CNC machine**. Pure hard-wired NC is a **historical category**. In KTU exam answers, however, you must still discuss NC for theoretical completeness.

---

## 2.4 Incremental vs. Absolute Positioning — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 Incremental Positioning System

- The tool's **current position is the reference** for the next move.
- All coordinates in the program represent **distances to be moved** from the current position.
- In the program, the command is **ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ** (change in position).
- Errors in one move **propagate** to subsequent moves.

**Worked example:** A drill must move from A(0,0) → B(10,20) → C(30,40).
- Incremental program: `ΔX=+10, ΔY=+20` (to B), then `ΔX=+20, ΔY=+20` (to C).
- If the first move is off by 0.1 mm, the second move inherits that 0.1 mm error.

### 2.4.2 Absolute Positioning System

- A **single fixed origin** (machine zero, usually set at a corner of the workpiece fixture) is the reference.
- All coordinates in the program are **measured from this origin**.
- The command is **X, Y, Z** (position from origin).
- Errors do **not** propagate; each coordinate is independently defined.

**Worked example:** Same points A, B, C.
- Absolute program: `X=10, Y=20` (to B), then `X=30, Y=40` (to C).
- If the first move has a 0.1 mm error, the second move is still independently correct.

### 2.4.3 G-Code Identification (Fanuc / Siemens conventions)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| **G90** | Absolute programming mode |
| **G91** | Incremental programming mode |
| **G92** | Set absolute coordinate register (used in some controls) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing "incremental" with "differential." In KTU, examiners often pose a question asking **"If G90 is active and you want to move to X=50, what value do you type?"** — answer is **50** (the absolute coordinate), NOT the distance moved.

---

## 2.5 Open Loop vs. Closed Loop Control Systems

### 2.5.1 Open Loop System

- **No feedback** is taken from the output (final tool position).
- Control is based purely on the input command.
- The most common actuator is a **stepper motor**, which moves a fixed angular step per pulse.
- If the load is too high, the stepper can **miss steps** without the system knowing — leading to positional error.

**Block diagram (logical flow):**
`Input pulse → Controller → Stepper driver → Stepper motor → Leadscrew → Table`

### 2.5.2 Closed Loop System

- A **position feedback sensor** (rotary encoder, linear scale, resolver, or LVDT) measures actual position.
- The measured value is **compared** with the commanded value in a **comparator**.
- Any **error signal** is sent back to the controller, which adjusts the drive.
- The actuator is typically a **servo motor** (DC or AC).

**Block diagram (logical flow):**
`Input → Comparator ← Feedback sensor → Servo driver → Servo motor → Leadscrew → Table`

### 2.5.3 Transfer Function Perspective (Conceptual)

In a control-systems sense, we can model the open-loop transfer function as:

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_a(s) \cdot G_m(s)$$

where $G_c(s)$ is the controller, $G_a(s)$ is the actuator, and $G_m(s)$ is the mechanical load.

For a closed-loop system with unity feedback:

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

The term $[1 + G_{OL}(s)]$ in the denominator is the **loop gain**. It reduces steady-state error and rejects disturbances — the fundamental reason closed-loop NC is more accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Note:** While closed-loop systems are more accurate, very high loop gain can cause **oscillation and instability**. KTU often asks: "Why not always use closed loop?" The answer: **cost, complexity, and potential instability**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this entire table for any NC question in the KTU 2024 exam.

| Concept | Key Formula / Definition | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|
| NC Definition | Programmed automation via numerical codes | — |
| Typical NC accuracy | $\Delta x$ | $\pm 0.0025$ to $\pm 0.025$ mm |
| Stepper motor step angle | $\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$ | degrees / pulse |
| Table displacement per pulse | $d = \theta_s \cdot \frac{L}{2\pi}$ | mm / pulse |
| Linear feed rate | $f = n \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ | mm/min |
| Spindle speed | $N = \frac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm |
| Open-loop transfer function | $G_{OL}(s) = G_c \cdot G_a \cdot G_m$ | — |
| Closed-loop transfer function | $G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}}{1 + G_{OL}}$ | — |
| Incremental coordinate | $\Delta x = x_{new} - x_{old}$ | mm |
| Absolute coordinate | $X = x_{measured} - x_{origin}$ | mm |
| PTP Path | Position-controlled only at endpoints | — |
| Contouring Path | Position-controlled continuously | — |

*(Note: $\vert$ symbols are avoided inside table cells to prevent markdown breaking; the above uses standard mathematical notation.)*

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

NC systems underpin virtually all of **modern manufacturing**:
- **Aerospace** — turbine blades, fuselage skins, complex 3D components.
- **Automotive** — engine blocks, cylinder heads, body panel dies.
- **Medical** — orthopedic implants, surgical instruments, dental prosthetics.
- **Electronics** — PCB drilling, semiconductor wafer dicing.
- **Die & Mold** — injection mold cavities (multi-axis contouring).
- **Defence** — gun barrels, missile casings.

The transition from NC to CNC was the **foundation** for modern **Industry 4.0**, **Smart Manufacturing**, and the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stepper Motor Step Angle and Table Resolution

The **step angle** $\theta_s$ of a stepper motor is determined by the number of magnetic pole pairs or teeth on the rotor:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$$

where $N_{steps}$ is the number of steps per revolution of the motor shaft.

**Step 1 — Identify the input.**
For a standard hybrid stepper motor, $N_{steps} = 200$ (i.e., 1.8° per step).

**Step 2 — Compute step angle in degrees.**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to radians for further calculation.**

$$\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi}{180^{\circ}} = 0.03142 \text{ rad per step}$$

**Step 4 — Relate to linear table motion.**
The leadscrew has a pitch $L$ (mm per revolution). When the motor turns one step, the leadscrew rotates by $\theta_s$, moving the table linearly by:

$$d = \frac{\theta_s}{2\pi} \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**
Let $L = 5$ mm/rev (common leadscrew pitch). Then:

$$d = \frac{0.03142}{2\pi} \cdot 5 = \frac{0.03142}{6.2832} \cdot 5$$

$$d = 0.005 \cdot 5 = 0.025 \text{ mm per step}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.**
This means each pulse from the controller advances the table by **0.025 mm = 25 µm**. This is the **theoretical resolution** of an open-loop stepper-driven NC axis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Position Error and Steady-State Response

Consider a first-order plant with a proportional controller. Let the open-loop transfer function be:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K}{s + a}$$

**Step 1 — Form the closed-loop transfer function with unity feedback.**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{\frac{K}{s+a}}{1 + \frac{K}{s+a}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $(s+a)$.**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K}{s + a + K}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the new pole and time constant.**

$$s_{pole} = -(a + K)$$

$$\tau = \frac{1}{a + K}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Final Value Theorem for a step input of magnitude $R$.**
For a step input, the steady-state output is:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{CL}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{s} = R \cdot \frac{K}{a + K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute steady-state error.**

$$e_{ss} = R - y_{ss} = R \left(1 - \frac{K}{a + K}\right) = R \cdot \frac{a}{a + K}$$

**Step 6 — Analyze the result.**
As $K$ (the loop gain / controller gain) increases:

$$e_{ss} = R \cdot \frac{a}{a + K} \longrightarrow 0 \text{ as } K \to \infty$$

This is the **fundamental benefit of closed-loop control**: increasing loop gain drives the steady-state error toward zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Increasing $K$ beyond a critical value makes the closed-loop pole $-(a+K)$ move further left in the s-plane, **speeding up response** but also risking **overshoot and oscillation**. The optimal $K$ balances accuracy and stability — a concept KTU tests in Control Systems modules as well.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Between Incremental and Absolute Coordinates

Suppose a tool starts at origin O(0, 0) and must visit four points in sequence: A, B, C, D. Their absolute coordinates are:

$$A = (10, 5), \quad B = (25, 15), \quad C = (40, 20), \quad D = (55, 30)$$

**Step 1 — Compute incremental coordinates using the relation:**

$$\Delta x_i = X_{i} - X_{i-1}$$

$$\Delta y_i = Y_{i} - Y_{i-1}$$

**Step 2 — From O(0, 0) to A(10, 5):**

$$\Delta x_1 = 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_1 = 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — From A(10, 5) to B(25, 15):**

$$\Delta x_2 = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_2 = 15 - 5 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — From B(25, 15) to C(40, 20):**

$$\Delta x_3 = 40 - 25 = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_3 = 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — From C(40, 20) to D(55, 30):**

$$\Delta x_4 = 55 - 40 = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_4 = 30 - 20 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Resulting incremental program:**

$$\{\Delta x_i, \Delta y_i\} = \{(10, 5), (15, 10), (15, 5), (15, 10)\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reverse Conversion:** To convert incremental back to absolute, sum successive $\Delta$ values cumulatively. This is the very reason **error accumulates** in incremental systems — a small rounding error at each step adds up.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Spindle Speed for a Turning Operation

For a turning operation on a lathe, the spindle speed $N$ in rpm is computed from the cutting speed $v$ (m/min) and workpiece diameter $D$ (mm):

**Step 1 — Begin with circumference of the workpiece.**

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — Cutting speed is linear surface speed:**

$$v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(with consistent units)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a convenient form for mm and rpm:**

$$N = \frac{v}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(if } v \text{ in mm/rev and } D \text{ in mm)}$$

But cutting speed is typically given in m/min, so multiply by 1000 to convert to mm/min:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Given $v = 200$ m/min and $D = 50$ mm:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 200}{\pi \cdot 50} = \frac{200{,}000}{157.08} = 1273 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Practical adjustment.**
The NC controller will round this to a programmed value such as $N = 1273$ or $N = 1275$ rpm, depending on the machine's resolution.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right NC Architecture

The table below guides engineering decisions for KTU application-style questions.

| Application Scenario | Recommended Motion Type | Recommended Control Loop | Recommended Coordinate System |
|---|---|---|---|
| PCB drilling 1000 holes | PTP | Open loop (stepper) | Incremental |
| Aircraft wing skin profile | Contouring | Closed loop (servo) | Absolute |
| Injection mold cavity (3D) | Contouring (5-axis) | Closed loop (servo) | Absolute |
| Punched sheet metal | PTP | Open or closed | Incremental |
| Turbine blade (5-axis) | Contouring | Closed loop (linear scales) | Absolute |
| Tapping a few holes in a casing | PTP | Closed loop (for tap safety) | Incremental |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic NC System

This is the **canonical block diagram** every KTU student must reproduce in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRG[Part Program Punched Tape or G Code] --> MCU[Machine Control Unit MCU]
    MCU --> DRV1[Servo or Stepper Drive X Axis]
    MCU --> DRV2[Servo or Stepper Drive Y Axis]
    MCU --> DRV3[Servo or Stepper Drive Z Axis]
    DRV1 --> MOT1[X Axis Motor]
    DRV2 --> MOT2[Y Axis Motor]
    DRV3 --> MOT3[Z Axis Motor]
    MOT1 --> LS1[Leadscrew X]
    MOT2 --> LS2[Leadscrew Y]
    MOT3 --> LS3[Leadscrew Z]
    LS1 --> TB[Machine Tool Table and Spindle]
    LS2 --> TB
    LS3 --> TB
    TB --> FB[Position Feedback Sensor Encoders or Resolvers]
    FB --> MCU
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The MCU reads the part program, sends commands to the drives, which power the motors. The motors turn the leadscrews, which move the table. In closed-loop mode, the position sensor sends actual position back to the MCU for error correction.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of NC Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NC[Numerical Control Systems]
    NC --> M1[Based on Motion Type]
    NC --> M2[Based on Feedback]
    NC --> M3[Based on Coordinate Reference]
    NC --> M4[Based on Computing Hardware]

    M1 --> PTP[Point to Point PTP]
    M1 --> CNT[Contouring Continuous Path]

    M2 --> OL[Open Loop Stepper Motor]
    M2 --> CL[Closed Loop Servo Motor plus Feedback]

    M3 --> INC[Incremental]
    M3 --> ABS[Absolute]

    M4 --> NCHW[Hard Wired NC Relays Tubes]
    M4 --> CNCSW[Computer NC Microprocessor]
    M4 --> DNC[Direct NC Networked to Host]
```

---

## 4.3 Open Loop vs. Closed Loop Control — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpenLoop
        A1[Input Pulses] --> A2[Controller] --> A3[Stepper Driver] --> A4[Stepper Motor] --> A5[Leadscrew] --> A6[Table]
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop
        B1[Reference Position Command] --> B2[Comparator] --> B3[Servo Controller] --> B4[Servo Driver] --> B5[Servo Motor] --> B6[Leadscrew] --> B7[Table]
        B7 --> B8[Position Sensor Encoder]
        B8 --> B2
    end
```

> **Key difference visible in the diagram:** Closed loop has a feedback arrow from output (table) to the comparator, while open loop has a one-way flow with no return.

---

## 4.4 PTP vs. Contouring — Trajectory Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PTP_System
        P1[Point A] --> P2[Point B]
        P2 --> P3[Point C]
        P3 --> P4[Point D]
    end
    P1 -.- NOTE1[Path between points is uncontrolled rapid traverse]
    P2 -.- NOTE1
    P3 -.- NOTE1

    subgraph Contouring_System
        C1[Start Point] --> C2[Curved Toolpath]
        C2 --> C3[Intermediate Point]
        C3 --> C4[End Point]
    end
    C2 -.- NOTE2[Path is precisely controlled linear circular interpolation]
    C3 -.- NOTE2
```

---

## 4.5 Incremental vs. Absolute Coordinate Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Absolute_System
        A0[Machine Origin O 0 0] --> AX1[A 30 20]
        A0 --> AX2[B 60 50]
        A0 --> AX3[C 80 70]
    end

    subgraph Incremental_System
        I0[Start Position 0 0] --> IX1[Move DeltaX 30 DeltaY 20 to A]
        IX1 --> IX2[Move DeltaX 30 DeltaY 30 to B]
        IX2 --> IX3[Move DeltaX 20 DeltaY 20 to C]
    end
```

> **Reading note:** In absolute mode, every coordinate is referenced to the same origin O. In incremental mode, every move is referenced to the previous position.

---

## 4.6 NC vs. CNC Hardware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Conventional_NC
        T1[Punched Tape Reader] --> T2[Hard Wired Logic Board Relays and Diodes] --> T3[Discrete Drive Circuit] --> T4[Machine Axes]
    end

    subgraph CNC
        C1[Program File G Code] --> C2[Microprocessor or Industrial PC] --> C2A[RAM and ROM Memory] --> C3[Interpolator and Controller Software] --> C4[Servo or Stepper Drive] --> C5[Machine Axes]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 x 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Contouring NC systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring |
|---|---|---|
| Path between endpoints | Not controlled; tool moves at rapid traverse | Precisely controlled along a defined path |
| Path accuracy | Only at endpoints | All along the path |
| Axes of simultaneous motion | Typically 2 (X, Y) for positioning | 2 or more (X, Y, Z simultaneously) |
| Programming complexity | Simple | Complex (requires interpolation) |
| **Example** | Drilling machine | Milling machine |

**(3 marks: 1 for path concept, 1 for axes/comparison, 1 for example)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between Incremental and Absolute coordinate systems in NC programming. State the corresponding G-code commands.**

**Model Answer:**

- In **Incremental** positioning, coordinates specify the **distance to move from the current position**. Errors accumulate across moves. **G91** is the Fanuc code.
- In **Absolute** positioning, coordinates specify the **distance from a fixed origin** (machine zero). Each coordinate is independent and errors do not accumulate. **G90** is the Fanuc code.

**(3 marks: 1 for incremental definition + G91, 1 for absolute definition + G90, 1 for error-accumulation distinction)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks): Explain with neat block diagrams the architecture of an Open Loop and a Closed Loop NC positioning system. Compare their relative merits.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Open Loop System (3.5 marks):**

In an **open loop NC system**, there is no feedback from the output to the input. The flow is:

`Input Pulses → Controller → Stepper Motor Driver → Stepper Motor → Leadscrew → Machine Table`

- The MCU sends a train of pulses; each pulse rotates the stepper motor by a fixed step angle (e.g., **1.8°**).
- The leadscrew converts this rotation into linear table movement.
- **No sensor** checks the actual position. If the load is too high, the stepper may **miss steps** and the table will be in the wrong position without the system knowing.
- Typical resolution: **0.025 mm per pulse** (for 1.8° stepper with 5 mm pitch leadscrew).

**Part 2 — Closed Loop System (3.5 marks):**

In a **closed loop NC system**, a position feedback sensor (rotary encoder on motor shaft, or linear scale on table) provides the **actual position** to a comparator.

`Reference Position → Comparator → Servo Controller → Servo Driver → Servo Motor → Leadscrew → Table`
`                                                                                              ↑`
`                                                                                          Feedback Sensor`

- The **comparator** computes the **error** = (Commanded Position) − (Actual Position).
- The error is fed to the servo controller, which drives the motor to **minimize the error**.
- Closed loop achieves **higher accuracy** (typical ±0.0025 mm) and **higher torque/feed rates**.
- Disadvantages: **higher cost, more complex, risk of instability.**

**Comparison Table (for 1 of the 7 marks, embedded):**

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None | Position sensor + comparator |
| Motor | Stepper | Servo |
| Accuracy | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Missed step detection | Not possible | Possible (alarm triggered) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditional on tuning |

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks): A stepper motor has 200 steps/revolution and is connected to a leadscrew of pitch 5 mm/rev. Calculate (i) the step angle, (ii) the linear displacement of the table per pulse, and (iii) the number of pulses required to move the table 75 mm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{steps} = 200$ steps/rev
- $L = 5$ mm/rev (leadscrew pitch)

**(i) Step angle (2 marks):**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

**In radians:**

$$\theta_s = 1.8 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.03142 \text{ rad/step}$$

**(ii) Linear displacement per pulse (3 marks):**

$$d = \frac{L}{N_{steps}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{200 \text{ steps}} = 0.025 \text{ mm/step}$$

**Equivalently:**

$$d = \theta_s \cdot \frac{L}{2\pi} = 0.03142 \cdot \frac{5}{6.2832} = 0.025 \text{ mm/step}$$

**(iii) Number of pulses for 75 mm (2 marks):**

$$N_{pulses} = \frac{\text{Total displacement}}{d} = \frac{75 \text{ mm}}{0.025 \text{ mm/pulse}} = 3000 \text{ pulses}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- Step angle = **1.8°** (or 0.03142 rad)
- Displacement per pulse = **0.025 mm**
- Pulses needed for 75 mm = **3000 pulses**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks): Classify NC systems based on (i) motion type, (ii) feedback, and (iii) coordinate reference. Give at least two examples for each category.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Motion Type (2.5 marks):**

- **Point-to-Point (PTP):** Tool moves to specific positions; path between points is uncontrolled. Examples: **Drilling machine, Boring machine, Punch press, Tapping machine.**
- **Contouring (Continuous Path):** Tool follows a precisely controlled continuous path. Examples: **Milling machine, Lathe (turning), Wire EDM, Profile grinder.**

**(ii) Classification by Feedback (2.5 marks):**

- **Open Loop:** No position feedback. Actuator: **Stepper motor.** Example: low-cost PCB drilling machine.
- **Closed Loop:** Position feedback via encoder/scale; uses **Servo motor** (DC or AC). Example: CNC vertical machining center.

**(iii) Classification by Coordinate Reference (2 marks):**

- **Incremental (G91):** Coordinates are distances from current position. Errors accumulate.
- **Absolute (G90):** Coordinates are distances from a fixed machine origin. No error accumulation.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks): A CNC milling machine is in G90 (absolute) mode. The tool starts at the origin (0, 0). The part program commands the following positions in sequence: (i) move to (30, 20), (ii) drill, (iii) move to (50, 50), (iv) drill, (v) move to (80, 30), (vi) drill. If, due to a backlash error, the second move overshoots by 0.05 mm in the X-direction, calculate the actual position of the tool at the third drill point. State whether the same error would affect an incremental (G91) system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** G90 (Absolute) mode. Three absolute drill points: A(30,20), B(50,50), C(80,30).

**Step 1 — In absolute mode, each move is referenced to the origin (0,0).**

- Move 1: X = 30, Y = 20 → Tool at A(30, 20) ✓
- Move 2: X = 50, Y = 50. Due to overshoot of 0.05 mm, the actual X is **50 + 0.05 = 50.05 mm.**
- Actual position after Move 2: **(50.05, 50)**.

**Step 2 — Move 3 in absolute mode is still referenced to the origin.**

- Move 3: X = 80, Y = 30 → Tool at C(80, 30) ✓
- **The error in Move 2 does NOT propagate to Move 3 in absolute mode.**
- Actual position at third drill point: **(80, 30)**.

**Step 3 — Incremental mode analysis:**

In incremental mode, the same moves would be:
- Move 1: ΔX = 30, ΔY = 20 (to A).
- Move 2: ΔX = 20, ΔY = 30 (to B). With 0.05 mm overshoot, the tool is at (50.05, 50).
- Move 3: ΔX = 30, ΔY = −20 (to C). Starting from (50.05, 50), the tool reaches **(80.05, 30)** — the X error is carried over.

**Step 4 — Comparison (2 marks for the comparison):**

| Mode | X-position at third drill |
|---|---|
| Absolute (G90) | 80.00 mm (no error propagation) |
| Incremental (G91) | 80.05 mm (error carried forward) |

**Final Answer:** In absolute mode, the tool reaches **(80, 30) mm** — the error is **not carried forward**. In incremental mode, the error **propagates**, and the tool would reach **(80.05, 30) mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing "NC" with "CNC"** — writing them as synonyms. They are different generations. Always clarify the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (e.g., step angle in degrees vs. radians; feed in mm/min vs. mm/rev). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for missing units.
> 3. **Writing "closed loop is always better"** — this is a trap. Mention the **stability vs. accuracy tradeoff** for full marks.
> 4. **In incremental/absolute conversions** — students often swap Δx and x, or forget to subtract the previous coordinate.
> 5. **In PTP vs. contouring** — students often claim "PTP is used for drilling" but forget to mention **why** (path doesn't matter because the tool plunges vertically and the workpiece is approached at rapid traverse).
> 6. **Block diagrams without labels** — every block must be labeled (Controller, Driver, Motor, Leadscrew, Table, Feedback Sensor). Unlabeled blocks get zero marks.
> 7. **Spelling "contouring" as "contoring"** — small issue, but the official KTU spelling is **contouring**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4):**

- **NC** = Programmable automation via coded numerical instructions. Pioneered at **MIT in the 1950s** by **John T. Parsons**.
- **Three sub-systems** of any NC machine: **Program + MCU + Machine Tool**.
- **Advantages** of NC fall into 4 categories: **Production, Economic, Quality, Strategic** (mention 2 from each).
- **Classification by Motion** = **PTP** (drilling, boring, tapping) vs. **Contouring** (milling, turning, EDM).
- **Classification by Feedback** = **Open Loop** (stepper, no feedback) vs. **Closed Loop** (servo, with encoder/scale).
- **Classification by Coordinate** = **Incremental (G91)** vs. **Absolute (G90)**. Absolute does not accumulate errors.
- **NC vs. CNC** = hard-wired logic vs. software-based control. Modern machines are essentially all CNC.
- **Open loop** transfer function: $G_{OL} = G_c G_a G_m$. **Closed loop** transfer function: $G_{CL} = \frac{G_{OL}}{1 + G_{OL}}$.
- **Steady-state error** in a closed-loop system reduces as loop gain $K$ increases: $e_{ss} = R \cdot \frac{a}{a + K}$.
- **Stepper step angle** formula: $\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$.
- **Table resolution per pulse**: $d = \frac{L}{N_{steps}}$ where $L$ = leadscrew pitch.
- **Spindle speed** for turning: $N = \frac{1000 v}{\pi D}$ (with $v$ in m/min, $D$ in mm, result in rpm).
- **Contouring requires interpolation**: **G01 (linear), G02 (CW circular), G03 (CCW circular)**.
- **Closed loop = better accuracy but risk of instability**; **open loop = simple and cheap but no error detection**.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers: mm, mm/min, rpm, degrees, steps.
- **Always draw block diagrams** with **labeled blocks** for part-b questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Programmable Logic Controller and Industrial Robotics - Programmable Logic Controller: Basic PLC Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT863 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Programmable Logic Controller and Industrial Robotics - Programmable Logic Controller: Basic PLC Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is defined by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** as a *"digitally operating electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions for implementing specific functions, such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations, to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for PEEBT863 (Mechatronics), the PLC is treated as the **central nervous system** of an automated manufacturing cell — a ruggedized industrial computer that replaces hard-wired relay logic panels with **software-based ladder logic**, **Function Block Diagrams (FBD)**, or **Structured Text (ST)** programs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise (Exact Wording):**
> "A PLC is a solid-state, microprocessor-based, user-programmable controller that employs programmable memory to store instructions and to implement functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic in order to control machines and industrial processes."

## 1.2 The Bedford Analogy — A Simple Intuitive Overview

To understand a PLC, imagine a **modern washing machine** that you use at home:

- The **knobs/dials** you turn = **Inputs** (sensors, switches, push-buttons)
- The **drum motor, water pump, and heater** = **Outputs** (motor contactors, solenoids, valves)
- The **hidden electronic card** inside that decides *"when water should flow"*, *"when to spin"*, *"when to stop"* = the **CPU + Memory** of the PLC
- The **mains cord and internal battery** = **Power Supply Unit (PSU)**

A PLC does **exactly the same job** for an entire factory line — only it is far more rugged, far more powerful, and its decision-making logic can be rewritten simply by typing a new program into a laptop, instead of **re-wiring thousands of relays**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The PLC as a "Digital Reflex Brain":**
> Just like our spinal cord executes reflex actions (input → decision → output) **without involving the conscious brain**, a PLC continuously polls its inputs, makes lightning-fast logical decisions, and updates its outputs in a fixed **Scan Cycle**, typically every **5 ms to 20 ms**.

## 1.3 Why PLCs Exist — The Relay-Replacement Story

Before PLCs (pre-**1968**, invented at **General Motors / Bedford Associates**), industrial control was done using **electromechanical relays** wired in panels. For a car plant with 200+ relays, a single design change required:
- **Rewiring** hundreds of copper wires
- **Days of downtime**
- **Thousands of dollars** in electrician labor

The PLC solved this by making the *logic* **software**, not *hardware*. Now a designer can change a control sequence by **editing one line of ladder logic** on a laptop in minutes.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember (Bolded for Recall):**
> - **Scan Time of a typical small PLC:** **5 ms to 20 ms**
> - **Operating temperature range (industrial grade):** **0 °C to 60 °C**
> - **Programming languages standardised by IEC 61131-3:** **5 languages** (LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC)
> - **Typical I/O count for a small PLC:** **8 to 128 points**
> - **Battery backup for RAM memory:** **5 years** (typical lithium backup)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of PLC Architecture

A PLC, regardless of brand (Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix, Mitsubishi FX5U, Schneider M221), is built from **five core functional blocks**. Understanding these is **mandatory** for the KTU board exam.

### Pillar 1 — The Processor / Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- Contains the **microprocessor** (often 32-bit RISC architecture in modern PLCs)
- Executes the **control program** stored in memory
- Manages **communication** between all sub-modules
- Handles **arithmetic, logic, sequencing, and timing** operations
- Includes a **watchdog timer** that resets the system if the scan cycle exceeds a safety limit

### Pillar 2 — The Memory Unit
Divided into distinct zones with different retention characteristics:

| Memory Zone | Purpose | Volatility | Typical Size |
|-------------|---------|------------|--------------|
| **System ROM** | Boot loader, OS kernel, firmware | Non-volatile | **128 KB – 2 MB** |
| **User Program RAM** | Ladder / FBD logic downloaded by user | Volatile (battery-backed) | **64 KB – 4 MB** |
| **Data Memory** | Timers, counters, M-coils, process variables | Volatile | **8 KB – 1 MB** |
| **Retentive Memory** | Values preserved across power-off | Non-volatile (Flash/EEPROM) | **16 KB – 512 KB** |
| **I/O Image Table** | Snapshot of physical input/output states | Refreshed each scan | **Bit-addressable** |

### Pillar 3 — Input / Output Modules (I/O Section)
This is the **only** part of the PLC that physically interfaces with the outside world.

**Input Modules (Sensing the World):**
- **Digital Inputs (DI):** ON/OFF signals — push-buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photo-electric eyes. Common voltage levels: **24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, **230 V AC**.
- **Analog Inputs (AI):** Continuously varying signals — thermocouples (mV), RTDs (ohms), pressure transmitters (**4–20 mA** or **0–10 V**). An **ADC** converts them to digital counts (e.g., **12-bit** → **0 to 4095**).

**Output Modules (Commanding the World):**
- **Digital Outputs (DO):** Relay (≤ 2 A), Transistor (≤ 0.5 A), Triac (for AC loads). Drive contactors, solenoid valves, indicator lamps.
- **Analog Outputs (AO):** Variable voltage/current — drive **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**, proportional valves, control heaters. Uses a **DAC**.

### Pillar 4 — Power Supply Unit (PSU)
- Converts **230 V AC, 50 Hz** mains to isolated **5 V DC** (logic) and **24 V DC** (sensor/solenoid supply)
- Must tolerate **±15 % mains fluctuation** and brief **brown-outs**
- Typically rated **10 W to 100 W** depending on I/O count

### Pillar 5 — Programming / Peripheral Device
- A **Personal Computer (PC)** with vendor-specific software (e.g., TIA Portal for Siemens, RSLogix for AB, GX Works for Mitsubishi)
- A **Hand-Held Programming Console (HHC)** for small troubleshooting tasks
- Communication via **RS-232**, **RS-485**, **USB**, or **Ethernet/IP**

## 2.2 The Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of a PLC

A PLC does **not** run continuously like a desktop computer. It runs in a **deterministic, cyclic, repetitive** manner called the **Scan Cycle**.

The scan cycle has **four mandatory phases**, executed in strict order:

1. **Input Scan** — CPU reads the status of **every physical input** and copies it into the **Input Image Table (IIT)**. This snapshot freezes the inputs so that if a sensor changes state mid-scan, the program sees a consistent world.
2. **Program Scan (Logic Solve)** — CPU executes the user program **rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom**, left-to-right (for ladder logic). It uses the IIT for inputs and writes intermediate results to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan** — The values in the OIT are **latched simultaneously** to the physical output terminals, energising/de-energising real-world loads.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead)** — Communication with programming device, self-diagnostics, updating timers/counters, servicing interrupts. Usually **1 ms to 5 ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Because the PLC only updates outputs **once per scan**, the *worst-case* response time of any input is **almost two full scan cycles** (one cycle for input to be read, one for the resulting output to be latched).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this entire table — these are the highest-yield equations for Module 4.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation / Definition | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | **ms** | Determines maximum control loop speed |
| 2 | **Worst-Case I/O Response Time** | $T_{resp}^{max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in}$ | **ms** | Safety-critical interlock design |
| 3 | **Best-Case I/O Response Time** | $T_{resp}^{min} = T_{scan} - T_{in}$ | **ms** | High-speed packaging lines |
| 4 | **Analog Resolution (n-bit ADC)** | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | **V / count** | Selecting ADC precision for transducers |
| 5 | **Digital Count from Analog Signal** | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{Q} \right\rfloor$ | **counts** | Engineering unit conversion |
| 6 | **Scaling — Engineering Units** | $E = \left(\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N_{max} - N_{min}}\right) \cdot (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}$ | **bar, °C, m/s** | HMI display of process variable |
| 7 | **Memory Address (Word n, Bit b)** | $A = (n \cdot 16) + b$ | **bit index** | Direct bit manipulation in ST |
| 8 | **Power Dissipation Estimate** | $P_{PLC} \approx P_{CPU} + N_{out} \cdot I_{out} \cdot V_{drop}$ | **W** | Cabinet thermal design |
| 9 | **Standard 4–20 mA Span** | $I_{signal} = 4 + 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{PV - PV_{min}}{PV_{max} - PV_{min}}\right)$ | **mA** | Current-loop transmitter calibration |
