# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define an Industrial Robot. List any four essential characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An industrial robot (per ISO TR/8373-2) is an *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose, manipulative machine with several degrees of freedom, used in industrial automation.*

Four essential characteristics:
1. **Automatically controlled** — operates without human intervention.
2. **Re-programmable** — task changes via software, not hardware.
3. **Multi-purpose** — can perform multiple distinct operations.
4. **Multi-degree-of-freedom** — has at least 3 independent axes for spatial manipulation.

*(3 marks — full credit for ISO definition + any 4 characteristics.)*

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Repeatability and Accuracy of a Robot.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Closeness of actual position to commanded target | Closeness of multiple actual positions to each other when repeating the same command |
| **Statistical Nature** | Deterministic (single measurement) | Statistical ($\pm 3\sigma$) |
| **Depends on** | Calibration, kinematic errors, backlash | Mechanical stiffness, sensor precision |
| **Value Range** | Larger (poorer) | Smaller (better) |
| **Industry priority** | Important for off-line programming | Most important for mass production |

*(3 marks — proper distinction required, not just definitions.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Robot Anatomy and Specifications**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Format]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: With a neat block diagram, explain the major subsystems of an industrial robot. List any 6 important specifications used to characterize an industrial robot.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram — 2 marks for diagram)*

**Six Major Subsystems (each 0.5 mark = 3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Manipulator (Arm + Wrist):** Rigid links connected by prismatic/revolute joints, providing positioning and orientation DOF.
2. **End-Effector (Gripper/Tool):** Device mounted on the wrist that interacts with the workpiece (gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer).
3. **Actuators:** Hydraulic, pneumatic, or electric motors that power joint motions.
4. **Sensors:** Internal (encoders, resolvers) and external (vision, force) sensors for feedback and perception.
5. **Controller:** Microprocessor-based unit executing the motion program and closing servo loops.
6. **Power Supply & Drive Electronics:** Provides regulated power (including servo amplifiers) to all subsystems.

**Six Robot Specifications (each 0.33 mark = 2 marks):**
1. Number of axes (DOF)
2. Payload capacity
3. Reach / Working envelope
4. Repeatability
5. Resolution
6. Accuracy / Speed / Memory

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A robot joint uses a 14-bit absolute encoder with a 100:1 gear reduction. The joint can rotate through 270°. Calculate (i) control resolution in degrees, and (ii) control resolution in arc-seconds.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Encoder total counts.**
$$N_{\text{counts}} = 2^{14} = 16384 \text{ counts/rev of motor}$$

**Step 2: Encoder resolution per motor revolution.**
$$\theta_{\text{encoder}} = \frac{360^\circ}{16384} = 0.02197^\circ \text{ per count}$$

**Step 3: Apply gear ratio to find joint resolution.**
Since motor turns 100 times for 1 joint revolution:
$$R = \frac{360^\circ}{16384 \times 100}$$
$$R = \frac{360^\circ}{1638400}$$
$$R = 0.0002197^\circ \text{ per count}$$

**(i) Resolution in degrees:**
$$R \approx 2.197 \times 10^{-4} \;^\circ$$

**Step 4: Convert to arc-seconds. (1 degree = 3600 arc-seconds)**
$$R = 2.197 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600$$
$$R = 0.7909 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

**(ii) Resolution in arc-seconds:**
$$\boxed{R \approx 0.791 \text{ arc-seconds per count}}$$

**Note:** The 270° joint range is the mechanical limit; it does not affect the *per-count* resolution of the encoder, only the total reachable positions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Robot Classification and Working Envelope**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Format]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Classify industrial robots based on coordinate system. Sketch and explain the working envelope of any three types.**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (1 mark):** Industrial robots are classified into 5 main types based on coordinate system:
1. Cartesian (PPP)
2. Cylindrical (RPP)
3. Spherical/Polar (RRP)
4. Articulated (RRR)
5. SCARA

**Three Types Explained (2 marks each):**

1. **Cartesian Robot (PPP):** Three prismatic joints along mutually perpendicular X, Y, Z axes. The working envelope is a **rectangular cuboid**. Used for pick-and-place, palletizing, and CNC loading. *(2 marks)*

2. **Cylindrical Robot (RPP):** A rotary joint at the base, followed by two prismatic joints (one vertical, one radial). The working envelope is a **cylindrical volume**. Used in machine tending and simple assembly. *(2 marks)*

3. **Articulated Robot (RRR):** Three revolute joints (waist, shoulder, elbow) followed by a 3-DOF wrist. The working envelope is a **complex spherical region** with multiple joint-limit cutouts. Most versatile; used in welding, painting, and assembly. *(2 marks)*

**Sketch (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 or describe envelope shapes verbally with neat ASCII / labeled diagrams.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A spherical robot has the following joint limits: Reach $r$: 0 to 1500 mm; Vertical sweep $\theta$: 0° to 180°; Azimuth $\phi$: 0° to 270°. Calculate the working envelope volume. State two assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The working envelope is approximated as a **spherical sector** (segment of a full sphere).
2. The end-effector can reach every point within the limits (no mechanical obstructions ignored).

**Step 1: Formula for working volume of a spherical sector.**
$$V = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3 \cdot \frac{\Omega}{4\pi}$$
where $\Omega$ = solid angle subtended.

Alternatively, decompose:
$$V = V_{\text{full sphere}} \times \frac{\text{Polar sweep fraction}}{360°} \times \frac{\text{Azimuth fraction}}{360°}$$

**Step 2: Compute fractions.**
$$\text{Polar fraction} = \frac{180°}{360°} = 0.5$$
$$\text{Azimuth fraction} = \frac{270°}{360°} = 0.75$$

**Step 3: Full sphere volume.**
$$V_{\text{full}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1500)^3 \;\text{mm}^3$$
$$V_{\text{full}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (3.375 \times 10^9)$$
$$V_{\text{full}} = 1.4137 \times 10^{10} \;\text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Multiply by fractions.**
$$V = 1.4137 \times 10^{10} \times 0.5 \times 0.75$$
$$V = 1.4137 \times 10^{10} \times 0.375$$
$$\boxed{V \approx 5.301 \times 10^{9} \;\text{mm}^3 \approx 5301 \;\text{liters}}$$

**Conclusion:** This represents the accessible workspace of the robot's end-effector.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing Resolution with Repeatability:** Many students write "resolution is the smallest repeatable motion." This is **wrong**. Resolution is about *commanded increments*, not *achieved positions*.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3σ rule:** Repeatability is NOT ±1σ or ±2σ. KTU strictly expects **±3σ (99.74%)**. Writing just "±σ" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up arm vs wrist DOF:** Position = first 3 DOF (arm); Orientation = last 3 DOF (wrist). Writing the reverse costs marks.
> 4. **Using joint range in encoder calculation:** The 270° joint range is a *red herring* in Question A(b) above. Don't include it in the denominator. Only encoder bits and gear ratio matter for *per-count resolution*.
> 5. **Skipping the unit conversion:** Always express answer in matching units. KTU examiners often deduct for inconsistent units (e.g., mm³ without conversion).
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Especially for working-volume problems, KTU deducts 1 mark if assumptions are missing.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ISO Definition** of industrial robot: *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose, manipulative machine with several DOF*.
- ✅ **6 Major Subsystems:** Manipulator, End-Effector, Actuators, Sensors, Controller, Power Supply & Drive.
- ✅ **Arm = Positioning DOF (3); Wrist = Orientation DOF (3).**
- ✅ **Three Actuator Types:** Hydraulic (heavy), Pneumatic (light/fast), Electric (precise).
- ✅ **Joint Types:** Prismatic (P, sliding) and Revolute (R, rotary) — each provides 1 DOF.
- ✅ **8 Key Specifications:** DOF, Payload, Reach, Repeatability, Resolution, Accuracy, Speed, Memory.
- ✅ **Resolution Formula:** $R = \frac{\text{Range}}{2^n}$ (control) plus mechanical backlash.
- ✅ **Repeatability Formula:** $RP = \pm 3\sigma$ — **statistical**, NOT deterministic.
- ✅ **Accuracy Formula:** $A = \vert P_{\text{commanded}} - P_{\text{actual}} \vert$ — **deterministic**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy to memorize:** $R < RP < A$ (Resolution smallest, Accuracy largest).
- ✅ **Classification by coordinate system:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA.
- ✅ **Classification by control:** Servo (closed-loop, precise) vs Non-servo (open-loop, cheap).
- ✅ **Working Envelope formulas:**
  - Cartesian: rectangular cuboid $V = L \times W \times H$
  - Cylindrical: $V = \pi r^2 h$
  - Spherical: $V = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3 \cdot \frac{\Omega}{4\pi}$
- ✅ **Robotics ⊂ Automation ⊃ Industrial Robotics** — robotics is a *specialized subset*.
- ✅ **Standard applied metric:** ISO 9283 governs performance measurement of industrial robots.
- ✅ **High-yield numericals for KTU:** Encoder resolution, repeatability bands, working-envelope volumes.
